# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## **PART A — 3 Mark Questions (Short Answer)**

### **Question 1: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Define the principle of Additive Manufacturing as per ASTM F2792 standards. List the seven standard AM process categories.**

**Model Answer:**

The principle of Additive Manufacturing (AM) is defined by ASTM F2792 as *a process of joining materials to make objects from 3D model data, usually layer upon layer, as opposed to subtractive manufacturing methodologies*. It is characterized by the discrete, sequential deposition of material in a layer-wise manner to form a physical part from a digital model.

The **seven standard AM process categories** as per ASTM F2792 / ISO/ASTM 52900 are:
1. **VAT Photopolymerization (VPP)**
2. **Material Extrusion (MEX)**
3. **Material Jetting (MJT)**
4. **Binder Jetting (BJT)**
5. **Powder Bed Fusion (PBF)**
6. **Directed Energy Deposition (DED)**
7. **Sheet Lamination (SHL)**

---

### **Question 2: [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**Explain the staircase effect in Additive Manufacturing. How is it quantitatively expressed?**

**Model Answer:**

The **staircase effect** is a geometric artifact inherent to the layer-by-layer principle of AM. When a surface is inclined relative to the horizontal build platform, each deposited layer produces a discrete "step" rather than a smooth inclined surface, resembling a staircase when viewed in cross-section. This directly affects the surface finish and dimensional accuracy of AM parts.

It is quantitatively expressed by the **staircase step height formula**:

$$h_{step} = t_{layer} \times \sin(\theta)$$

Where $t_{layer}$ is the layer thickness and $\theta$ is the angle of the surface relative to the build platform. The staircase effect is minimized by reducing the layer thickness, which is why high-precision processes like SLA use layer thicknesses as low as **10–25 µm**.

---

## **PART B — 14 Mark Questions (ESE Module Choice)**

### **Question A: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the **generalized principle of Additive Manufacturing** with a clear block diagram of the workflow from CAD model to final part. List any **four key differences** between additive and conventional subtractive manufacturing.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A part of height **80 mm** is to be built in an FDM (Fused Deposition Modeling) machine with a layer thickness of **0.20 mm**. The average deposition time per layer is **25 seconds**, and the machine setup time is **35 minutes**. Calculate:
1. The total number of layers.
2. The total build time in hours.
3. The Volumetric Build Rate (VBR) if each layer has a cross-sectional area of **$2000 \text{ mm}^2$**.

#### **Model Solution for Part A(a):**

**Principle of AM:**
The principle of AM is based on the layer-wise addition of material. A 3D CAD model is first created, then converted to STL format, sliced into 2D horizontal cross-sections, and built layer by layer using an appropriate energy source and material feedstock.

**Block Diagram (Reproduced):**

```
CAD Model -> STL File -> Slicer -> G-code -> Build Setup -> Layer-by-Layer Fabrication -> Post-Processing -> Final Part
```

**Four Key Differences: AM vs. Subtractive Manufacturing:**

| S.No. | Aspect | Additive Manufacturing | Subtractive (CNC) Manufacturing |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Material Addition** | Material is *added* layer-by-layer | Material is *removed* by cutting |
| 2 | **Tooling** | No part-specific tooling required | Requires molds, dies, jigs, fixtures |
| 3 | **Waste Generation** | Near-zero waste (recyclable powder/resin) | High waste (up to 80–95% of billet) |
| 4 | **Geometric Complexity** | Cost independent of complexity | Cost increases sharply with complexity |
| 5 | **Part Size** | Limited by build volume | Can machine very large parts |
| 6 | **Lead Time** | Excellent for low-volume production | Better suited for high-volume production |

#### **Model Solution for Part A(b):**

**Given:**
- Part height, $H = 80 \text{ mm}$
- Layer thickness, $t_{layer} = 0.20 \text{ mm}$
- Deposition time per layer, $t_{dep} = 25 \text{ s}$
- Setup time, $T_{setup} = 35 \text{ min} = 2100 \text{ s}$
- Cross-sectional area, $A_{layer} = 2000 \text{ mm}^2$

**(1) Total Number of Layers:**

$$n = \frac{H_{part}}{t_{layer}} = \frac{80 \text{ mm}}{0.20 \text{ mm}} = 400 \text{ layers}$$

**(2) Total Build Time:**

$$T_{build} = (n \times t_{dep}) + T_{setup} = (400 \times 25) + 2100 = 10000 + 2100 = 12100 \text{ s}$$

$$T_{build} = \frac{12100}{3600} = 3.361 \text{ hours}$$

**(3) Volumetric Build Rate (VBR):**

$$VBR = \frac{A_{layer} \times t_{layer}}{t_{dep}} = \frac{2000 \text{ mm}^2 \times 0.20 \text{ mm}}{25 \text{ s}} = \frac{400 \text{ mm}^3}{25 \text{ s}} = 16 \text{ mm}^3/\text{s}$$

**Final Answers:** $n = 400 \text{ layers}$, $T_{build} = 3.361 \text{ h}$, $VBR = 16 \text{ mm}^3/\text{s}$.

---

### **Question B: [KTU University Exam – June 2024] (Alternative Choice)**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **ASTM F2792 classification of AM processes** with examples. For each category, state the **physical principle of material consolidation**.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A circular cylindrical part of diameter **40 mm** and height **50 mm** is being built in an SLA (Stereolithography) machine. The layer thickness is **0.05 mm**, and the laser scan speed enables curing of each layer in **8 seconds**. The setup time is **40 minutes**. Calculate:
1. The number of layers and the build time in minutes.
2. The **staircase step height** for a surface inclined at **60°** to the build platform.
3. The **effective build time per unit volume** of the part in $\text{h}/\text{cm}^3$.

#### **Model Solution for Part B(a):**

The principle of material consolidation varies across the seven ASTM categories:

1. **VAT Photopolymerization (VPP):** Principle is **photochemical polymerization**. UV light selectively cures liquid photopolymer resin. Examples: **SLA (Stereolithography), DLP (Digital Light Processing)**.

2. **Material Extrusion (MEX):** Principle is **thermal extrusion**. A heated nozzle melts and extrudes thermoplastic filament. Example: **FDM (Fused Deposition Modeling)**.

3. **Material Jetting (MJT):** Principle is **drop-on-demand jetting**. Inkjet-style printheads deposit liquid photopolymer droplets. Example: **PolyJet, MultiJet Printing**.

4. **Binder Jetting (BJT):** Principle is **adhesive bonding**. A liquid binder is selectively jetted onto a powder bed. Example: **ExOne's Metal Binder Jetting** for sand molds and metal parts.

5. **Powder Bed Fusion (PBF):** Principle is **selective thermal fusion** of powder using a laser or electron beam. Examples: **SLM (Selective Laser Melting), SLS (Selective Laser Sintering), EBM (Electron Beam Melting)**.

6. **Directed Energy Deposition (DED):** Principle is **focused energy with simultaneous material feed**. A laser or plasma melts material as wire or powder is injected. Example: **Laser Metal Deposition (LMD), EBAM**.

7. **Sheet Lamination (SHL):** Principle is **adhesive/thermal bonding of sheets**. Sheets are cut, stacked, and joined. Example: **Laminated Object Manufacturing (LOM), ultrasonic additive manufacturing (UAM)**.

#### **Model Solution for Part B(b):**

**Given:**
- Cylinder: Diameter $D = 40 \text{ mm}$, Height $H = 50 \text{ mm}$
- Layer thickness, $t_{layer} = 0.05 \text{ mm}$
- Time per layer, $t_{dep} = 8 \text{ s}$
- Setup time, $T_{setup} = 40 \text{ min} = 2400 \text{ s}$
- Surface angle, $\theta = 60°$

**(1) Number of Layers and Build Time:**

$$n = \frac{50 \text{ mm}}{0.05 \text{ mm}} = 1000 \text{ layers}$$

$$T_{build} = (n \times t_{dep}) + T_{setup} = (1000 \times 8) + 2400 = 8000 + 2400 = 10400 \text{ s}$$

$$T_{build} = \frac{10400}{60} = 173.33 \text{ min} \approx 2.889 \text{ hours}$$

**(2) Staircase Step Height at $\theta = 60°$:**

$$h_{step} = t_{layer} \times \sin(\theta) = 0.05 \times \sin(60°)$$

$$h_{step} = 0.05 \times 0.8660 = 0.0433 \text{ mm} = 43.3 \text{ µm}$$

**(3) Effective Build Time per Unit Volume:**

Volume of cylinder:

$$V_{part} = \pi \times \left(\frac{D}{2}\right)^2 \times H = \pi \times (20)^2 \times 50 = \pi \times 400 \times 50 = 20000\pi \text{ mm}^3$$

$$V_{part} = 20000 \times 3.14159 = 62831.85 \text{ mm}^3 = 62.832 \text{ cm}^3$$

Effective build time per unit volume:

$$\tau_{eff} = \frac{T_{build}}{V_{part}} = \frac{2.889 \text{ h}}{62.832 \text{ cm}^3} = 0.0460 \text{ h}/\text{cm}^3$$

**Final Answers:** $n = 1000 \text{ layers}$, $T_{build} = 173.33 \text{ min}$, $h_{step} = 43.3 \text{ µm}$, $\tau_{eff} = 0.046 \text{ h}/\text{cm}^3$.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
>
> 1. **Confusing STL with Slicer Output:** Students often write that the slicer outputs an STL file. **Incorrect.** The slicer inputs the STL and outputs **G-code**. STL is the *input*; G-code is the *output*.
> 2. **Wrong ASTM Acronyms:** Mixing up **PBF vs DED**. PBF uses a **pre-laid powder bed** and recoater. DED **injects material coaxially** with the energy source. A direct question asking to "classify SLM" requires the answer **PBF**, not DED.
> 3. **Unit Conversion Errors in Build Time:** Failing to convert setup time from minutes to seconds (or vice versa) is a **2-mark deduction** in KTU ESE. Always state the unit conversion step explicitly.
> 4. **Ignoring Setup Time:** Some students compute $T_{build} = n \times t_{dep}$ *only*, ignoring $T_{setup}$. The full formula is $T_{build} = n \times t_{dep} + T_{setup}$.
> 5. **Forgetting Binary vs Decimal File Size:** For STL file size, KTU accepts both $10^6$ and $2^{20}$ as a megabyte but prefers clarity. State the assumption.

---

## **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 2: Principle of AM**
>
> - **ASTM F2792** is the governing standard defining AM. **7 categories** must be memorized: VPP, MEX, MJT, BJT, PBF, DED, SHL.
> - The **core principle** of AM is the **layer-by-layer addition** of material from a digital 3D model.
> - **STL (Standard Tessellation Language)** is the standard file format — 12 numbers per triangular facet (9 for vertices + 3 for normal vector).
> - **Slicer software** (Cura, Simplify3D) converts STL → **G-code**, which contains the 2D toolpath for each layer.
> - **Layer thickness ($t_{layer}$)** is the single most important process parameter — it controls **build time** (inversely) and **surface finish** (directly).
> - **Staircase effect** is mathematically modeled as $h_{step} = t_{layer} \times \sin(\theta)$.
> - **Build time** = (Number of layers × Time per layer) + Setup time.
> - **VBR (Volumetric Build Rate)** = (Cross-sectional area × Layer thickness) / (Time per layer).
> - **AM** is **near-zero waste**, **tool-less**, and **complexity-free** (cost independent of geometry) — this is its key advantage over CNC.
> - **Build Volume** is the maximum 3D envelope (L × W × H) of a machine, e.g., $220 \times 220 \times 250 \text{ mm}$ for desktop FDM.
> - **Photopolymerization** (VPP) uses UV light, **Powder Bed Fusion** (PBF) uses lasers/electron beams, and **DED** uses focused energy with coaxial material feed.
> - The **G-code toolpath** is the 2D outline that the energy source traces for each layer — it is the actual blueprint of the part.
> - **AM principle** inverts the cost-complexity relationship of CNC: in AM, complex parts do **not** cost more than simple parts of the same volume.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Materials
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET524 - ADDITIVE MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Materials

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

In the context of **Additive Manufacturing (AM)**, *Materials* refers to the entire class of feedstocks—polymers, metals, ceramics, composites, and bio-materials—whose form (filament, powder, wire, resin, sheet, or pellet) and thermo-physical properties must be precisely tailored to enable layer-by-layer consolidation via thermal, chemical, photochemical, or mechanical energy sources.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** This topic encompasses the classification, properties, form-factor requirements, process-material compatibility, and characterization metrics of feedstocks used in vat photopolymerization, powder bed fusion, material extrusion, material jetting, binder jetting, and directed energy deposition processes.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of AM materials like **ingredients in a specialized kitchen**. Just as a baker chooses flour (loose powder), butter sticks (filaments), or liquid batter (resin) depending on the dish, an AM engineer picks:
- **Fine metallic powder** for a rocket nozzle (laser melting),
- **PLA filament** for a toy figurine (Fused Filament Fabrication),
- **Liquid photopolymer resin** for a dental crown (SLA).

The *form* of the ingredient must match the *cooking method* (laser, nozzle, UV light), and the *chemistry* must "set" correctly under the precise energy delivered. Wrong ingredient = burnt cake or failed print.

## 1.3 Material Classification Overview

| Class | Typical Form | Compatible AM Process |
|---|---|---|
| Thermoplastics | Filament, pellet, powder | FFF/FDM, SLS, MJF |
| Photopolymers | Liquid resin | SLA, DLP, MJP |
| Metals | Powder, wire | SLM, DMLS, EBM, DED, WAAM |
| Ceramics | Powder, slurry, paste | Binder Jetting, Robocasting, SLA (filled) |
| Composites | Fiber-filled filament, powder | FFF, SLM (with reinforcements) |
| Biomaterials | Powder, hydrogel, bio-ink | Bioprinting, SLS, SLA |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** ASTM F42 / ISO/ASTM 52900 classifies AM processes by the **type of feedstock** and the **state of the material** during joining—understanding this linkage is the basis of any materials question.

## 1.4 Why Material Choice is Critical in AM

- **Process Window Compatibility:** Melting point, glass transition ($T_g$), and degradation temperature must align with the energy source's delivered power.
- **Layer Adhesion:** Determines inter-layer bond strength (z-direction) versus in-plane strength.
- **Anisotropy:** Different material-process combinations yield different anisotropic behaviors.
- **Post-processing needs:** Support removal, sintering, infiltration, HIP—all material-dependent.
- **Functional Performance:** Fatigue, corrosion, biocompatibility, thermal conductivity depend on material microstructure.
- **Cost & Safety:** Powder handling, toxicity, recyclability.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Metric Highlight:** For metal powder bed fusion, typical particle size range is **15 µm to 45 µm** with **sphericity ≥ 0.9**—this is a frequently tested KTU number.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 General Material Requirements in AM

Every AM process imposes a triad of constraints on its feedstock:

1. **Rheological requirement** — how the material flows under the energy source (viscosity for extrusion, melt flow for polymers, wetting for binder jetting).
2. **Thermal requirement** — adequate thermal conductivity, controlled solidification shrinkage, and a sufficient gap between melting ($T_m$ or $T_g$) and decomposition ($T_d$).
3. **Geometric/Form requirement** — particle size distribution (PSD), flowability, and packing density for powders; diameter tolerance and roundness for filaments; viscosity and cure kinetics for resins.

## 2.2 Thermoplastic Materials (Polymers)

### 2.2.1 Bulk Material Properties Required
- **Glass Transition Temperature ($T_g$):** Below this, polymer is rigid; above, it is rubbery.
- **Melting Temperature ($T_m$):** For semi-crystalline polymers.
- **Melt Flow Index (MFI):** Indicates processability—too low MFI clogs nozzles; too high causes poor dimensional accuracy.
- **Thermal Degradation Temperature ($T_d$):** Must be **≥ 20–30 °C** above the processing temperature.

### 2.2.2 Common Polymers in AM

| Polymer | $T_g$ (°C) | $T_m$ (°C) | Key Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| PLA (Polylactic Acid) | 60 | 150–160 | Education, prototyping |
| ABS (Acrylonitrile Butadiene Styrene) | 105 | 220–230 | Functional prototypes, jigs |
| PA12 (Nylon) | 50 | 175–180 | SLS, end-use parts |
| PA6 / PA66 | 50–60 | 215–265 | Industrial SLS |
| PC (Polycarbonate) | 147 | 260–270 | High-strength parts |
| PEEK (Polyether Ether Ketone) | 143 | 343 | Aerospace, medical implants |
| PEKK | 156 | 305–337 | Aerospace structural |
| TPU (Thermoplastic Polyurethane) | varies | varies (rubbery) | Flexible parts (SLS, FFF) |
| PPS / PPSU | 90 / 220 | 280 / 200 | High-temp engineering |
| ULTEM (PEI) | 217 | — | High-performance FFF |

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield KTU Fact:** **PA12 (PA 2200, PA 2201)** dominates SLS due to its narrow melting range and excellent flowability.

### 2.2.3 Photopolymers (Resins)
- **Composition:** Monomers + Oligomers + Photoinitiators + Additives.
- **Mechanism:** Photoinitiators (Type I: cleavage, e.g., BAPO; Type II: H-abstraction, e.g., benzophenone) generate free radicals or cations that polymerize the resin under UV (~355–405 nm) or visible light.
- **Common Resin Chemistries:**
  - **Acrylates** (rapid cure, brittle)
  - **Methacrylates** (tough, biocompatible — e.g., dental)
  - **Epoxies** (cationic, low shrinkage, used in DLP)
  - **Vinyl ethers** (specialty)

## 2.3 Metal Powders for AM

### 2.3.1 Production Methods
1. **Gas Atomization (GA):** Most common; produces highly spherical powders (used in SLM, DMLS, EBM).
2. **Plasma Atomization (PA):** Ultra-spherical, high-purity (used for titanium).
3. **Water Atomization (WA):** Irregular, lower cost; suitable for binder jetting.
4. **Plasma Rotating Electrode Process (PREP):** Spherical, but limited in fine sizes.

### 2.3.2 Characterization Metrics
- **Particle Size Distribution (PSD):** Typically measured by laser diffraction; D10, D50, D90 reported.
- **Sphericity (Ψ):** Ratio of actual surface area to that of a sphere of equal volume; ideal = 1.0.
- **Aspect Ratio:** Length/width—must be close to 1.0 for good flow.
- **Flowability:** Measured by Hall flowmeter (sec/50g) or Carney funnel.
- **Apparent Density vs. Tap Density:** Used to compute Hausner Ratio and Carr Index.
- **Specific Surface Area (SSA):** Higher SSA → more oxidation risk.
- **Oxygen/Nitrogen Content (ppm):** Critical for reactive metals like Ti, Al.

### 2.3.3 Common Metal Alloys in AM
| Alloy Family | Examples | Application |
|---|---|---|
| Titanium | Ti-6Al-4V (Grade 5), Ti-6Al-7Nb, CP-Ti | Aerospace, medical implants |
| Aluminum | AlSi10Mg, AlSi12, Scalmalloy® | Automotive, aerospace (lightweighting) |
| Stainless Steel | 316L, 17-4 PH, 304L | Medical, tooling, marine |
| Nickel-based (Superalloys) | Inconel 625, 718, Hastelloy X | Gas turbines, rocket engines |
| Cobalt-Chrome | CoCrMo, ASTM F75 | Dental, orthopedic |
| Tool Steels | H13, Maraging 300 (18Ni300) | Molds, dies, inserts |
| Copper | Pure Cu, CuCrZr | Heat exchangers, induction coils |
| Refractory | Tungsten, Tantalum | Defense, nuclear |

## 2.4 Ceramics in AM

- **Forms:** Powders (alumina, zirconia), photocurable suspensions (filled resins), and pastes (silica, hydroxyapatite).
- **Processes:** Binder Jetting (green part → sintering), SLA/DLP with ceramic-filled resins (debinding + sintering), Robocasting/Direct Ink Writing.
- **Challenges:** High melting point, brittleness, thermal-shock cracking, ~20–30% shrinkage during sintering.

## 2.5 Composites

- **Particle-reinforced:** Glass/carbon fiber-filled filaments (e.g., CF-PLA, CF-PEEK, CF-PEEK composites with chopped fiber).
- **Continuous fiber:** Markforged's Continuous Filament Fabrication (CFF) with carbon, fiberglass, Kevlar® embedded in nylon matrix.
- **Metal Matrix Composites (MMC):** e.g., AlSi10Mg + SiC, fabricated by SLM.

## 2.6 Biomaterials for Medical AM

- **Polymers:** PLA, PLGA, PCL, PEG (for scaffolds and bioprinting).
- **Metals:** Ti-6Al-4V (implants), CoCrMo (dental), 316L (instruments).
- **Ceramics:** Hydroxyapatite (HA), β-Tricalcium Phosphate (β-TCP), Bioglass® (45S5).
- **Bio-inks:** Hydrogels like GelMA, Alginate, Collagen, Matrigel® for cell-laden extrusion.

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Metric | Expression | Unit | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| Volumetric Energy Density (VED) | $E_v = \dfrac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t}$ | J/mm³ | Process parameter (P=power, v=scan speed, h=hatch spacing, t=layer thickness) |
| Specific Surface Area (sphere) | $SSA = \dfrac{6}{\rho \cdot d}$ | m²/kg | Powder characterization |
| Hausner Ratio | $HR = \dfrac{\rho_{tap}}{\rho_{bulk}}$ | — | Flowability index; HR < 1.25 = good flow |
| Carr (Compressibility) Index | $CI = \dfrac{\rho_{tap} - \rho_{bulk}}{\rho_{tap}} \times 100$ | % | Flowability; <15% = good flow |
| Packing Fraction (mono-sized spheres) | $\phi_{max} = 0.74$ (FCC/HCP) | — | Theoretical max packing |
| Aspect Ratio | $AR = \dfrac{L}{W}$ | — | Powder shape |
| Sphericity | $\Psi = \dfrac{4\pi A}{P^2}$ | — | Shape metric, ideal = 1 |
| Cure Depth (SLA, Beer–Lambert) | $C_d = D_p \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{E}{E_c}\right)$ | mm | Photopolymerization; $D_p$ = penetration depth, $E$ = exposure, $E_c$ = critical exposure |
| Melt Flow Index relation | $MFI = \dfrac{m \cdot 600}{t \cdot \rho_{melt}}$ | g/10 min | Polymer processability |
| Solidification shrinkage (volumetric) | $\Delta V\% = \dfrac{\rho_s - \rho_l}{\rho_s} \times 100$ | % | Predict distortion |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** The **Volumetric Energy Density (VED)** is the single most-cited metric correlating process parameters to densification and defect formation in powder bed fusion—board questions frequently ask to compute or interpret it.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Volumetric Energy Density (VED)

The **VED** consolidates four primary process parameters into a single scalar that approximates the energy delivered per unit volume of powder bed (SLM/DMLS) or per unit volume of melt pool.

**Step 1 — Define energy delivered by the laser per unit time.**
The laser delivers power $P$ (W = J/s) at a constant rate.

$$E_{time} = P \quad [\text{W}]$$

**Step 2 — Compute the time the laser spends over a unit length of scan.**
If the laser moves at scan speed $v$ (mm/s), the dwell time per unit length $dx$ is:

$$dt = \frac{dx}{v} \quad [\text{s/mm}]$$

**Step 3 — Energy deposited per unit length of scan track.**
Multiplying power by dwell time per unit length:

$$E_{length} = P \cdot \frac{1}{v} = \frac{P}{v} \quad [\text{J/mm}]$$

**Step 4 — Energy deposited per unit area of a single layer.**
With a hatch spacing $h$ (mm) between adjacent scan tracks, energy per unit area is:

$$E_{area} = \frac{P}{v \cdot h} \quad [\text{J/mm}^2]$$

**Step 5 — Energy deposited per unit volume of consolidated material.**
Accounting for the layer thickness $t$ (mm), the energy per unit volume becomes:

$$E_v = \frac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t} \quad [\text{J/mm}^3]$$

This is the canonical **VED** formula.

> **Logic Check:** Faster scanning ($v \uparrow$) reduces energy delivered → risk of lack-of-fusion porosity. Higher power ($P \uparrow$) or thicker layers ($t \uparrow$) → risk of keyholing and evaporation. VED acts as a single dial to navigate this window.

### Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style)

> **Problem:** In an SLM process for **316L stainless steel**, the laser power is **200 W**, scan speed is **800 mm/s**, hatch spacing is **0.10 mm**, and layer thickness is **0.03 mm**. Compute the VED and comment on the resultant density.

**Step 1 — Substitute values:**

$$E_v = \frac{200}{800 \times 0.10 \times 0.03}$$

**Step 2 — Compute denominator:**

$$800 \times 0.10 \times 0.03 = 2.4 \quad [\text{mm}^3/\text{s}]$$

**Step 3 — Final division:**

$$E_v = \frac{200}{2.4} = 83.33 \quad [\text{J/mm}^3]$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation:**
For 316L, the literature-accepted VED window is **~60 to 100 J/mm³** for > 99.5% density. A value of **83.33 J/mm³** lies inside this window, predicting **near-full density** with minimal lack-of-fusion defects.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often forget to convert layer thickness from **micrometers to millimeters**. This single error multiplies the answer by 1000.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Cure Depth in Stereolithography (SLA)

The **Beer–Lambert law** describes the exponential attenuation of UV light intensity with depth into a photopolymer:

$$I(z) = I_0 \cdot e^{-\alpha z}$$

where $\alpha$ is the absorption coefficient and $I_0$ the surface intensity.

**Step 1 — Define the critical exposure $E_c$** — the minimum UV dose required at depth $z$ to initiate gelation/cure.

**Step 2 — The exposure at depth $z$** is:

$$E(z) = I_0 \cdot t_{exp} \cdot e^{-\alpha z}$$

where $t_{exp}$ is the exposure time.

**Step 3 — Set $E(z) = E_c$ and solve for $z = C_d$ (cure depth):**

$$E_c = I_0 \cdot t_{exp} \cdot e^{-\alpha \cdot C_d}$$

**Step 4 — Take natural logarithm and isolate $C_d$:**

$$C_d = \frac{1}{\alpha} \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{I_0 \cdot t_{exp}}{E_c}\right)$$

**Step 5 — Rewrite using the standard Jacobs notation**, where $D_p = 1/\alpha$ is the **penetration depth** and $E = I_0 \cdot t_{exp}$ is the **surface exposure**:

$$\boxed{C_d = D_p \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{E}{E_c}\right)}$$

### Worked Example
For a resin with $D_p = 0.10$ mm, $E_c = 12$ mJ/cm², and applied exposure $E = 60$ mJ/cm²:

$$C_d = 0.10 \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{60}{12}\right) = 0.10 \cdot \ln(5) = 0.10 \times 1.6094 = 0.1609 \text{ mm}$$

So each layer can be cured to a depth of **0.161 mm**, allowing layer thicknesses up to this value without uncured resin remaining.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Hausner Ratio and Carr Index

**Step 1 — Measure bulk density** $\rho_{bulk}$ by pouring powder into a known-volume container under gravity.

**Step 2 — Measure tap density** $\rho_{tap}$ after standardized tapping (e.g., 100–300 taps in a graduated cylinder).

**Step 3 — Compute Hausner Ratio:**

$$HR = \frac{\rho_{tap}}{\rho_{bulk}}$$

**Step 4 — Compute Carr Index:**

$$CI = \frac{\rho_{tap} - \rho_{bulk}}{\rho_{tap}} \times 100 \quad [\%]$$

**Interpretation Standard (US Pharmacopeia & AM literature):**

| HR | CI (%) | Flowability |
|---|---|---|
| 1.00 – 1.11 | ≤ 10 | Excellent |
| 1.12 – 1.18 | 11 – 15 | Good |
| 1.19 – 1.25 | 16 – 20 | Fair |
| 1.26 – 1.34 | 21 – 25 | Passable |
| 1.35 – 1.45 | 26 – 31 | Poor |
| > 1.46 | > 32 | Very Poor |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Key Takeaway:** A *good* AM powder (e.g., gas-atomized Ti-6Al-4V for SLM) has **HR ≤ 1.25** and **CI ≤ 15%**. Anything higher causes uneven recoating, porosity, and recoater jams.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Packing Fraction of Spherical Powders

The maximum packing fraction of **monodisperse, non-cohesive spheres** in the most efficient arrangement (FCC or HCP) is geometrically:

$$\phi_{max} = \frac{\pi}{3\sqrt{2}} \approx 0.74$$

For **polydisperse** powders, smaller particles fill interstices of larger ones, raising $\phi$ to **0.85 – 0.95**. This is why AM powders use carefully tuned PSDs (typically bimodal or trimodal distributions).

> **Logic:** Higher packing → lower porosity in green state → less shrinkage during sintering/binding → fewer defects in final part.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 AM Material Classification Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[AM Feedstocks] --> B[Polymers]
    A --> C[Metals]
    A --> D[Ceramics]
    A --> E[Composites]
    A --> F[Biomaterials]
    A --> G[Smart Materials]

    B --> B1[Thermoplastics: PLA, ABS, PA12, PEEK]
    B --> B2[Photopolymers: Acrylates, Epoxies]
    B --> B3[Elastomers: TPU, TPE]

    C --> C1[Powder: GA, PA, WA, PREP]
    C --> C2[Wire: GMAW, PAW feedstock]
    C --> C3[Typical Alloys: Ti6Al4V, AlSi10Mg, 316L, Inconel]

    D --> D1[Oxide Ceramics: Al2O3, ZrO2]
    D --> D2[Non-Oxide: SiC, Si3N4]
    D --> D3[Bioceramics: HA, TCP]

    E --> E1[Particle Reinforced: CF-PLA, CF-PEEK]
    E --> E2[Continuous Fiber: CFF Markforged]
    E --> E3[MMC: AlSi10Mg + SiC]

    F --> F1[Bio-inks: GelMA, Alginate, Collagen]
    F --> F2[Resorbable: PLA, PLGA, PCL]
    F --> F3[Implant Metals: Ti6Al4V, CoCrMo]

    G --> G1[Shape Memory: Nitinol, PLA-based SMP]
    G --> G2[4D Printing Stimuli: Heat, Light, pH]
```

## 4.2 Powder Production Process Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Metal Melting] --> B[Method Selection]
    B --> C[Gas Atomization]
    B --> D[Plasma Atomization]
    B --> E[Water Atomization]
    B --> F[PREP]

    C --> C1[Highly spherical, 10-150 um, used in SLM/DMLS]
    D --> D1[Ultra spherical, high purity, used for Ti]
    E --> E1[Irregular shape, low cost, used in Binder Jetting]
    F --> F1[Spherical, larger size, niche aerospace]

    C1 --> G[Powder Bed Fusion]
    D1 --> G
    E1 --> H[Binder Jetting]
    F1 --> G
```

## 4.3 Material-Process Compatibility Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[FFF / FDM] --> M1[Thermoplastic Filament]
    P2[SLA / DLP] --> M2[Photopolymer Resin]
    P3[SLS] --> M3[Polymer Powder / Metal Powder / Ceramic Powder]
    P4[SLM / DMLS] --> M4[Metal Powder]
    P5[EBM] --> M5[Metal Powder - conductive]
    P6[DED] --> M6[Metal Powder or Wire]
    P7[Material Jetting] --> M7[Photopolymer droplets or wax]
    P8[Binder Jetting] --> M8[Polymer / Metal / Sand Powder + Liquid Binder]
    P9[Bioprinting] --> M9[Bio-ink hydrogel]

    M1 -.heat.-> E1[Heated nozzle]
    M2 -.UV.-> E2[UV / Visible light]
    M3 -.laser.-> E3[CO2 or fiber laser]
    M4 -.laser.-> E4[Fiber laser]
    M5 -.electron beam.-> E5[High vacuum e-beam]
    M6 -.laser / arc.-> E6[Coaxial nozzle]
    M7 -.UV.-> E7[Inkjet head with UV curing]
    M8 -.binder.-> E8[Printhead deposits binder]
    M9 -.extrusion.-> E9[Pneumatic or piston]
```

## 4.4 Powder Characterization Parameter Interrelationships

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Powder Lot] --> B[Sieve Analysis / Laser Diffraction]
    A --> C[Scanning Electron Microscopy]
    A --> D[Hall Flowmeter Test]
    A --> E[Tap Density Test]
    A --> F[Inert Gas Fusion / ICP-OES]

    B --> B1[PSD: D10, D50, D90]
    C --> C1[Sphericity, Aspect Ratio, Surface Morphology]
    D --> D1[Flow Rate sec per 50g]
    E --> E1[Bulk Density, Tap Density]
    F --> F1[O, N, H, S Content ppm]

    B1 --> G[Quality Decision]
    C1 --> G
    D1 --> G
    E1 --> G
    F1 --> G

    G --> H{All specs met?}
    H -->|Yes| I[Approve for Build]
    H -->|No| J[Re-process: Sieve, Blend, or Reject]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**List any three polymers commonly used in FDM/FFF additive manufacturing and mention one key application area for each.**

**Model Answer:**
1. **PLA (Polylactic Acid):** Most widely used in FFF; used in education, prototyping, and hobbyist prints due to ease of printing, low warping, and biodegradability.
2. **ABS (Acrylonitrile Butadiene Styrene):** Used in functional prototypes, jigs, and fixtures; offers better toughness and heat resistance than PLA, but requires a heated bed and enclosed chamber.
3. **PEEK (Polyether Ether Ketone):** A high-performance thermoplastic; used in aerospace structural components and medical implants (e.g., spinal cages) due to its high strength-to-weight ratio and biocompatibility.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**What is the significance of powder sphericity in metal additive manufacturing?**

**Model Answer:**
Powder sphericity is a critical quality attribute for metal AM feedstocks. A higher sphericity (close to 1.0) ensures:
- **Uniform powder flow** during recoating, which is essential for consistent layer deposition in SLM/DMLS/EBM.
- **Higher packing density** of the powder bed, leading to denser final parts with fewer defects.
- **Reduced entrapment of gas** (argon/nitrogen) between particles, which would otherwise form porosity.
- **Lower oxidation risk** because spherical particles have lower specific surface area than irregular ones.

Irregular, non-spherical powders (e.g., water-atomized) lead to poor flow, uneven layers, and higher porosity—suitable for binder jetting but problematic for powder bed fusion.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A — 14 Marks [Internal Choice Option A]
**[KTU University Exam - Model Question, Module 2]**

**(a)** Classify the various materials used in additive manufacturing processes. Discuss in detail the polymers used in FDM and SLA processes, highlighting their specific requirements and at least two examples in each category. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the various powder production methods used in metal AM. Discuss the powder characterization parameters (PSD, sphericity, flowability, Hausner ratio) with their significance. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Solution A(a) — Polymers in FDM and SLA

**Classification of AM Materials:**
Additive manufacturing materials can be classified into six major groups: **polymers, metals, ceramics, composites, biomaterials, and smart materials** (4D printing). Polymers are the most widely used and include thermoplastics (for FDM/FFF, SLS), photopolymers (for SLA/DLP), and elastomers (for SLS, FDM). Metals in powder or wire form are used in SLM, DMLS, EBM, and DED processes. Ceramics in powder, slurry, or photocurable suspension form are used in binder jetting, robocasting, and SLA. Composites include fiber-reinforced thermoplastics and metal matrix composites. Biomaterials include bio-inks (hydrogels) and biocompatible metals/polymers. Smart materials enable 4D printing through stimuli-responsive behavior.

**FDM/FFF Polymers:**
- **Key Requirements:** Consistent filament diameter (1.75 mm or 2.85 mm tolerance ±0.05 mm), low moisture absorption, appropriate melt flow index, $T_g$ above service temperature, and a sufficient gap between $T_m$ and decomposition temperature $T_d$.
- **Examples:**
  1. **PLA:** Biodegradable, low warp, easy to print, $T_m$ ≈ 150–160 °C; used in education and prototypes.
  2. **ABS:** Tough, $T_m$ ≈ 220–230 °C, requires heated bed and enclosure; used in functional prototypes and jigs.
  3. **PETG:** Chemical resistant, food-safe grades available; used in consumer products and enclosures.
  4. **Nylon (PA6/PA12):** Tough, abrasion-resistant; used in industrial end-use parts.

**SLA Photopolymers:**
- **Key Requirements:** Low viscosity (typically < 5 Pa·s at 25 °C), high photoreactivity, appropriate UV absorption (matched to 355–405 nm), low volume shrinkage, and biocompatible options for medical/dental.
- **Examples:**
  1. **Acrylate-based resins:** Fast cure, brittle; used in general prototyping.
  2. **Methacrylate-based resins:** Tough, biocompatible; used in dental models and hearing aids.
  3. **Epoxy-based resins (cationic):** Low shrinkage, high accuracy; used in jewelry and investment casting patterns.

---

#### Solution A(b) — Metal Powder Production & Characterization

**Powder Production Methods:**
1. **Gas Atomization (GA):** Molten metal is disintegrated by a high-velocity inert gas (argon/nitrogen) jet. Produces highly spherical powders (10–150 µm) with good flowability. Most widely used for SLM/DMLS of steels, Ni-, Al-, and Ti-alloys.
2. **Plasma Atomization (PA):** Metal wire/feedstock is melted by plasma torches; produces ultra-spherical, high-purity powders (used for Ti and reactive metals).
3. **Water Atomization (WA):** Molten metal is broken by high-pressure water jets. Irregular shape, lower cost, more surface oxides. Suitable for binder jetting.
4. **Plasma Rotating Electrode Process (PREP):** A rotating metal bar is melted by plasma; centrifugal force ejects spherical droplets. Used for high-purity aerospace alloys.
5. **Electrode Induction Melting Gas Atomization (EIGA):** Suitable for reactive and refractory metals.

**Characterization Parameters:**
- **Particle Size Distribution (PSD):** Typically D10 = 20–30 µm, D50 = 30–40 µm, D90 = 45–55 µm. Too fine → poor flow, oxidation; too coarse → poor layer packing and resolution.
- **Sphericity (Ψ):** Ratio of projected area to a circle of equal perimeter. Ideal ≥ 0.9 for SLM/DMLS. Low Ψ causes recoater streaking.
- **Flowability:** Hall flowmeter (sec/50g); lower time = better flow. Influences recoating consistency.
- **Hausner Ratio (HR):** $\rho_{tap} / \rho_{bulk}$. HR < 1.25 indicates good flow; HR > 1.4 indicates poor flow.
- **Carr Index (CI):** Related to HR; CI < 15% = good flow.
- **Apparent and Tap Density:** Used to compute HR and CI.
- **Oxygen/Nitrogen Content:** For titanium, O < 1000 ppm is desirable; high O embrittles the part.
- **Specific Surface Area:** Affects moisture absorption and oxidation; lower is better for reactive metals.

---

### Question B — 14 Marks [Internal Choice Option B]
**[KTU University Exam - Model Question, Module 2]**

**(a)** Discuss in detail the various categories of metal alloys used in additive manufacturing. For **316L stainless steel** processed by SLM, compute the Volumetric Energy Density (VED) given: laser power = 250 W, scan speed = 1000 mm/s, hatch spacing = 0.12 mm, layer thickness = 0.04 mm. Comment on the resultant densification. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Write notes on **(i)** biomaterials for medical AM, **(ii)** ceramic materials and their processing challenges, and **(iii)** smart materials for 4D printing. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Solution B(a) — Metal Alloys and VED Calculation

**Metal Alloy Categories in AM:**

1. **Titanium Alloys:** Ti-6Al-4V (Grade 5) is the most common — used in aerospace brackets and biomedical implants (ASTM F136). CP-Ti (Grade 2) and Ti-6Al-7Nb (for medical) are also used.
2. **Aluminum Alloys:** AlSi10Mg, AlSi12, and Scalmalloy® — lightweight, used in aerospace and automotive. Hypereutectic AlSi alloys are preferred due to low thermal cracking.
3. **Stainless Steels:** 316L (medical, marine, chemical), 17-4 PH (tooling, aerospace — precipitation hardenable), 304L (general purpose).
4. **Nickel-Based Superalloys:** Inconel 625, Inconel 718, Hastelloy X — used in turbine blades, rocket engine components due to high-temperature strength and corrosion resistance.
5. **Cobalt-Chrome:** CoCrMo (ASTM F75) — used in dental crowns, bridges, and orthopedic implants.
6. **Tool Steels:** H13, Maraging 300 (18Ni300) — used in conformal cooling inserts, dies.
7. **Copper and Copper Alloys:** Pure Cu, CuCrZr — used in heat exchangers, induction coils (high thermal/electrical conductivity).
8. **Refractory Metals:** Tungsten, Tantalum, Molybdenum — used in defense and nuclear applications.

**VED Calculation:**

Given: $P = 250$ W, $v = 1000$ mm/s, $h = 0.12$ mm, $t = 0.04$ mm.

$$E_v = \frac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t} = \frac{250}{1000 \times 0.12 \times 0.04}$$

**Step 1 — Denominator:**

$$1000 \times 0.12 \times 0.04 = 4.8 \quad [\text{mm}^3/\text{s}]$$

**Step 2 — Final division:**

$$E_v = \frac{250}{4.8} = 52.08 \quad [\text{J/mm}^3]$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation for 316L:**
The literature-accepted VED window for 316L SLM is approximately **60 to 100 J/mm³** for densification > 99.5%. A value of **52.08 J/mm³** falls **below** this window, indicating a **lack-of-fusion-dominated regime**. The part will likely exhibit inter-layer porosity, poor mechanical strength, and weak inter-track bonding. To remediate, the user must reduce scan speed or hatch spacing, or increase power.

---

#### Solution B(b) — Notes on Biomaterials, Ceramics, and Smart Materials

**(i) Biomaterials for Medical AM:**
- **Polymers:** PLA, PLGA, PCL (resorbable scaffolds); PEG, GelMA, alginate (hydrogel bio-inks for extrusion bioprinting).
- **Metals:** Ti-6Al-4V (orthopedic and dental implants), CoCrMo (ASTM F75; dental bridges), 316L (surgical instruments).
- **Ceramics:** Hydroxyapatite (HA), β-Tricalcium Phosphate (β-TCP) for bone scaffolds; 45S5 Bioglass® for bone-bonding applications.
- **Applications:** Patient-specific implants (cranial plates, spinal cages), scaffolds for tissue engineering, drug-delivery devices, surgical guides, dental restorations.

**(ii) Ceramic Materials and Processing Challenges:**
- **Types:** Alumina (Al₂O₃), Zirconia (ZrO₂ — used in dental crowns via SLA followed by sintering), Silicon Carbide (SiC), Silicon Nitride (Si₃N₄).
- **Processes:** Binder jetting (ceramic powder + binder, then sintered), SLA/DLP with ceramic-filled photopolymer resins (debinding then sintering), Robocasting/Direct Ink Writing of ceramic pastes.
- **Challenges:**
  1. **High melting temperatures** require post-processing sintering at 1400–1700 °C.
  2. **Brittleness and thermal shock** lead to cracking during rapid heating/cooling.
  3. **Volumetric shrinkage** of 20–30% during sintering causes distortion and dimensional inaccuracy.
  4. **Powder flow** is poor for irregular ceramic powders; specialized feeding systems are needed.
  5. **Machining of sintered ceramics** is difficult and expensive.

**(iii) Smart Materials for 4D Printing:**
- **Definition:** 4D printing = 3D printing + time, where the printed object changes its shape, property, or function in response to an external stimulus.
- **Common Smart Materials:**
  1. **Shape Memory Alloys (SMA):** Nitinol (Ni-Ti) — recovers a pre-defined shape upon heating (used in medical stents, actuators).
  2. **Shape Memory Polymers (SMP):** PLA-based, polyurethane — recoverable strain up to 400%.
  3. **Hydrogels:** pH-, temperature-, or moisture-responsive (used in soft robotics and drug delivery).
  4. **Liquid Crystal Elastomers (LCE):** Light-activated shape change.
- **Applications:** Self-deployable aerospace structures, biomedical stents, adaptive grippers, smart textiles, and soft robotics.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Materials Questions:**
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency in VED:** Most common error — students report VED in J/m³ or J/mm³ without checking. The KTU standard is **J/mm³**. Watch for **µm vs mm** in layer thickness.
> 2. **Confusing $T_g$ and $T_m$:** Many students interchange glass transition and melting temperatures. **Polymers above $T_g$ are rubbery; above $T_m$ they are liquid** (semi-crystalline). Amorphous polymers only have $T_g$.
> 3. **Mixing up Gas and Water Atomization Properties:** Gas atomization → spherical (SLM); Water atomization → irregular (binder jetting). Examiners specifically test this distinction.
> 4. **Hausner Ratio and Carr Index Reversal:** Always HR = $\rho_{tap}/\rho_{bulk}$, never the reverse. CI is in **percent**, HR is dimensionless.
> 5. **Forgetting the photoinitiator function** in SLA — at least one sentence on photoinitiators is mandatory for full marks.
> 6. **Sloppy photopolymer chemistry:** Acrylates ≠ Methacrylates ≠ Epoxies. Examiner awards marks for correct resin chemistry matching the application.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **AM materials** are classified into **polymers, metals, ceramics, composites, biomaterials, and smart materials**.
- **FFF/FDM** uses **thermoplastic filaments** (PLA, ABS, PETG, PA, PEEK, PEI/ULTEM).
- **SLA/DLP** uses **photopolymer resins** (acrylates, methacrylates, epoxies) cured by UV/visible light; cure depth follows **Beer–Lambert law**: $C_d = D_p \cdot \ln(E/E_c)$.
- **SLS** uses **polymer powders** (PA12 dominant).
- **SLM/DMLS/EBM** use **gas-atomized metal powders** (Ti, Al, steel, Ni, Co-Cr, tool steels).
- **DED** uses **metal powder or wire** blown co-axially with a laser or arc.
- **Binder Jetting** tolerates **irregular (water-atomized) powders** and uses a liquid binder.
- **Powder characterization** hinges on **PSD, sphericity, flowability, HR (≤1.25 ideal), CI (≤15% ideal), and oxygen content** (Ti < 1000 ppm).
- **VED = P / (v · h · t)** is the master process metric; optimal windows are alloy-specific (316L: 60–100 J/mm³; AlSi10Mg: 30–70 J/mm³; Ti-6Al-4V: 40–80 J/mm³).
- **Biomaterials** span **bioresorbable polymers, biocompatible metals (Ti, CoCr, 316L), and bioceramics (HA, β-TCP, Bioglass)**.
- **Ceramic AM** always requires **debinding and sintering**; expect **20–30% shrinkage**.
- **4D printing** uses **smart materials** (Nitinol, SMP, hydrogel, LCE) to enable stimuli-responsive post-print shape change.
- **Composites in AM** include **chopped-fiber filaments** (CF-PLA, CF-PEEK) and **continuous fiber reinforcement** (Markforged CFF).
- The relationship between **PSD, packing density, and final part density** is the cornerstone of any metal AM quality discussion.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Process Parameters
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET524 - ADDITIVE MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Process Parameters

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Process Parameters in Additive Manufacturing (AM)** are defined as the *set of controllable, measurable, and adjustable input variables* that govern the physical, thermal, chemical, and mechanical transformations during the layer-by-layer fabrication of a 3D object. They directly determine the **part quality**, **build time**, **dimensional accuracy**, **surface finish**, and **mechanical integrity** of the manufactured component.

In the KTU 2024 scheme framework, process parameters are broadly classified into **four categories** based on the AM system class:

| AM System Class | Representative Process | Key Process Parameters |
|---|---|---|
| **Vat Photo-Polymerization** | Stereolithography (SLA) | Wavelength, Intensity, Exposure Time, Layer Thickness |
| **Material Extrusion** | Fused Deposition Modeling (FDM) | Nozzle Temperature, Bed Temperature, Layer Height, Print Speed, Infill |
| **Powder Bed Fusion (PBF)** | Selective Laser Sintering (SLS) / SLM | Laser Power, Scan Speed, Hatch Spacing, Layer Thickness, Powder Size |
| **Sheet Lamination** | Laminated Object Manufacturing (LOM) | Laser Power, Feed Rate, Sheet Thickness, Adhesive Activation |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Idli Maker" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: Cooking Idlis in a Steamer Mold**
> Imagine you are steaming **idlis** (a South-Indian steamed cake). To get a perfect idli you control: steam *temperature* (analogous to **laser/nozzle temperature**), the *duration* the batter is exposed to steam (**exposure time / scan speed**), the *gap* between batter layers (**layer thickness**), and the *pattern* of batter pouring (**hatch spacing / infill pattern**). If the steam is too hot, the idli burns. If the batter is too thin, it cracks. If the layers are too thick, it doesn't cook inside.
> 
> **Additive Manufacturing is exactly this — except instead of steam and batter, we use lasers, nozzles, and polymer/metal powders.** The "recipe" controlling the outcome is called the **Process Parameter Set**.

## 1.3 Why Process Parameters Matter — The "Quality Triangle"

Every AM process attempts to simultaneously optimize three competing goals, forming what is known as the **AM Quality Triangle**:

> **Quality Triangle Vertices:**
> 1. **Mechanical Strength** (↑ requires higher energy input)
> 2. **Surface Finish** (↑ requires finer layers → longer build time)
> 3. **Build Time / Productivity** (↑ requires thicker layers, lower resolution)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Syllabus Highlight (KTU Module 2):** Any change in a process parameter has a **coupled effect** on multiple output characteristics. Mastering AM requires understanding these *inter-dependencies*, not just memorizing values.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of AM Process Parameters

The KTU 2024 syllabus groups process parameters into **three functional tiers**:

### Tier-1: **Primary (Direct) Parameters**
These are the *user-input* variables set directly on the machine console.

- **Laser Power (P)** — measured in **Watts (W)**
- **Scan Speed (v)** — measured in **mm/s**
- **Hatch Spacing (h)** — measured in **mm**
- **Layer Thickness (t)** — measured in **mm** or **µm**
- **Nozzle Temperature (T_n)** — measured in **°C**
- **Build Platform Temperature (T_b)** — measured in **°C**

### Tier-2: **Derived (Calculated) Parameters**
These are *computed* from primary parameters and govern the actual physical transformation.

- **Volumetric Energy Density (VED)** — J/mm³
- **Material Deposition Rate (MDR)** — mm³/s
- **Line Energy Density (LED)** — J/mm

### Tier-3: **Response (Output) Parameters**
These are the *measured outcomes* on the final part.

- **Tensile Strength (MPa)**
- **Surface Roughness Ra (µm)**
- **Dimensional Deviation (mm)**
- **Porosity (%)**
- **Build Time (hours)**

## 2.2 Process Parameters in FDM (Fused Deposition Modeling)

In FDM, a thermoplastic filament is melted and extruded through a heated nozzle. The **five critical process parameters** are:

1. **Nozzle Temperature (T_n):** Too low → poor layer adhesion. Too high → oozing, stringing, material degradation.
2. **Bed Temperature (T_b):** Controls first-layer adhesion and **warping** (differential cooling shrinkage).
3. **Layer Height (t):** Smaller layer height → better resolution but longer build time. Typically **0.1 mm to 0.3 mm**.
4. **Print Speed (v):** Higher speed → faster build but may cause under-extrusion.
5. **Infill Density & Pattern:** Influences strength-to-weight ratio.

> [!NOTE]
> **Dimensional accuracy formula in FDM:**
> $$\Delta D = f(t,\ v,\ T_n,\ T_b)$$
> Where $\Delta D$ is the dimensional deviation. Practically, designers apply a **shrinkage compensation factor** of approximately **0.5% to 2%** for ABS and PLA.

## 2.3 Process Parameters in SLA (Stereolithography)

In SLA, a UV laser photo-cures a liquid resin layer by layer.

- **Critical Dose (E_c):** Minimum UV energy (mJ/cm²) required to initiate polymerization.
- **Penetration Depth (D_p):** Depth (µm) of light into the resin, governed by Beer-Lambert law.
- **Working Curve Equation (Jacobs, 1992):**
$$C_d = D_p \cdot \ln\left(\frac{E_{\max}}{E_c}\right)$$
Where $C_d$ is the **cured depth**, $E_{\max}$ is the **maximum exposure energy** at the resin surface.

## 2.4 Process Parameters in Powder Bed Fusion (SLM / SLS)

This is the **most heavily tested area** in KTU university exams.

### The Master Equation — Volumetric Energy Density (VED)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **VED (E_v)** is the single most important derived parameter in PBF. It quantifies the total energy delivered per unit volume of powder:
> $$E_v = \frac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t}$$
> Units: **J/mm³**

### KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Physical Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E_v = \dfrac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t}$ | Volumetric Energy Density (PBF) | J/mm³ |
| 2 | $E_L = \dfrac{P}{v}$ | Linear Energy Density (line scan) | J/mm |
| 3 | $E_A = \dfrac{P}{v \cdot h}$ | Areal Energy Density (per layer) | J/mm² |
| 4 | $C_d = D_p \cdot \ln(E_{\max}/E_c)$ | Jacobs' Working Curve (SLA) | µm |
| 5 | $Q = v \cdot w \cdot t$ | Volumetric material flow (FDM) | mm³/s |
| 6 | $t_{build} = \dfrac{N \cdot t_l}{v_z}$ | Total build time | hours |
| 7 | $R_a \approx k \cdot t^{n}$ | Surface roughness vs layer thickness | µm |
| 8 | $P_{or} = 1 - \dfrac{\rho_{part}}{\rho_{solid}}$ | Relative porosity | dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical LaTeX Note:** When using absolute value signs, ALWAYS use `\vert` instead of `|`. Example: write $\vert x \vert$ not $\vert x\vert$. This prevents markdown table breaking.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace (GE Aviation, Boeing):** PBF process parameters are tuned to print fuel nozzles and turbine blades with optimized VED for **Inconel 718** and **Ti-6Al-4V**.
- **Medical Implants:** SLA parameters control biocompatible resin curing for dental crowns and surgical guides.
- **Automotive (Bugatti, BMW):** FDM parameters for jigs, fixtures, and low-volume functional prototypes.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Volumetric Energy Density (VED)

**Objective:** Derive the expression $E_v = \dfrac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t}$ from first principles.

### Step 1 — Define Laser Power
The laser emits energy at a constant rate $P$ (Watts = J/s).

### Step 2 — Define Linear Energy Density (Energy per unit length of scan)
As the laser moves at scan speed $v$ (mm/s), the energy delivered along a unit length of scan path is:
$$E_L = \frac{P}{v}$$

**Explanation:** If the laser emits $P$ joules every second, and travels $v$ mm in that same second, then per mm it deposits $P/v$ joules. Units: $\dfrac{J/s}{mm/s} = J/mm$.

### Step 3 — Define Areal Energy Density (Energy per unit area of a hatch)
Adjacent scan lines are separated by hatch spacing $h$ (mm). The energy per unit area is:
$$E_A = \frac{E_L}{h} = \frac{P}{v \cdot h}$$

**Explanation:** Divide the linear energy by the spacing between scan lines to get energy per mm². Units: $J/mm^2$.

### Step 4 — Define Volumetric Energy Density (Energy per unit volume)
Each scanned layer has thickness $t$ (mm). To get energy per unit **volume**, divide areal energy by layer thickness:
$$E_v = \frac{E_A}{t} = \frac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t}$$

### Final Master Equation
$$\boxed{E_v = \frac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t}}$$

Units: $\dfrac{W}{mm/s \cdot mm \cdot mm} = \dfrac{J/s}{mm^3/s} = J/mm^3$. **Derivation complete.**

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — VED Calculation

**Problem:** A SLM machine processes **316L stainless steel** with the following parameters:
- Laser Power $P = 200\ W$
- Scan Speed $v = 800\ mm/s$
- Hatch Spacing $h = 0.12\ mm$
- Layer Thickness $t = 0.03\ mm$

**Find the Volumetric Energy Density.**

### Step 1 — Identify inputs
$P = 200\ W,\quad v = 800\ mm/s,\quad h = 0.12\ mm,\quad t = 0.03\ mm$

### Step 2 — Compute denominator
$$v \cdot h \cdot t = 800 \times 0.12 \times 0.03$$
$$= 800 \times 0.0036$$
$$= 2.88\ mm^3/s$$

### Step 3 — Compute VED
$$E_v = \frac{200}{2.88} = 69.44\ J/mm^3$$

**Final Answer:** $E_v \approx 69.44\ J/mm^3$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering interpretation:** For 316L SS, the optimal VED window is **60 – 100 J/mm³**. Our result of **69.44 J/mm³** lies inside this window, indicating good densification is achievable. If $E_v < 60$, expect lack-of-fusion porosity. If $E_v > 100$, expect keyhole porosity.

## 3.3 Derivation of Build Time for FDM

**Objective:** Derive the total build time for an FDM part.

Let:
- $N$ = total number of layers
- $t_l$ = average layer thickness (mm)
- $H$ = total part height (mm) = $N \cdot t_l$
- $v_z$ = vertical build speed (mm/s)

Number of layers:
$$N = \frac{H}{t_l}$$

Time per layer (excluding travel moves) $\approx t_{\text{layer}}$.

Total build time:
$$t_{build} = N \cdot t_{\text{layer}} = \frac{H \cdot t_{\text{layer}}}{t_l}$$

For simplified estimation, assuming constant $v_z$:
$$t_{build} = \frac{H}{v_z} = \frac{N \cdot t_l}{v_z}$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Each Parameter

| Parameter | ↑ Increase Causes | ↓ Decrease Causes | Optimal Range (SLM-316L) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Laser Power (P)** | Higher melt pool, denser part, possible keyhole | Lower density, lack of fusion | 150 – 250 W |
| **Scan Speed (v)** | Faster build, may cause porosity | Higher energy input, slower build | 600 – 1200 mm/s |
| **Hatch Spacing (h)** | Less overlap, lower density | Better overlap, denser part | 0.08 – 0.15 mm |
| **Layer Thickness (t)** | Faster build, stair-stepping | Better resolution, slower build | 0.02 – 0.05 mm |
| **VED (E_v)** | Densification ↑ then keyholing | Lack-of-fusion porosity | 60 – 100 J/mm³ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — AM Process Parameter Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Design STL File] --> B[Process Parameter Selection]
    B --> C{AM System Class}
    C --> D1[Vat Photopolymerization SLA]
    C --> D2[Material Extrusion FDM]
    C --> D3[Powder Bed Fusion SLM]
    C --> D4[Sheet Lamination LOM]
    D1 --> E1[Params: Wavelength Intensity Exposure Time]
    D2 --> E2[Params: Nozzle Temp Bed Temp Layer Height]
    D3 --> E3[Params: Laser Power Scan Speed Hatch Spacing]
    D4 --> E4[Params: Laser Power Feed Rate Sheet Thickness]
    E1 --> F1[Cured Depth via Jacobs Equation]
    E2 --> F2[Material Flow Rate Q v w t]
    E3 --> F3[Volumetric Energy Density P over v h t]
    E4 --> F4[Sheet Bonding Quality]
    F1 --> G[Part Quality Output]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    F4 --> G
    G --> G1[Mechanical Strength]
    G --> G2[Surface Roughness]
    G --> G3[Dimensional Accuracy]
    G --> G4[Porosity]
    G1 --> H[Final Part Certification]
    G2 --> H
    G3 --> H
    G4 --> H
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Coupling Effects of Process Parameters

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    LP[Laser Power P] --> VED[VED Ev]
    SS[Scan Speed v] --> VED
    HS[Hatch Spacing h] --> VED
    LT[Layer Thickness t] --> VED
    VED --> MP[Melt Pool Dynamics]
    MP --> DEN[Part Density]
    MP --> STR[Tensile Strength]
    MP --> RES[Residual Stress]
    DEN --> QC[Quality Control]
    STR --> QC
    RES --> QC
    QC --> ACC[Accept or Reject Part]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — FDM Process Parameter Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Filament Feed] --> B[Heated Nozzle Tn]
    B --> C[Molten Extrusion]
    C --> D[Layer Deposition Height t]
    D --> E[Build Platform Tb]
    E --> F[Part Cooling and Solidification]
    F --> G{Quality Check}
    G -->|Pass| H[Next Layer]
    G -->|Fail Warping| I[Adjust Tb Upward]
    G -->|Fail Under Extrusion| J[Increase Tn]
    G -->|Fail Poor Adhesion| K[Reduce Print Speed v]
    H --> A
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Note:** The above flowcharts represent the **functional control loop** of process parameters, illustrating how changes in inputs propagate to outputs — this is the KTU Module 2 conceptual focus.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define Volumetric Energy Density. State its significance in Selective Laser Melting.*

**Model Answer:**

> **Volumetric Energy Density (VED)** is the amount of laser energy delivered per unit volume of powder in Powder Bed Fusion processes. It is given by:
> $$E_v = \frac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t}$$
> where $P$ is laser power, $v$ is scan speed, $h$ is hatch spacing, and $t$ is layer thickness. **Unit:** J/mm³.
> 
> **Significance:** VED governs the **melt pool size, density, porosity, and mechanical strength** of SLM parts. An optimal VED window ensures full melting of powder; lower VED causes lack-of-fusion porosity, while higher VED causes keyhole porosity and vaporization.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*List any six process parameters affecting the quality of parts produced by Fused Deposition Modeling (FDM).*

**Model Answer:**

The six primary process parameters affecting FDM part quality are:

1. **Nozzle Temperature (T_n)** — controls melt viscosity and inter-layer bonding.
2. **Build Platform Temperature (T_b)** — controls first-layer adhesion and warping.
3. **Layer Height (t)** — affects resolution and surface finish.
4. **Print Speed (v)** — affects extrusion quality and build time.
5. **Infill Density (%)** — controls strength-to-weight ratio.
6. **Raster Angle (°)** — affects anisotropic mechanical strength.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model Paper]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the classification of process parameters in Additive Manufacturing. Differentiate between **primary**, **derived**, and **response** parameters using a suitable example from Powder Bed Fusion. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Derive the expression for **Volumetric Energy Density (VED)** in SLM. A SLM machine uses the following settings: $P = 250\ W$, $v = 1000\ mm/s$, $h = 0.10\ mm$, $t = 0.04\ mm$. Calculate the VED and comment on whether it lies in the optimal range for Ti-6Al-4V (optimal: 60 – 120 J/mm³). **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a)

**Classification of Process Parameters:**

**Primary Parameters** — User-input variables set on the machine:
- Laser Power (P), Scan Speed (v), Hatch Spacing (h), Layer Thickness (t), Oxygen Content in chamber.

**Derived Parameters** — Calculated from primary inputs:
- VED, Linear Energy Density, Areal Energy Density.

**Response Parameters** — Measured output characteristics:
- Density, Tensile Strength, Surface Roughness, Dimensional Deviation.

> **Example (SLM of Ti-6Al-4V):** Setting $P = 250\ W$ (primary) yields $E_v = 250/(1000 \cdot 0.10 \cdot 0.04) = 62.5\ J/mm^3$ (derived), which produces a part with density 99.2% and tensile strength 950 MPa (response).

#### Model Solution for (b)

**Derivation of VED:**

As derived in Section 3.1:
$$E_v = \frac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t}$$

**Numerical Calculation:**

Given: $P = 250\ W$, $v = 1000\ mm/s$, $h = 0.10\ mm$, $t = 0.04\ mm$

**Step 1 — Compute denominator:**
$$v \cdot h \cdot t = 1000 \times 0.10 \times 0.04 = 4.0\ mm^3/s$$

**Step 2 — Compute VED:**
$$E_v = \frac{250}{4.0} = 62.5\ J/mm^3$$

**Comment:** Since **62.5 J/mm³** lies within the optimal window of **60 – 120 J/mm³** for Ti-6Al-4V, the part is expected to show high density (>99%) with minimal porosity.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the major process parameters in **Stereolithography (SLA)** with the help of Jacobs' working curve equation. Define **critical energy** and **penetration depth**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the process parameters in **Fused Deposition Modeling (FDM)** with respect to their effect on **dimensional accuracy** and **mechanical strength**. Support your answer with a labeled block diagram of the FDM process. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a)

**Key SLA Process Parameters:**

1. **Wavelength ($\lambda$):** Typically **355 nm** (UV) for most commercial resins. Must match the photoinitiator absorption peak.
2. **Intensity (I):** Power per unit area (mW/cm²) of the laser spot.
3. **Exposure Time (t_exp):** Duration the laser dwells on a pixel.
4. **Critical Energy (E_c):** Minimum energy (mJ/cm²) required to initiate polymerization at the resin surface.
5. **Penetration Depth (D_p):** Depth (µm) to which light penetrates into the resin, governed by Beer-Lambert law.

**Jacobs' Working Curve Equation:**
$$C_d = D_p \cdot \ln\left(\frac{E_{\max}}{E_c}\right)$$

Where:
- $C_d$ = cured depth (µm)
- $E_{\max} = I \cdot t_{\text{exp}}$ = maximum exposure energy at the surface
- $D_p$ = penetration depth (a resin property)
- $E_c$ = critical energy (a resin property)

**Engineering Use:** By plotting $C_d$ vs $\ln(E_{\max})$, a straight line is obtained. Its slope gives $D_p$ and x-intercept gives $E_c$. This is used to select exposure parameters for a desired layer thickness.

#### Model Solution for (b)

**FDM Process Parameters and Their Effects:**

| Parameter | Effect on Dimensional Accuracy | Effect on Mechanical Strength |
|---|---|---|
| **Nozzle Temp (T_n)** | Higher T → more shrinkage on cooling | Higher T → better inter-layer bonding → ↑ strength |
| **Bed Temp (T_b)** | Higher T → less warping, better first-layer accuracy | Affects first-layer adhesion |
| **Layer Height (t)** | Smaller t → better accuracy, finer details | Smaller t → more inter-layer bonds → ↑ strength |
| **Print Speed (v)** | High v → vibration, inaccuracy | High v → poor bonding, ↓ strength |
| **Infill Density** | Minimal direct effect | ↑ density → ↑ strength, ↑ weight |
| **Raster Angle** | Minimal direct effect | Affects anisotropy; 0°/90° gives best strength |

**Block Diagram (FDM):**

Filament → Heated Nozzle (T_n) → Extruded Bead → Layer Deposition (t) → Platform (T_b) → Cooling → Next Layer

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> - Students often **skip units** in the final answer (e.g., write 62.5 instead of 62.5 J/mm³) — **lose 0.5 mark**.
> - In derivation questions, examiners want to see **the meaning of each term** — not just substitution.
> - In FDM questions, students must **distinguish** between parameters affecting *strength* vs *accuracy* — confusing them loses 2-3 marks.
> - **Do NOT** confuse *Linear* ($E_L$), *Areal* ($E_A$), and *Volumetric* ($E_v$) energy densities — this is the **most repeated mistake** in KTU valuation.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Process Parameters (Module 2)**

- ✅ Process parameters are classified into **Primary**, **Derived**, and **Response** types.
- ✅ The **master equation** for PBF processes is $E_v = P / (v \cdot h \cdot t)$ — units **J/mm³**.
- ✅ For 316L SS, optimal VED = **60–100 J/mm³**; for Ti-6Al-4V = **60–120 J/mm³**.
- ✅ In **SLA**, Jacobs' working curve is $C_d = D_p \cdot \ln(E_{\max}/E_c)$.
- ✅ In **FDM**, the 6 critical parameters are: **T_n, T_b, t, v, infill, raster angle**.
- ✅ **Warping** in FDM is controlled by **bed temperature** and **enclosure temperature**.
- ✅ **Lack-of-fusion porosity** occurs at *low* VED; **keyhole porosity** at *high* VED.
- ✅ **Surface roughness Ra** is empirically related to **layer thickness** by $R_a \approx k \cdot t^n$.
- ✅ Always state **units** in numerical answers — marks are deducted otherwise.
- ✅ Process parameters are **inter-dependent** — changing one affects multiple outputs (the AM Quality Triangle).
- ✅ **Critical energy $E_c$** and **penetration depth $D_p$** are *resin properties* in SLA, not machine settings.
- ✅ Build time estimation: $t_{build} = H / v_z = N \cdot t_l / v_z$.
- ✅ Relative porosity: $P_{or} = 1 - \rho_{part}/\rho_{solid}$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Advantages and Applications of: Stereo Lithography (SLA)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET524 - ADDITIVE MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Advantages and Applications of: Stereo Lithography (SLA)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stereo Lithography (SLA)** is a **Vat Photopolymerization** based Additive Manufacturing process, patented by **Charles (Chuck) W. Hull in 1984** (US Patent 4,575,330) and commercialized in **1988 by 3D Systems Inc.** The process uses a focused, computer-controlled **ultra-violet (UV) laser beam** to selectively cure and solidify a **photosensitive liquid photopolymer resin** layer-by-layer, where each cured layer represents a cross-sectional slice of the 3D CAD model derived via the **STL file format**.

The process falls under the **ISO/ASTM 52900:2021** classification of **"Vat Polymerization"** and is recognized as the **first commercialized AM technology** in the world.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy: "Building a Cake, One Iced Layer at a Time"** 🍰
>
> Imagine you have a giant tray of **liquid white chocolate icing** (the photopolymer resin). Now, instead of pouring, you use a tiny **UV flashlight (the laser)** that you move precisely across the surface. Wherever the light touches, the icing **magically hardens** (photopolymerization) into a solid layer. You let it harden for a moment, then slide the tray down a millimeter, spread a fresh layer of icing on top, and repeat — tracing the **next slice of your cake design** until you have a complete 3D cake. That is exactly how SLA works!

- The **UV laser** = the "magic flashlight" that hardens the resin.
- The **photopolymer resin** = the "white chocolate icing."
- The **build platform** = the "tray" that dips down.
- The **STL file** = the recipe that guides every move of the laser.

### 1.3 Standard Performance Metrics (SLA Benchmarks)

> [!NOTE]
> **Typical SLA System Parameters (Industrial-Grade Benchmarks):**
> - **Layer thickness (Z-resolution):** **25 – 100 µm** (0.025 – 0.1 mm)
> - **Lateral (XY) resolution:** **25 – 75 µm**
> - **Laser source:** **UV Laser Diode / He-Cd (325 nm) / Solid-State Nd:YAG (355 nm)**
> - **Resin curing wavelength:** **355 – 405 nm**
> - **Build envelope (typical):** **145 × 145 × 175 mm** to **800 × 800 × 600 mm**
> - **Surface roughness (Ra):** **0.8 – 3.0 µm** (as-built)
> - **Tolerances:** **± 0.1 mm** (for parts under 100 mm)
> - **Post-cure shrinkage:** **0.1 – 0.5 %**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Recap (Why SLA has these Advantages)

Before listing advantages, it is critical to understand **why** SLA has them. The advantages stem directly from the physics of the process:

1. **No-contact curing:** The laser never touches the part physically → no mechanical tool wear, no part-tool contact stress.
2. **Liquid-to-solid transition:** No thermal distortion (unlike FDM's heat) → high dimensional accuracy.
3. **Layer-by-layer via optics:** The beam is steered by **galvanometer mirrors** → sub-pixel accuracy.
4. **Cross-linking of monomers:** Generates **isotropic, near-homogeneous properties** in XY.

### 2.2 The "8 Pillars" of SLA Advantages

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why SLA dominates the prototyping market — a structured breakdown:**

#### A. Dimensional & Surface Quality Advantages
- **Superior Surface Finish:** SLA produces the **smoothest surface finish** among all polymer AM processes (Ra ≈ 0.8 µm), suitable for **Class-A aesthetic prototypes** (e.g., automotive interior mockups).
- **High Dimensional Accuracy:** Typical tolerance of **± 0.1 mm** (under 100 mm part size). Best-in-class among all polymer 3D printers.
- **Fine Feature Resolution:** Capable of producing **micro-features (< 100 µm)**, ideal for microfluidics, filters, and intricate jewelry patterns.
- **Watertight & Hermetic Parts:** Each layer is fully bonded to the previous — produces **fluid-tight geometries** without seams.

#### B. Mechanical & Material Advantages
- **Isotropic Mechanical Properties:** Due to uniform photopolymerization, parts behave similarly in X, Y, and Z (after proper post-cure), unlike FDM (anisotropic).
- **Wide Range of Engineering Resins:** Standard, tough, flexible (Shore A 50–95), clear, high-temperature (HDT > 130 °C), castable, dental, and biocompatible resins are available.
- **High Optical Clarity:** Clear resins transmit light up to **90 % transparency** post-cure — used for **lenses and light pipes**.

#### C. Process & Economic Advantages
- **No Tooling Required:** Eliminates the **NRE (Non-Recurring Engineering) cost** of injection molds — perfect for low-volume, high-mix production.
- **Fast Lead Time:** A typical 50 mm prototype is produced in **2 – 6 hours**, vs. **2 – 4 weeks** for traditional tooling.
- **Complex Internal Geometries:** Internal lattices, channels, and undercuts are achievable with **sacrificial supports** (that dissolve in NaOH/water).
- **Repeatability & Reproducibility:** With controlled laser energy density, batch-to-batch variation is minimal — **ISO 9001 friendly**.

#### D. Application-Driven Advantages
- **Direct Pattern for Investment Casting:** SLA patterns burn out cleanly with **< 0.05 % ash residue** when using **castable resin** — replaces wax injection.
- **Excellent Master for Silicone Molding:** Master patterns are dimensionally stable and can be used for **vacuum casting (polyurethane replication)**.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Essential SLA Equations (Board-Exam Ready):**

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units | Engineering Utility |
|---|--------------------|-----------|-------|---------------------|
| 1 | **Cure Depth (Cd)** — Beer's Law for photopolymerization | $C_d = D_p \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{E_0}{E_c}\right)$ | µm | Predicts how deep the laser polymerizes a single spot. |
| 2 | **Energy Dose (E)** delivered per unit area | $E = \dfrac{P}{v \cdot s}$ | mJ/cm² | Controls cure — too low = no cure, too high = over-cure. |
| 3 | **Critical Exposure (Ec)** — minimum energy to gel resin | $E_c$ (resin property) | mJ/cm² | Threshold below which polymerization does not occur. |
| 4 | **Penetration Depth (Dp)** — depth at which intensity drops to 1/e | $D_p = \dfrac{1}{\alpha}$ | µm | Material constant (resin property). |
| 5 | **Working Curve (Jacob's Working Curve)** | $C_d = D_p \cdot \ln(E_0) - D_p \cdot \ln(E_c)$ | µm | Linear plot of Cd vs ln(E) — used to characterize resin. |
| 6 | **Layer Thickness Validation** | $C_d \geq C_z$ (cure depth ≥ layer thickness) | µm | Ensures proper inter-layer adhesion. |
| 7 | **Build Time (Approx.)** | $T \approx \dfrac{V_{part}}{A_{spot} \cdot v_{scan} \cdot h} \cdot k$ | hours | Estimation for process planning. |
| 8 | **Post-Cure Shrinkage** | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{L_{pre} - L_{post}}{L_{pre}} \times 100\,\%$ | % | Compensated in CAD scaling. |

**Where:**
- $E_0$ = energy at the resin surface
- $P$ = laser power
- $v$ = scan speed
- $s$ = hatch spacing
- $h$ = layer thickness
- $\alpha$ = absorption coefficient of the resin
- $V_{part}$ = part volume
- $k$ = process efficiency factor (typically 0.1 – 0.4)

### 2.4 Real-World Utility of These Equations

> [!TIP]
> **Engineer's use-case:** When qualifying a new resin on a Form 3 (3D Systems / Formlabs), the technician first runs a **Window-Pane Test** to generate a **Jacob's Working Curve**. From the slope and intercept, $D_p$ and $E_c$ are extracted. These two parameters then **dictate all process parameters** — laser power, scan speed, and layer height — for that specific resin. Without this data, parts will be either **under-cured (weak, sticky)** or **over-cured (brittle, warped)**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Jacob's Working Curve (Cure Depth Model)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The fundamental SLA equation governs EVERY advantage SLA has over other AM processes — accuracy, resolution, surface finish.**

#### Step 1 — State the physical principle

When a UV laser hits the surface of a photopolymer resin, its intensity $I(z)$ decreases exponentially as it penetrates the liquid, due to **absorption and scattering** (governed by the **Beer–Lambert Law**).

$$I(z) = I_0 \cdot e^{-\alpha \cdot z}$$

**Text Logic:** Intensity at depth $z$ equals the surface intensity times exponential decay; $\alpha$ is the absorption coefficient (resin property).

---

#### Step 2 — Define the penetration depth

The **penetration depth $D_p$** is defined as the depth at which intensity drops to a value of $I_0/e$ (i.e., $I/I_0 = 1/e$).

$$I(D_p) = \dfrac{I_0}{e} = I_0 \cdot e^{-\alpha \cdot D_p}$$

Solving for $D_p$:

$$e^{-\alpha \cdot D_p} = e^{-1} \implies -\alpha \cdot D_p = -1$$

$$\boxed{D_p = \dfrac{1}{\alpha}}$$

**Text Logic:** By definition of $D_p$, the exponent must equal $-1$, so dividing through gives penetration depth as the reciprocal of the absorption coefficient.

---

#### Step 3 — Set up the photopolymerization condition

For the resin to cure, the energy delivered at depth $z$ must exceed the **critical exposure $E_c$** (resin's minimum gel energy).

$$E(z) \geq E_c$$

The energy at depth $z$ is proportional to the intensity at that depth, so:

$$E(z) = E_0 \cdot e^{-\alpha \cdot z} \geq E_c$$

**Text Logic:** The deeper you go, the less energy remains; the deepest depth that still cures is called the **cure depth $C_d$**, which is where $E(z) = E_c$.

---

#### Step 4 — Solve for Cure Depth $C_d$

At the maximum depth that cures (cure depth), the equality holds:

$$E_0 \cdot e^{-\alpha \cdot C_d} = E_c$$

Rearranging:

$$e^{-\alpha \cdot C_d} = \dfrac{E_c}{E_0}$$

Taking the natural log on both sides:

$$-\alpha \cdot C_d = \ln\!\left(\dfrac{E_c}{E_0}\right) = -\ln\!\left(\dfrac{E_0}{E_c}\right)$$

Multiplying by $-1$ and substituting $D_p = 1/\alpha$:

$$\alpha \cdot C_d = \ln\!\left(\dfrac{E_0}{E_c}\right)$$

$$C_d = \dfrac{1}{\alpha} \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{E_0}{E_c}\right)$$

$$\boxed{C_d = D_p \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{E_0}{E_c}\right)}$$

**Text Logic:** This is the **Jacob's Working Curve equation** — a logarithmic relationship that predicts how deep the laser will cure for a given exposure energy.

---

#### Step 5 — Interpret the equation for SLA Advantages

- **Higher $E_0$ (more energy) → deeper cure:** Explains why higher laser power can build thicker layers (faster build, but less accuracy).
- **Lower $E_c$ (more sensitive resin) → deeper cure:** Explains why engineering-grade resins (low $E_c$) achieve fine features.
- **Smaller $D_p$ (opaque resin) → less depth penetration:** Explains why $D_p$ must be greater than layer thickness $C_z$ for proper inter-layer bonding.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: SLA vs Other AM Processes (Comparative Advantage)

> [!NOTE]
> **The single most important comparative table for KTU exams — explaining *why* SLA is chosen over FDM/SLS for specific applications.**

| Parameter | **SLA (Vat Polymerization)** | **FDM (Material Extrusion)** | **SLS (Powder Bed Fusion)** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Surface Roughness (Ra)** | **0.8 – 3.0 µm** (Best) | 8 – 20 µm | 10 – 25 µm |
| **Dimensional Tolerance** | **± 0.1 mm** (Best) | ± 0.3 mm | ± 0.2 mm |
| **Minimum Feature Size** | **50 µm** (Best) | 200 µm | 400 µm |
| **Isotropy** | **Near-isotropic** (Best) | Highly anisotropic | Near-isotropic |
| **Build Speed (Volume/hour)** | Medium | Slow | Fast (bulk parts) |
| **Support Structure** | Required (vertical walls) | Required (everywhere) | **None needed** |
| **Material Cost (per kg)** | High (₹ 8,000 – 15,000) | Low (₹ 1,500 – 3,000) | Medium (₹ 4,000 – 7,000) |
| **Best Application** | **Prototyping, Casting, Dental** | Concept models, Jigs | Functional nylon parts |
| **Watertight Parts** | **Yes (Best)** | Sometimes (delamination risk) | Yes (porous ~ 3-5%) |

### 3.3 The "Design Rule" Equations for SLA Applications

> [!TIP]
> **Three rules-of-thumb that govern SLA application selection (often asked in KTU 14-mark questions):**

#### Rule 1: Minimum Wall Thickness

For SLA, the minimum printable wall thickness is governed by the laser spot diameter and resin flow:

$$t_{min} = 2 \cdot d_{spot} + \delta_{flow}$$

where $d_{spot}$ = laser spot diameter (typically **75 – 250 µm**), and $\delta_{flow}$ = resin flow margin (~ 0.1 mm).

**Application implication:** SLA's tiny spot diameter enables **thin-walled microfluidic channels** — not possible in FDM.

---

#### Rule 2: Minimum Hole Diameter (XY Plane)

$$D_{hole,min} = d_{spot} \cdot \sqrt{2} \approx 1.4 \cdot d_{spot}$$

For a 100 µm spot: $D_{hole,min} \approx 140 \,\mu m$.

**Application implication:** Drives SLA's use in **jewelry, filter screens, and dental surgical guides** where fine perforations are needed.

---

#### Rule 3: Maximum Overhang Angle (Without Supports)

$$\theta_{max} = 30^\circ \text{ to } 45^\circ \text{ from vertical}$$

Below this angle, the part cures directly onto the previous layer (no supports). Above this, **sacrificial tree-like supports** are required.
