| 8 | Green Density | $\rho_{green} = \rho_{th} \cdot \phi_{pack}$ | kg/m³ | Sizing the part before sintering |
| 9 | Final Density (after infiltration) | $\rho_{final} = \phi_{pack} \cdot \rho_{th} + (1-\phi_{pack}) \cdot \rho_{binder}$ | kg/m³ | For infiltrated plaster parts |
| 10 | Sintering Shrinkage | $\Delta L / L = 1 - \sqrt[3]{\rho_{green} / \rho_{th}}$ | dimensionless | Compensate CAD dimensions |

**Where:**
- $H_{part}$ = part height (m)
- $t_l$ = layer thickness (m)
- $A_{layer}$ = bounding-box area of layer (m²)
- $d_{dot}$ = dot pitch (m)
- $v_{head}$ = print head traverse speed (m/s)
- $w_{head}$ = print head width (number of nozzles × nozzle pitch)
- $d_{noz}$ = nozzle orifice diameter
- $\gamma_{LV}$ = liquid-vapour surface tension of binder
- $\theta$ = contact angle between binder and powder
- $r_{pore}$ = effective pore radius of powder bed
- $\phi_{pack}$ = powder packing fraction
- $\rho_{th}$ = theoretical density of powder material
- $p_{sat}$ = binder saturation fraction (typically **50%–80%**)

## 2.3 Engineering Real-World Utility

- **Foundry Industry:** Sand binder jetting is used by **Voxeljet, ExOne, and voxeljet's VX systems** to produce sand moulds and cores for **aluminium, iron, and steel castings**, replacing traditional wood patterns.
- **Architectural Prototyping:** Full-colour binder jetting (Z Corp 3D printers) is used to create realistic 1:100 scale architectural models in **a single print run**.
- **Medical:** Patient-specific anatomical models (bone, organ) for pre-surgical planning.
- **Mass Production:** HP's **Metal Jet** and Desktop Metal's **Production System** target mass production of metal parts (millions/year) — claimed to be the only AM process with a credible path to **>1000 parts/day**.

## 2.4 Physics of Binder Penetration — The Washburn Equation

The depth $L$ to which the binder wicks into a powder bed of average pore radius $r_{pore}$ in time $t$ is given by the **Washburn equation**:

$$L = \sqrt{\dfrac{r_{pore} \cdot \gamma_{LV} \cdot \cos\theta \cdot t}{2 \cdot \eta_{binder}}}$$

where $\eta_{binder}$ is the dynamic viscosity of the binder (Pa·s). For a typical water-based binder with $\gamma_{LV} \approx 0.072$ N/m, $\theta \approx 30°$, $r_{pore} \approx 20\ \mu m$, $\eta_{binder} \approx 0.001$ Pa·s, the binder penetrates ≈ **0.5 mm in 0.1 s** — which is more than enough to bridge a 0.1 mm layer.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Quick Fact:** If the binder viscosity increases (e.g., by adding more polymer), the penetration depth decreases, causing **layer-delamination defects**. This is the most commonly reported failure mode in BJ.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# BINDER JETTING — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Binder Saturation & Sintering Shrinkage Compensation

**Problem Statement:** A metal part of theoretical density $\rho_{th} = 8900\ \text{kg/m}^3$ (pure copper) is binder-jet printed. The powder bed packing fraction is $\phi_{pack} = 0.60$. After de-binding and sintering, what uniform linear scaling factor $k$ must be applied to the **original CAD model dimensions** so that the sintered part matches the target dimension?

### Step 1 — Identify the Governing Relationship

The green part density equals the powder packing fraction times theoretical density:

$$\rho_{green} = \phi_{pack} \cdot \rho_{th}$$

### Step 2 — Substitute the Numerical Values

$$\rho_{green} = 0.60 \times 8900 = 5340\ \text{kg/m}^3$$

### Step 3 — Apply the Volumetric Shrinkage Equation

If a volume $V_0$ of green material (with mass $m = \rho_{green} V_0$) is fully sintered to theoretical density, the new volume $V_f$ satisfies mass conservation:

$$m = \rho_{th} \cdot V_f = \rho_{green} \cdot V_0$$

$$V_f = \dfrac{\rho_{green}}{\rho_{th}} \cdot V_0 = \phi_{pack} \cdot V_0$$

### Step 4 — Derive the Linear Shrinkage Fraction

The linear dimensions shrink isotropically by a factor $\alpha$ per axis, so the volume scales by $\alpha^3$:

$$\alpha^3 = \dfrac{V_f}{V_0} = \phi_{pack}$$

$$\alpha = \sqrt[3]{\phi_{pack}} = \sqrt[3]{0.60} \approx 0.8434$$

### Step 5 — Compute the Required CAD Scaling Factor $k$

To compensate, every CAD linear dimension must be **inflated** by:

$$k = \dfrac{1}{\alpha} = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt[3]{\phi_{pack}}} = \dfrac{1}{0.8434} \approx 1.186$$

### Step 6 — Verify with an Example

If the target dimension is 10 mm, the CAD should be drawn as $10 \times 1.186 = 11.86$ mm. After sintering, the part shrinks to:

$$L_{final} = k^{-1} \cdot L_{CAD} = 0.8434 \times 11.86 = 10.00\ \text{mm} \quad \checkmark$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Total Build Time for a Rectangular Box

**Problem Statement:** A hollow rectangular box has external dimensions $L \times W \times H = 50 \times 30 \times 25\ \text{mm}$, with a uniform wall thickness of 2 mm. The layer thickness is $t_l = 0.1\ \text{mm}$. The print head has $N_{noz} = 96$ nozzles spaced at $p = 0.085\ \text{mm}$, head traverse speed $v_{head} = 0.3\ \text{m/s}$, and the per-pass droplet pitch is $d_{dot} = 0.085\ \text{mm}$. Setup time is $t_{setup} = 600\ \text{s}$. Estimate the build time.

### Step 1 — Compute the Number of Layers

$$N_z = \left\lceil \dfrac{H}{t_l} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \dfrac{25}{0.1} \right\rceil = 250$$

### Step 2 — Compute the Effective Print Head Width

$$w_{head} = N_{noz} \cdot p = 96 \times 0.085 = 8.16\ \text{mm}$$

### Step 3 — Compute the Bounding-Box Layer Area

The slice has a perimeter frame (outer box minus inner box):

$$A_{layer} = (L \cdot W) - (L - 2t) \cdot (W - 2t)$$

Substituting $t = 2\ \text{mm}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
A_{layer} &= (50 \times 30) - (46 \times 26) \\
&= 1500 - 1196 \\
&= 304\ \text{mm}^2
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 4 — Compute the Scan Time per Layer

Convert units consistently: $A_{layer}$ in m², $d_{dot}$ in m, $v_{head}$ in m/s, $w_{head}$ in m.

$$A_{layer} = 304 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{m}^2$$
$$d_{dot} = 85 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{m}$$
$$w_{head} = 8.16 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m}$$

$$t_{scan} = \dfrac{A_{layer} \cdot d_{dot}}{v_{head} \cdot w_{head}} = \dfrac{304 \times 10^{-6} \times 85 \times 10^{-6}}{0.3 \times 8.16 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Numerator} &= 304 \times 85 \times 10^{-12} = 25{,}840 \times 10^{-12} = 2.584 \times 10^{-8} \\
\text{Denominator} &= 2.448 \times 10^{-3} \\
t_{scan} &= \dfrac{2.584 \times 10^{-8}}{2.448 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 1.0556 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{s/layer}
\end{aligned}$$

> **Important correction:** The above formula gives the *time to fire the same number of drops*; in practice one must also consider the **number of stripes (passes)** the head must make to cover the bounding box of the slice. Re-deriving with stripe count:

### Step 4 (Corrected) — Stripe-Based Scan Time

Number of head passes across the bounding-box width $W$:

$$N_{pass} = \left\lceil \dfrac{W}{w_{head}} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \dfrac{30}{8.16} \right\rceil = 4$$

Length of each pass ≈ $L$ (with overshoot for acceleration):

$$t_{pass} = \dfrac{L}{v_{head}} = \dfrac{50 \times 10^{-3}}{0.3} \approx 0.1667\ \text{s}$$

Per-layer scan time:

$$t_{scan} = N_{pass} \cdot t_{pass} = 4 \times 0.1667 = 0.667\ \text{s}$$

### Step 5 — Total Build Time

$$T_{build} = N_z \cdot t_{scan} + t_{setup}$$

$$T_{build} = 250 \times 0.667 + 600 = 166.7 + 600 = 766.7\ \text{s} \approx 12.8\ \text{min}$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Defects and Their Root Causes

| Defect | Visual Symptom | Root Cause | Mitigation |
|---|---|---|---|
| Layer delamination | Horizontal cracks | Insufficient binder penetration | Reduce binder viscosity or increase droplet size |
| Porosity (in metal BJ) | Internal voids | Green density too low | Use finer powder, optimize packing |
| Incomplete curing | Soft, friable parts | Under-saturation of binder | Increase binder saturation $p_{sat}$ |
| Powder shift | Geometric inaccuracy | Recoater collision with part | Add sacrificial shell or offset |
| Bleeding | Binder spreads outside geometry | Excess binder volume | Reduce print head voltage / duty cycle |
| Sintering distortion | Warping, non-uniform shrinkage | Anisotropic green density | Use support powder bed; tune sintering cycle |

## 3.4 Comparative Analytical Matrix — Binder Jetting vs Other AM Processes

| Criterion | Binder Jetting | SLS (Polymer) | SLM (Metal) | FDM |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Heat during print? | **No** | Yes (laser) | Yes (laser) | Yes (extrusion) |
| Support structures? | **Not needed** | Not needed | Required | Required |
| Material variety | **Very High** (any powder + binder) | Limited polymers | Limited metals | Thermoplastics only |
| Green part strength | Low (handle with care) | Medium | N/A (solid) | Medium |
| Post-processing | **Mandatory** (depowder, sinter, infiltrate) | Minimal | Minimal (support removal) | Minimal |
| Achievable density | 50%–95% (after sintering) | ~95% | >99% | ~95% |
| Throughput | **Very High** | High | Low | Low |
| Surface finish | **Moderate** (rough) | Good | Excellent | Layer lines visible |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Memory Hook:** Binder Jetting's defining competitive edge is its **combination of high throughput + no heat during printing + no supports**. These three together make it unique among AM processes.

# BINDER JETTING — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Define Binder Jetting. List any four materials that can be processed by this method.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (2 marks):** Binder Jetting is an additive manufacturing process in which a liquid bonding agent is selectively jetted onto a powder bed using an inkjet print head, layer by layer, to build a 3D part without the application of heat during the layer formation stage.
>
> **Materials (1 mark — any four):**
> 1. Foundry sand (silica) — for moulds
> 2. Gypsum / plaster — for colour prototypes
> 3. Stainless steel powder — for metal BJ
> 4. Titanium powder — for biomedical metal BJ
> 5. Ceramic powders — e.g., alumina, zirconia

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**State any three advantages and two limitations of the Binder Jetting process.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Advantages (1½ marks — any three):**
> 1. **No heat input during printing** — eliminates residual stress and warping.
> 2. **No support structures required** — surrounding powder supports the part.
> 3. **High build speed and large build volumes.**
> 4. Compatible with a wide range of materials.
>
> **Limitations (1½ marks — any two):**
> 1. **Low green-part strength** — requires careful handling and post-processing.
> 2. **Mandatory post-processing** (infiltration, sintering) — adds cost and time.
> 3. **Porosity** in the as-printed state reduces mechanical strength.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 1**
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **working principle of the Binder Jetting process**. List the major sub-systems of a binder jetting machine. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **capillary mechanism of binder penetration** into the powder bed. Derive the **Washburn equation** and explain how it determines the choice of layer thickness and binder viscosity. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question A**

#### (a) Working Principle and Sub-Systems (7 marks)

**Working Principle (4 marks):**
Binder Jetting builds a 3D object by **selectively depositing liquid binder onto successive thin layers of powder**. The five sequential steps per layer are:

1. **Powder spreading** — A counter-rotating roller or recoater spreads a uniform layer of powder (typically **0.08–0.2 mm** thick) across the build platform. The feed piston rises, pushing powder into the recoater's path; the build piston simultaneously drops by one layer thickness.
2. **Powder compaction** — The roller compacts the powder lightly to achieve a packing density of **55%–65%** of the theoretical density.
3. **Binder jetting** — A multi-nozzle inkjet print head traverses the build area, ejecting **30–80 picolitre droplets** of binder at locations defined by the sliced 2D geometry of the part. The head typically has **96–256 nozzles** spanning a width of **8–25 mm**.
4. **Binder penetration and setting** — The binder wicks into the powder via **capillary action**, creating inter-particle bonds (chemical, mechanical, or both). Penetration must extend slightly into the **previous layer** to ensure inter-layer adhesion.
5. **Recoat and repeat** — Build platform lowers, fresh powder is spread, and the cycle repeats.

**Major Sub-Systems (3 marks — any 6):**
- CAD slicing and process control software
- Build chamber with feed and build pistons
- Powder delivery and re-coater mechanism
- Multi-nozzle inkjet print head and binder reservoir
- Heated platen (typically **30–60 °C**) for accelerating binder reaction
- Closed-loop sensors (temperature, humidity, powder level)
- Post-processing equipment (depowdering station, curing oven, infiltration tank, sintering furnace)

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Students often confuse the **feed piston** (which moves *up*) with the **build piston** (which moves *down*). Swapping these descriptions will cost **2 marks**.

---

#### (b) Washburn Equation & Capillary Mechanism (7 marks)

**Capillary Mechanism (3 marks):**
When a binder droplet lands on the powder bed, surface tension $\gamma_{LV}$ and the contact angle $\theta$ between the binder and the powder drive the liquid into the inter-particle pores. This is identical to the well-known capillary rise phenomenon. The **Laplace pressure** inside a cylindrical pore of radius $r_{pore}$ is:

$$P_c = \dfrac{2 \cdot \gamma_{LV} \cdot \cos\theta}{r_{pore}}$$

This pressure difference causes the binder front to advance.

**Derivation of the Washburn Equation (3 marks):**

Applying the Hagen-Poiseuille law for viscous flow in a cylindrical pore of radius $r_{pore}$ under a constant pressure $P_c$:

$$Q = \dfrac{\pi \cdot r_{pore}^4 \cdot P_c}{8 \cdot \eta_{binder} \cdot L}$$

where $Q$ is the volumetric flow rate and $L$ is the instantaneous penetration depth. Since $Q = \pi r_{pore}^2 \cdot \dfrac{dL}{dt}$, we have:

$$\pi r_{pore}^2 \cdot \dfrac{dL}{dt} = \dfrac{\pi r_{pore}^4 \cdot P_c}{8 \cdot \eta_{binder} \cdot L}$$

Simplifying:

$$L \cdot dL = \dfrac{r_{pore}^2 \cdot P_c}{8 \cdot \eta_{binder}} \cdot dt$$

Substituting $P_c = \dfrac{2 \gamma_{LV} \cos\theta}{r_{pore}}$ and integrating from $0$ to $L$:

$$L = \sqrt{\dfrac{r_{pore} \cdot \gamma_{LV} \cdot \cos\theta \cdot t}{2 \cdot \eta_{binder}}}$$

**Application to BJ (1 mark):**
For binder to penetrate one full layer plus ~10% of the previous layer (≈ $1.1 t_l$), the time $t$ available (≈ inter-pass dwell time, typically **0.05–0.2 s**) must satisfy:

$$1.1 \cdot t_l \le \sqrt{\dfrac{r_{pore} \cdot \gamma_{LV} \cdot \cos\theta \cdot t}{2 \cdot \eta_{binder}}}$$

This sets the **upper bound on $\eta_{binder}$** and **lower bound on $r_{pore}$**, which in turn constrains the powder size and the binder chemistry.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 2**
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 Retest]**

**(a)** Compare **Binder Jetting** with **Selective Laser Sintering (SLS)** under the headings: (i) heat input, (ii) support requirements, (iii) achievable density, (iv) typical materials, and (v) throughput. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **Metal Binder Jetting workflow** including green-part handling, de-binding, and sintering. How does sintering shrinkage compensation differ from the isotropic scaling used in DMLS/SLM? **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question B**

#### (a) Comparison Table (7 marks)

| Criterion | Binder Jetting | Selective Laser Sintering |
|---|---|---|
| (i) Heat input | **No heat during print** — only mild oven heating (30–60 °C) | **High localised heat** from a CO₂ laser; powder bed pre-heated to just below melting point |
| (ii) Support requirements | **None** — powder bed itself supports the part | **None** — unfused powder supports the part |
| (iii) Achievable density | **50%–60% green** → 80%–95% after sintering/infiltration; never reaches 100% without HP+MIM | **90%–95%** for polymers; lower for metals with full melting in DMLS/SLM |
| (iv) Typical materials | Plaster, sand, metals, ceramics, sugar | Polyamides (PA12, PA11), some metals, TPU |
| (v) Throughput | **High** (entire layer printed in one pass of wide print head) | **Moderate** (laser scans the entire cross-section point-by-point) |

**Award 1 mark per correct row; 1 additional mark for any two additional valid comparative points.**

---

#### (b) Metal Binder Jetting Workflow (7 marks)

**Workflow Stages (4 marks):**

1. **Green Part Printing** — A layer of metal powder (typical PSD **15–45 µm**) is spread, and a polymer binder is jetted. The binder **dissolves a polymer coating** on the powder or **glues** particles at contact points.
2. **Depowdering** — After printing, the build box is removed, and **loose powder is vacuumed or brushed away**. The green part is **extremely fragile** and must be handled with care.
3. **Curing** — The part is placed in a low-temperature oven (≈ **180–200 °C**) for several hours to **fully cross-link the binder** and gain handling strength.
4. **Thermal De-binding** — The part is heated slowly to ≈ **400–600 °C** to **burn out the polymer binder**, leaving a "brown part" with only residual porosity.
5. **Sintering** — The brown part is heated to **0.7–0.9 T_m** of the metal (e.g., **1200–1400 °C** for steels) for several hours. **Diffusion-driven densification** occurs, and the part shrinks.

**Sintering Shrinkage Compensation (3 marks):**

In **DMLS/SLM**, the part is built near-net shape in the final material, so **shrinkage is minimal** (typically <0.5%) and is corrected by trial-and-error per-machine scaling factors.

In **Metal Binder Jetting**, shrinkage is **large and predictable** (typically **12%–20% linear**, or 30%–50% volumetric) because the green part has only 50%–60% theoretical density. Compensation is done **isotropically in the CAD file** using:

$$k = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt[3]{\phi_{pack}}}$$

Every dimension in the STL is scaled by $k$ before printing. The uniformity of shrinkage allows **isotropic** scaling (uniform in X, Y, Z), unlike DMLS where shrinkage is **anisotropic and part-geometry-dependent**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Do **not** state that Metal Binder Jetting achieves 99% density "as-printed." The green part has ~60% density; only **after sintering and optional hot isostatic pressing (HIP)** does it reach **>97%**. Skipping this clarification can cost **1–2 marks**.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!NOTE]
> **Read this 5 minutes before the exam — it is the densest possible summary of this module.**

### Must-Know Definitions
- **Binder Jetting (BJ):** Powder bed AM process using a jetted liquid binder.
- **3DP™:** Original commercial name (Z Corporation) for binder jetting.
- **Green Part:** The as-printed part before sintering — fragile and binder-rich.
- **Brown Part:** The part after de-binding, before sintering.
- **Capillary Action:** The driving force of binder wicking into powder pores.

### Must-Know Formulas
- Number of layers: $N_z = H / t_l$
- Washburn penetration depth: $L = \sqrt{\dfrac{r_{pore} \gamma_{LV} \cos\theta \, t}{2 \eta_{binder}}}$
- Laplace capillary pressure: $P_c = \dfrac{2 \gamma_{LV} \cos\theta}{r_{pore}}$
- Sintering shrinkage compensation factor: $k = 1 / \sqrt[3]{\phi_{pack}}$

### Must-Know Numbers
- Layer thickness: **0.08–0.2 mm**
- Droplet volume: **30–80 picolitres**
- Packing fraction: **55%–65%**
- Sintering temperature: **0.7–0.9 T_m**
- Linear shrinkage during sintering: **12%–20%**
- Print head nozzle count: **96–256**
- Build speed: **20–40 mm/h vertical**

### Must-Know Comparisons
- **BJ vs SLS:** BJ has no heat during print; SLS uses a laser.
- **BJ vs SLM/DMLS:** BJ has higher throughput, lower density, more post-processing.
- **BJ vs FDM:** BJ is powder-based and faster, but requires post-processing.

### Must-Know Applications
- **Plaster BJ:** Architecture, medical models, full-colour prototypes.
- **Sand BJ:** Foundry moulds and cores.
- **Metal BJ:** Mass production of small metal parts, dental, automotive.

### Must-Know Failure Modes
- **Layer delamination** → too-viscous binder
- **Porosity** → low green density
- **Bleeding** → over-saturation
- **Sintering distortion** → anisotropic green density

### High-Frequency KTU Buzzwords
- *"No support structure needed"*
- *"Post-processing is mandatory"*
- *"Capillary action drives binder penetration"*
- *"Isotropic shrinkage compensable in CAD"*
- *"Suitable for mass production due to high throughput"*

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET524 - ADDITIVE MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Material Jetting

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

**Material Jetting (MJ)** is a vat-photopolymerization-class additive manufacturing process in which **build material in the form of discrete liquid droplets is selectively jetted and instantly solidified (cured by ultraviolet light or by cooling) onto a build platform**, layer by layer, to construct a three-dimensional physical object directly from a CAD model.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition to Memorize:**
> *"Material Jetting is a droplet-based AM process wherein liquid photopolymer or wax droplets are jetted from a print head (analogous to a 2D inkjet printer), deposited drop-by-drop, and simultaneously cured by UV light, building the part in a layer-wise fashion with exceptionally high surface finish and dimensional accuracy."*

### 1.2 The Two Sub-Categories

| Sub-Type | Full Form | Droplet Generation Logic |
|----------|-----------|--------------------------|
| **CIJ** | Continuous Inkjet | Continuous stream of droplets, electrically charged, deflected by electrodes |
| **DOD** | Drop-on-Demand | Droplets produced *only* when required, by thermal or piezoelectric actuation |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition (The "Plain-English" View)

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The "3D Inkjet Printer":**
> Imagine a standard paper printer that sprays ink dots. Now imagine that instead of just spraying one color on paper, this printer has **multiple print heads (sometimes thousands of nozzles)**, each spraying a *different* material (or a different color, or a different hardness of resin). The "paper" is actually a moving platform that **lowers slightly** after every pass. After each pass, a **UV flashlight instantly freezes** the wet drops solid. By repeating this thousands of times, a full 3D object rises out of a liquid pool — almost like a magician pulling a statue out of a vat of resin, one microscopic droplet at a time.

### 1.4 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

- **Build Resolution:** **±0.1 mm (X-Y plane)**; layer thickness typically **14–28 µm**
- **Build Speed:** Up to **~10× faster** than Stereolithography (SLA) for similar surface quality
- **Nozzle Diameter:** **10 – 50 µm** (typically **20 µm**)
- **Droplet Volume:** **1 – 100 picolitres (pL)**
- **Curing Wavelength:** **365 – 405 nm (UV-A range)**
- **Standard Material Viscosity:** **10 – 100 mPa·s** at jetting temperature

### 1.5 Classification under ISO/ASTM 52900 (Mandatory KTU Note)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Under **ISO/ASTM 52900:2021**, Material Jetting is officially classified as a **"Material Jetting"** binder-jetting-adjacent process, but it is **distinct from Binder Jetting**. In MJ, the jetted material *itself* forms the part (often a photopolymer). In Binder Jetting, the jetted material is a *binder* that glues a powder bed.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Working Principle (The "How" Behind Material Jetting)

1. **CAD Slicing:** The 3D CAD model is digitally sliced into thousands of horizontal 2D cross-sections of thickness equal to one layer height *h*.
2. **Build Platform Initialization:** The build platform is positioned at the **top of the liquid resin vat** (or just below the print head).
3. **Droplet Ejection:** A multi-nozzle print head (containing **1,000+ nozzles** in industrial systems) ejects **picolitre-sized droplets** of liquid photopolymer onto the build platform along the path defined by the current slice.
4. **In-Situ UV Curing:** A **UV lamp mounted on the same gantry** (or a stationary UV flood lamp) immediately cures/polymerizes the deposited droplets, solidifying them into a thin solid layer.
5. **Platform Translation:** The build platform **moves down by one layer thickness** (e.g., 28 µm).
6. **Recoating:** A roller or blade re-coats a fresh, ultra-thin layer of liquid resin over the previously cured layer.
7. **Layer-by-Layer Build:** Steps 3–6 repeat until the entire part is complete.
8. **Post-Curing:** The part is removed and subjected to a **secondary UV flood cure** in a UV oven for full polymerization.
9. **Support Removal:** Wax-like or dissolvable support structures (jetting from a second print head) are melted or dissolved away.

### 2.2 The "WHY" Behind the Process

> [!NOTE]
> - **Why droplets?** Because droplets allow *pixel-level* control of material placement — much finer than a continuous extrusion.
> - **Why UV curing?** Because photopolymers undergo **chain-growth polymerization** within milliseconds when exposed to UV photons, allowing instant solidification without heating the part (no thermal distortion).
> - **Why multi-nozzle heads?** Because droplet volume is tiny; thousands of nozzles are required to build a part within a practical timeframe (e.g., a 100 mm × 100 mm layer in < 1 minute).

### 2.3 Continuous Inkjet (CIJ) vs Drop-on-Demand (DOD) — The Core Distinction

| Feature | **Continuous Inkjet (CIJ)** | **Drop-on-Demand (DOD)** |
|---|---|---|
| Droplet Generation | Continuous pressurized stream | Droplet only when voltage pulse applied |
| Droplet Rate | Very high (~100 kHz per nozzle) | Lower (~1–30 kHz per nozzle) |
| Deflection | Electrostatic plates deflect unwanted droplets into a gutter | No deflection; all droplets hit target |
| Material Waste | High (un-deflected drops recycled) | Very low (near 100% efficient) |
| Accuracy | Lower (due to drop drift) | **High** — preferred in AM |
| Industrial Use in AM | Rarely used in AM (more in packaging) | **Industry standard for Material Jetting** |

### 2.4 The Two DOD Actuation Mechanisms

| Actuation Type | Mechanism | Typical Frequency |
|---|---|---|
| **Thermal (Bubble Jet)** | A micro-heater vaporizes resin locally → bubble pushes droplet out | Up to ~10 kHz |
| **Piezoelectric** | A piezo crystal deforms under voltage → pressure pulse ejects droplet | Up to ~30 kHz |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU High-Yield Fact: **Piezoelectric DOD is the dominant industrial implementation** for Material Jetting AM systems (e.g., Stratasys PolyJet, 3D Systems MultiJet Printing) because thermal actuation can overheat sensitive photopolymers and biological materials.

### 2.5 Materials Used in Material Jetting

- **Photopolymer Resins** (acrylates, epoxides) — most common
- **Waxes** (for investment casting patterns)
- **Biological cells (bio-ink)** — emerging use in bioprinting
- **Multi-material combinations** (rigid + elastomeric in one build)
- **Support material:** wax-based, water-soluble

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are KTU-exam-relevant for Material Jetting calculations.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V_{\text{drop}} = \dfrac{\pi \, d^{3}}{6}$ | Volume of a single spherical droplet of diameter *d* | m³ |
| 2 | $Q = f \cdot V_{\text{drop}}$ | Volumetric flow rate from one nozzle; *f* = droplet frequency | m³/s |
| 3 | $V_{\text{layer}} = L \cdot W \cdot h$ | Volume of a single printed layer (length × width × layer thickness) | m³ |
| 4 | $N_{\text{layers}} = \dfrac{H}{h}$ | Total number of layers; *H* = part height, *h* = layer thickness | dimensionless |
| 5 | $T_{\text{build}} = N_{\text{layers}} \cdot t_{\text{layer}}$ | Total build time; *t*ᵢ = time per layer | seconds |
| 6 | $\sigma = \dfrac{F}{A}$ | Shear stress on droplet during ejection (viscous resistance) | Pa |
| 7 | $\eta = \dfrac{\sigma}{\dot{\gamma}}$ | Dynamic viscosity (Newton's law) — material must be **< 100 mPa·s** | Pa·s |
| 8 | $R_{\text{xy}} = 2 \cdot d_{\text{nozzle}}$ | X-Y plane print resolution ≈ twice nozzle diameter | m |
| 9 | $\text{We} = \dfrac{\rho \, v^{2} \, L}{\gamma}$ | Weber number — governs droplet stability; must be < critical value | dimensionless |
| 10 | $\text{Re} = \dfrac{\rho \, v \, L}{\eta}$ | Reynolds number — governs flow regime inside the nozzle | dimensionless |

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

Material Jetting is the **gold standard** for:
- **Investment casting pattern production** (foundry master patterns)
- **Multi-material prototypes** (rigid hinge + soft grip in one print)
- **High-fidelity visual prototypes** for automotive and consumer goods design reviews
- **Medical anatomical models** with multiple tissue-mimicking materials
- **Optically clear parts** (after post-processing)
- **Dental wax-ups** and clear aligner models

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Droplet Volume and Nozzle Resolution

**Given:** A DOD print head ejects spherical droplets of diameter $d$ from a circular nozzle of diameter $d_{\text{nozzle}}$.

**Step 1 — Express the droplet as a perfect sphere.**
A picolitre-scale photopolymer droplet, due to surface tension, assumes a spherical shape (the shape of minimum surface energy, governed by the Young-Laplace equation).

**Step 2 — Apply the standard sphere volume equation.**

$$
V_{\text{drop}} = \frac{\pi \, d^{3}}{6}
$$

*Text row:* The volume of a sphere is one-sixth of $\pi$ times the cube of its diameter; this is the fundamental geometric relation.

**Step 3 — Relate droplet diameter to nozzle diameter.**
In ideal DOD operation, the droplet diameter is **slightly larger** than the nozzle diameter due to viscous and surface-tension effects. A first-order approximation is:

$$
d \;\approx\; 1.5 \, d_{\text{nozzle}}
$$

*Text row:* Empirically, the droplet that detaches is roughly 1.5× the orifice diameter; this factor accounts for necking and Rayleigh instability.

**Step 4 — Substituting the typical industrial nozzle diameter of $d_{\text{nozzle}} = 20 \, \mu\text{m} = 20 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{m}$:**

$$
d = 1.5 \times 20 \, \mu\text{m} = 30 \, \mu\text{m} = 3.0 \times 10^{-5} \, \text{m}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the droplet volume.**

$$
V_{\text{drop}} = \frac{\pi \, (3.0 \times 10^{-5})^{3}}{6}
$$

$$
V_{\text{drop}} = \frac{\pi \times 2.7 \times 10^{-14}}{6} = 1.4137 \times 10^{-14} \, \text{m}^{3}
$$

*Text row:* Cubing $3.0 \times 10^{-5}$ gives $2.7 \times 10^{-14}$; multiplying by $\pi$ and dividing by 6 yields the result.

**Step 6 — Convert to picolitres for industrial relevance.**

$$
V_{\text{drop}} = 1.4137 \times 10^{-14} \, \text{m}^{3} \times \frac{10^{12} \, \text{pL}}{1 \, \text{m}^{3}} = 14.14 \, \text{pL}
$$

*Text row:* Since 1 m³ = 10¹² pL, the result is ~14 pL — squarely in the typical industrial range of 1–100 pL.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Build Time for a Simple Rectangular Block

**Given:** A block of dimensions $L = 100 \, \text{mm}$, $W = 50 \, \text{mm}$, $H = 25 \, \text{mm}$, printed at layer thickness $h = 28 \, \mu\text{m} = 2.8 \times 10^{-5} \, \text{m}$, with each layer taking $t_{\text{layer}} = 6 \, \text{s}$ to jet and cure.

**Step 1 — Calculate total number of layers.**

$$
N_{\text{layers}} = \frac{H}{h} = \frac{25 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{m}}{2.8 \times 10^{-5} \, \text{m}} = 892.86 \approx 893
$$

*Text row:* The total height of the part is divided by the layer thickness to obtain the number of horizontal cross-sections.

**Step 2 — Calculate total build time.**

$$
T_{\text{build}} = N_{\text{layers}} \times t_{\text{layer}} = 893 \times 6 \, \text{s} = 5358 \, \text{s}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to hours for practical use.**

$$
T_{\text{build}} = \frac{5358}{3600} \approx 1.49 \, \text{hours}
$$

*Text row:* 3600 seconds = 1 hour, so a 100×50×25 mm block takes about 1.5 hours — illustrating why MJ is favored for high-fidelity prototypes.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — X-Y Resolution from Nozzle Diameter

**Given:** A nozzle of diameter $d_{\text{nozzle}} = 20 \, \mu\text{m}$.

**Step 1 — Apply the resolution rule.**

$$
R_{\text{xy}} = 2 \cdot d_{\text{nozzle}}
$$

*Text row:* The minimum line spacing in the X-Y plane is approximately twice the nozzle diameter, because adjacent drops must overlap to fuse into a continuous line.

**Step 2 — Evaluate.**

$$
R_{\text{xy}} = 2 \times 20 \, \mu\text{m} = 40 \, \mu\text{m}
$$

*Text row:* This is the theoretical best X-Y resolution; in practice it is 100–200 µm due to drop-spreading on the substrate.

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Droplet Stability (Weber Number Check)

**Given:** A photopolymer droplet of density $\rho = 1100 \, \text{kg/m}^{3}$, ejection velocity $v = 5 \, \text{m/s}$, characteristic length $L = d = 30 \, \mu\text{m} = 3 \times 10^{-5} \, \text{m}$, surface tension $\gamma = 0.035 \, \text{N/m}$.

**Step 1 — Apply the Weber number definition.**

$$
\text{We} = \frac{\rho \, v^{2} \, L}{\gamma}
$$

*Text row:* Weber number = ratio of inertial force to surface tension force; values < critical (~10–12) ensure a stable droplet.

**Step 2 — Substitute numerical values.**

$$
\text{We} = \frac{1100 \times 5^{2} \times 3 \times 10^{-5}}{0.035}
$$

**Step 3 — Numerator evaluation.**

$$
1100 \times 25 \times 3 \times 10^{-5} = 1100 \times 7.5 \times 10^{-4} = 0.825
$$

**Step 4 — Final division.**

$$
\text{We} = \frac{0.825}{0.035} \approx 23.6
$$

*Text row:* A We ≈ 23.6 is **above** the critical range, suggesting secondary droplet breakup — the actual jetting velocity must therefore be reduced to ~3 m/s for stable drop formation. This is exactly why DOD systems operate at low ejection velocities.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Droplet Ejection Logic

| Criterion | **Thermal DOD** | **Piezoelectric DOD** |
|---|---|---|
| Actuator | Micro-resistor (~300 °C, 1–2 µs pulse) | Piezo crystal (room-temp deformation) |
| Ejection Mechanism | Vapor bubble expansion | Mechanical displacement of chamber wall |
| Compatible Materials | Aqueous inks, low-viscosity fluids | Wide range, including viscous photopolymers |
| Risk | Local overheating of sensitive resins (e.g., bio-inks) | Negligible thermal load |
| Frequency | Up to 10 kHz | Up to 30 kHz |
| Cost | Lower | Higher |
| Industrial AM Use | Limited | **Standard (Stratasys, 3D Systems)** |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Process Architecture — Material Jetting Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[CAD Model] --> B[Slicer Software]
    B --> C[Generate 2D Layer Data]
    C --> D[Initialize Build Platform at Top of Vat]
    D --> E[Multi-Nozzle Print Head Jets Photopolymer Droplets]
    E --> F[UV Lamp Cures Deposited Droplets in Situ]
    F --> G[Build Platform Lowers by One Layer Thickness h]
    G --> H{All Layers Printed?}
    H -- No --> E
    H -- Yes --> I[Remove Part from Build Platform]
    I --> J[Post-Cure in UV Oven]
    J --> K[Dissolve or Melt Support Material]
    K --> L[Finished 3D Part]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Material Jetting System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CTRL[Control Subsystem]
        C1[CAD / Slicer PC]
        C2[Motion Controller]
    end

    subgraph MECH[Mechanical Subsystem]
        M1[X-Y Gantry with Print Head]
        M2[Z-Axis Build Platform]
        M3[Resin Vat with Recoater Roller]
    end

    subgraph JET[Jetting Subsystem]
        J1[Multi-Nozzle DOD Print Head]
        J2[Ink Reservoir with Photopolymer]
        J3[Piezoelectric Actuators]
    end

    subgraph CUR[Curing Subsystem]
        U1[UV-A Lamp 365-405 nm]
        U2[Post-Cure UV Oven]
    end

    C1 --> C2
    C2 --> M1
    C2 --> M2
    M1 --> J1
    J2 --> J1
    J3 --> J1
    J1 --> U1
    M2 --> M3
    I[Finished Part] --> U2
```

### 4.3 Comparison Flow — CIJ vs DOD

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Material Jetting] --> B{Droplet Generation Mode}
    B -- Continuous Stream --> C[CIJ]
    B -- Only When Needed --> D[DOD]
    C --> E[Charging Electrode]
    E --> F[Deflection Plates]
    F --> G{Required for Build?}
    G -- Yes --> H[Substrate]
    G -- No --> I[Recycle to Reservoir]
    D --> P[Piezo or Thermal Pulse]
    P --> H
    H --> Q[UV Curing]
    Q --> R[Next Layer]
```

### 4.4 Layer-by-Layer Build Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Layer N Print] --> A2[Layer N Cure]
    A2 --> A3[Platform Down by h]
    A3 --> A4[Recoat Fresh Resin]
    A4 --> B1[Layer N+1 Print]
    B1 --> B2[Layer N+1 Cure]
    B2 --> B3[Platform Down by h]
    B3 --> A4
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define Material Jetting. With a neat sketch, explain the Drop-on-Demand (DOD) method of material jetting.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):** Material Jetting is a vat-photopolymerization-class additive manufacturing process in which **picolitre-sized liquid droplets** of photopolymer (or wax) are selectively jetted from a multi-nozzle print head onto a build platform, where they are **instantly cured by UV light** to build a part layer by layer.

**DOD Method Explanation (2 Marks):** In Drop-on-Demand, droplets are produced *only* when a voltage pulse is applied to a **piezoelectric actuator** (or a thermal heater). The actuator deforms the ink chamber wall, generating a pressure pulse that ejects a single droplet from the nozzle. The droplet travels a short distance (~1 mm), lands on the build platform, and is immediately cured by a co-located UV lamp. No droplet recycling is required, making DOD highly material-efficient and accurate.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**List any six advantages and three limitations of Material Jetting.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Advantages (½ mark each — list six):**
1. **Excellent surface finish** — up to ±0.1 mm accuracy, near-injection-moulded quality.
2. **Multi-material capability** — multiple print heads can jet different resins in a single build.
3. **High build speed** for thin-walled, high-detail parts.
4. **Wide range of mechanical properties** — from rubber-like to rigid in one part (e.g., PolyJet digital materials).
5. **No thermal distortion** — UV curing is a cold process.
6. **Excellent for visual prototypes and investment casting patterns.**

**Limitations (½ mark each — list three):**
1. **Limited material range** — predominantly photopolymers; metals and engineering ceramics not directly printable.
2. **High equipment and material cost.**
3. **Photopolymers degrade under prolonged UV/sunlight exposure** (poor long-term UV stability).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Style]**

**(a)** Explain the working principle of Material Jetting with a neat block diagram. List the major process parameters and explain how each affects the part quality. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the differences between **Continuous Inkjet (CIJ)** and **Drop-on-Demand (DOD)** modes of material jetting. Compare their suitability for Additive Manufacturing. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q-A(a)

**Working Principle (4 Marks):**
1. The CAD model is sliced into 2D layers of thickness *h* (typically 14–28 µm).
2. Liquid photopolymer is loaded into reservoirs feeding the print head.
3. The **multi-nozzle DOD print head** traverses the build platform, ejecting droplets at locations defined by the slice data.
4. A **UV-A lamp** (365–405 nm) co-mounted on the gantry cures each drop instantly upon deposition.
5. The build platform lowers by *h*; a **recoater** spreads a fresh resin film.
6. Layers stack until the full part height is reached; **post-curing** in a UV oven completes polymerization.

**Block Diagram (1 Mark):** Refer to SECTION 4.2 above.

**Process Parameters (2 Marks):**

| Parameter | Effect on Part Quality |
|---|---|
| Layer thickness *h* | Lower *h* → better surface finish, longer build time |
| Droplet volume $V_{\text{drop}}$ | Smaller volume → higher X-Y resolution |
| UV intensity & exposure time | Insufficient → under-cure; excessive → brittleness |
| Resin viscosity | High viscosity → poor jetting, missed drops |
| Build platform temperature | Affects resin flow and cure kinetics |
| Nozzle health | Clogged nozzles → voids, surface defects |

#### Model Solution to Q-A(b)

**Differences (5 Marks):**

| Aspect | **CIJ** | **DOD** |
|---|---|---|
| Droplet generation | Continuous pressurized stream | Pulsed, on demand |
| Deflection | Electrostatic plates steer charged drops | No deflection needed |
| Material waste | High (deflected drops recycled) | Near zero |
| Placement accuracy | Lower (drift, charge interaction) | High |
| Frequency | Very high | Moderate–high |
| Complexity | Complex (charging, deflection, gutter) | Simple (just pulse actuator) |

**Suitability for AM (2 Marks):**
- **DOD is preferred for AM** because accuracy is critical and material cost is high (waste is unacceptable).
- CIJ is generally unsuitable for AM due to its lower placement accuracy and need for conductive inks, but it dominates **industrial marking and coding**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2023 Style]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the volume of a single droplet ejected from a nozzle of diameter $d$ in Material Jetting. A print head ejects droplets of diameter 30 µm. Calculate the droplet volume in picolitres. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With suitable diagrams, explain the **layer-by-layer build sequence** in Material Jetting. List any four applications of Material Jetting in industry. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q-B(a)

**Derivation (4 Marks):**

A liquid droplet ejected from a nozzle assumes a near-spherical shape under surface tension (Rayleigh–Plateau stability).

$$
V_{\text{drop}} = \frac{\pi \, d^{3}}{6}
$$

*Justification:* Volume of a sphere = (4/3)πr³. With diameter $d = 2r$, this becomes (4/3)π(d/2)³ = πd³/6. The factor 1/6 is the cube of 1/2, and this is the standard result for any droplet in jetting analysis.

**Numerical Calculation (3 Marks):**

Given $d = 30 \, \mu\text{m} = 3.0 \times 10^{-5} \, \text{m}$:

$$
V_{\text{drop}} = \frac{\pi \, (3.0 \times 10^{-5})^{3}}{6}
$$

**Step 1:** Cube the diameter.

$$
(3.0 \times 10^{-5})^{3} = 27 \times 10^{-15} = 2.7 \times 10^{-14} \, \text{m}^{3}
$$

**Step 2:** Multiply by π.

$$
\pi \times 2.7 \times 10^{-14} = 8.482 \times 10^{-14} \, \text{m}^{3}
$$

**Step 3:** Divide by 6.

$$
V_{\text{drop}} = \frac{8.482 \times 10^{-14}}{6} = 1.414 \times 10^{-14} \, \text{m}^{3}
$$

**Step 4:** Convert to picolitres.

$$
V_{\text{drop}} = 1.414 \times 10^{-14} \times 10^{12} \, \text{pL} = 14.14 \, \text{pL}
$$

**Result:** The droplet volume is **14.14 pL** — within the typical industrial range of 1–100 pL. ✅

#### Model Solution to Q-B(b)

**Layer-by-Layer Build Sequence (4 Marks):** Refer to SECTION 4.4 diagram. Briefly:
1. Platform at top; print head jets droplets along slice path of Layer *n*.
2. UV lamp cures Layer *n*.
3. Platform descends by *h* (e.g., 28 µm).
4. Recoater spreads fresh resin.
5. Repeat for Layer *n+1*.
6. Continue until full height; post-cure; support removal.

**Applications (3 Marks — ¾ mark each):**
1. **Investment casting wax patterns** for aerospace turbine blades.
2. **Multi-material prototypes** (e.g., PolyJet overmoulded parts) for design validation.
3. **Dental models and clear aligner moulds.**
4. **Medical anatomical models** mimicking bone + soft tissue.
5. **Optical clear parts** for light-pipe prototypes.
6. **Bioprinting scaffolds** (emerging research).

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Material Jetting Answers:**
> 1. **Confusing Material Jetting with Binder Jetting** — Examiners frequently award **0 marks** if you mix the two. Remember: in **MJ, the jetted material is the part itself**; in **Binder Jetting, the jetted material is just an adhesive** for a powder bed.
> 2. **Writing "inkjet printer" without specifying DOD or CIJ** — Always clarify the mode; MJ specifically uses **DOD**, not CIJ.
> 3. **Forgetting the UV lamp** — The simultaneous in-situ UV curing is a **defining feature**; missing it costs 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Unit errors in drop volume** — Drop volume is in **picolitres (pL)**, not millilitres. Use $1 \, \text{m}^{3} = 10^{12} \, \text{pL}$.
> 5. **Stating "high temperature" curing** — MJ is a **cold process**; UV photopolymerization occurs at room temperature.
> 6. **In numerical questions, forgetting the 1.5× droplet-to-nozzle ratio** — A student who assumes $d = d_{\text{nozzle}}$ loses 1 mark on the calculated volume.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> 📌 **High-Density Revision Checklist — Material Jetting**

- [x] MJ is a **droplet-based, photopolymer AM** process — UV-cures liquid resin layer by layer.
- [x] Two modes: **CIJ** (continuous, recycled) and **DOD** (on-demand, no recycling). **AM uses DOD.**
- [x] DOD actuation: **Piezoelectric** (industrial standard) and **Thermal** (lower cost, limited use).
- [x] Nozzle diameter: **10–50 µm**; droplet diameter ≈ **1.5 × nozzle diameter**.
- [x] Droplet volume formula: $V_{\text{drop}} = \dfrac{\pi \, d^{3}}{6}$, typically **1–100 pL**.
- [x] X-Y resolution: $R_{\text{xy}} = 2 \cdot d_{\text{nozzle}}$.
- [x] Build time: $T_{\text{build}} = \dfrac{H}{h} \cdot t_{\text{layer}}$.
- [x] UV curing wavelength: **365–405 nm (UV-A)**.
- [x] Material viscosity must be **< 100 mPa·s** for stable jetting.
- [x] **Advantages:** excellent surface finish, multi-material, no thermal distortion, fast for small parts.
- [x] **Disadvantages:** limited to photopolymers/wax, high cost, UV-sensitive materials.
- [x] **Major applications:** investment casting patterns, dental, medical models, multi-material prototypes.
- [x] **Industrial systems:** Stratasys **PolyJet**, 3D Systems **MultiJet Printing (MJP)**.
- [x] **Key distinction from Binder Jetting:** In MJ, jetted droplets *are* the part; in BJ, jetted droplets are just *glue* for a powder bed.
- [x] **Distinction from SLA/DLP:** MJ jets discrete drops, SLA uses a scanned laser, DLP uses a projected mask.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fused Deposition Modelling (FDM)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMET524 - ADDITIVE MANUFACTURING > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Fused Deposition Modelling (FDM) # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Fused Deposition Modelling (FDM)** is a material extrusion–based Additive Manufacturing (AM) process (classified under **ASTM/ISO 52900** as the *Material Extrusion* process category) in which a continuous filament of a thermoplastic polymer is fed into a heated liquefier (extruder), melted to a semi-liquid state, and then selectively extruded through a computer-controlled nozzle onto a build platform where it solidifies to form a solid layer. Successive layers are deposited on top of one another, with each new layer fusing to the previous one as it cools, until the complete three-dimensional part is built. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Note (PEMET524 - Module 2):** FDM is treated as a flagship *Material Extrusion* process. The official synonym is **Fused Filament Fabrication (FFF)** — the term adopted by the open-source community after the expiration of the original Stratasys patent. Both terms are interchangeable in KTU answer scripts. The process was **invented and patented by Scott Crump in 1988** and commercialized by **Stratasys Inc.**, USA. It remains the most widely deployed AM technology worldwide, accounting for the largest installed base of low-cost desktop 3D printers. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview > [!NOTE] > **Plain-English Analogy — "The Automated Hot Glue Gun"** > Imagine holding a hot glue gun. You squeeze the trigger, melted glue flows out, and as it touches a surface it instantly hardens into a bead. Now imagine that the glue gun is mounted on a robot arm, controlled by a computer, and instead of tracing random shapes, it follows a precise cross-section (a "slice") of a 3D object. Once one layer is finished, the platform moves down a tiny distance (equal to one **layer thickness**), and a fresh layer is deposited on top. After several hundred or thousand such layers, a complete 3D part emerges from the machine. > The "glue" here is a thermoplastic filament (like **ABS, PLA, PETG, Nylon**, etc.), and the computer controller is the **G-code** generated by a **slicer software**. ## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics The following are the standard default metrics used in FDM, as typically reported in academic literature and by machine manufacturers (Stratasys, Ultimaker, Prusa, Creality): | Parameter | Typical Range / Value | |---|---| | Nozzle (Liquefier) Temperature | **190 °C – 320 °C** | | Build Platform Temperature | **25 °C – 120 °C** | | Layer Thickness (Z-resolution) | **0.05 mm – 0.40 mm** | | Standard Nozzle Diameter | **0.2 mm, 0.4 mm, 0.6 mm, 0.8 mm** | | Standard Filament Diameter | **1.75 mm** or **2.85 mm** | | Typical Build Volume | **150 × 150 × 150 mm³** to **600 × 600 × 600 mm³** (industrial) | | Volumetric Throughput | **~1 – 25 mm³/s** | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Fact:** The most common layer thickness specified in board exam numerical problems is **0.254 mm (0.010 inch)** — the historical Stratasys default. Memorize this value. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The FDM Process — Step-by-Step Operating Principle The FDM process is governed by a sequence of tightly coupled sub-systems. Each step is enumerated below in the *exact* logical order required for KTU descriptive answers. ### Step 1: Digital Model Preparation - The part is designed in **CAD software** (e.g., SolidWorks, Fusion 360, CATIA). - The CAD file is exported as an **STL (Standard Tessellation Language)** mesh. - The STL file is imported into a **slicer** (e.g., Cura, PrusaSlicer, Simplify3D). - The slicer slices the model into horizontal cross-sections of thickness equal to the desired **layer thickness (t)**. - For each slice, the slicer generates **G-code** — the toolpath instructions for the extruder. ### Step 2: Material Feeding - A spool of **thermoplastic filament** (typically **1.75 mm** or **2.85 mm** diameter) is fed into the machine. - The filament is gripped by a **drive mechanism** — usually a pair of **hobbed/knurled drive wheels** coupled to a **stepper motor** (the "feeder" or "extruder motor"). - The feed rate is termed the **F-rate in G-code (mm/min of filament)**. ### Step 3: Melting (Liquefaction) - The filament enters the **liquefier** — a heated metal block containing a **PTFE or metal-lined channel** with a heating element (cartridge heater + thermistor feedback loop, controlled by **PID control**). - Temperature is maintained just above the polymer's **glass transition temperature (Tg)** but typically close to or above the **melting temperature (Tm)** for semi-crystalline polymers. - Common nozzle temperatures: PLA ≈ **200 °C**, ABS ≈ **240 °C**, PETG ≈ **235 °C**, Nylon ≈ **260 °C**, PC ≈ **280 °C**. ### Step 4: Extrusion and Deposition - The molten polymer is forced through a **brass nozzle** of diameter **d_n** (typically 0.4 mm). - The nozzle traces the **2D toolpath** of the current layer while extruding material. - The extruded bead is called a **"road"** or **"raster"**, with characteristic dimensions: - **Road width (w)** ≈ nozzle diameter × **squash factor** (typically w ≈ 1.1 × d_n for a 0.4 mm nozzle → w ≈ 0.45 mm) - **Road height (h)** = layer thickness (t), because the layer is squashed to the platform Z-step. ### Step 5: Solidification and Bonding - The deposited road **cools and solidifies** almost instantly (natural convection / sometimes assisted by a **part cooling fan**). - The freshly deposited road fuses to the **underlying layer** through a combination of: - **Thermal bonding** (the new road re-melts the top skin of the previous layer). - **Mechanical interlocking** (roughness of the previous layer). - **Molecular diffusion** across the interface. ### Step 6: Z-Increment and Repetition - The **build platform (or nozzle) moves down by exactly one layer thickness (t)**. - Steps 4–6 are repeated for the next slice. ### Step 7: Post-Processing - After completion, the part is removed from the platform. - **Support structures** (deliberately deposited sacrificial geometry) are manually or chemically removed. - Optional finishing: **sanding, acetone smoothing (for ABS), painting, vapor polishing**. ## 2.2 The 'Why' and 'How' Behind the Process | Sub-System | Why it Exists | How it Works | |---|---|---| | **Drive wheels (extruder)** | To push solid filament reliably without slip | Stepper motor + hobbed gear + idler bearing; pressure pushes filament into liquefier | | **Liquefier** | To provide a controlled melt volume with steady viscosity | Heated aluminum block with thermistor; PID loop holds T within ±2 °C | | **Nozzle** | To shape the extrudate bead | A precision orifice of diameter d_n; orifice acts as a pressure-drop restriction | | **Build platform (heated bed)** | To prevent warping and improve first-layer adhesion | Heated aluminum plate; **PEI, BuildTak, or glue stick** surface treatments common | | **Gantry / Motion system** | To provide X-Y positioning | Stepper motors + belts (CoreXY / Cartesian) or lead screws; positional accuracy ~12.5 µm | | **Slicer / G-code generator** | To convert 3D model to machine instructions | Slicing algorithm + path planning (raster, contour, infill pattern) | ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet The following equations are **board-exam favourites** for FDM. Each is mathematically verifiable from first principles of extrusion and material continuity. > [!NOTE] > **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** Vertical bars and absolute-value symbols inside table cells have been replaced with `\vert` or `\mid` to prevent markdown table-parser breakage. | # | Formula Name | Equation | Variables & Units | Engineering Use | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Material Continuity (Volumetric Flow)** | $Q = A_{road} \cdot v_{print}$ | $Q$ = volumetric flow (mm³/s); $A_{road}$ = road cross-section (mm²); $v_{print}$ = print head velocity (mm/s) | Governs how much polymer the extruder must melt per second | | 2 | **Road Cross-Sectional Area** | $A_{road} = w \cdot t$ | $w$ = road width (mm); $t$ = layer thickness (mm) | Used to compute extrusion rate | | 3 | **Volumetric Throughput in Filament Terms** | $Q = \frac{\pi \, d_f^2}{4} \cdot v_{filament}$ | $d_f$ = filament diameter (mm); $v_{filament}$ = filament feed speed (mm/s) | Relates machine F-rate to actual polymer volume deposited | | 4 | **Layer Build Time (per layer)** | $T_{layer} = \frac{L_{path}}{v_{print}} \cdot \frac{1}{n_{nozzles}}$ | $L_{path}$ = total extrusion path length per layer (mm); $n_{nozzles}$ = number of nozzles | Time estimation for build planning | | 5 | **Total Build Time** | $T_{total} \approx N_{layers} \cdot T_{layer}$ | $N_{layers} = \frac{H}{t}$ ; $H$ = part height (mm) | Rough estimate (ignores acceleration, travel moves) | | 6 | **Squash Factor** | $SF = \frac{w_{deposited}}{d_n}$ | $w_{deposited}$ = actual road width; $d_n$ = nozzle diameter (mm) | Indicates degree of bead compression on the previous layer | | 7 | **Air Gap (controlling porosity)** | $g = w - p$ | $p$ = pitch (centre-to-centre distance between adjacent roads, mm); $w$ = road width (mm) | Positive g ⇒ porous part; Negative g ⇒ over-extrusion | | 8 | **Part Density (with air gaps)** | $\rho_{part} = \rho_{bulk} \cdot \left(1 - \frac{\vert g \vert}{w}\right)$ for $g \geq 0$ | $\rho_{bulk}$ = solid polymer density (g/cm³) | Predicts mass and mechanical strength of FDM part | | 9 | **Volumetric Flow Constraint** | $Q_{max} = \frac{\pi \, d_n^2}{4} \cdot v_{max}$ | $v_{max}$ = max practical extrusion velocity (mm/s) | Upper limit for a given nozzle size | | 10 | **Stanton-like Thermal Number (peeling adhesion)** | $St = \frac{h_{conv} \cdot L_c}{k_{polymer}}$ | $h_{conv}$ = convective heat transfer coefficient (W/m²K); $L_c$ = road width (m); $k$ = thermal conductivity (W/mK) | Used in warpage analysis | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Tip:** Equation (3) — the **material continuity equation linking filament feed speed to road dimensions** — is by far the most-tested formula. Be able to derive it from $Q_{in} = Q_{out}$ on your answer sheet. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility FDM is the **dominant technology in four high-impact engineering domains**: 1. **Rapid Prototyping (RP):** Concept models, ergonomic mockups, design verification — lead time reduced from weeks (injection moulding) to **hours**. 2. **Functional Prototyping:** End-use parts in Nylon (PA12), Polycarbonate (PC), and engineering composites (e.g., **carbon-fibre reinforced Nylon**). 3. **Low-Volume Manufacturing:** Custom jigs, fixtures, and tooling (e.g., aerospace bracket jigs at Boeing, Airbus). 4. **Biomedical:** Anatomical models for pre-surgical planning (CT-derived heart models), patient-specific implants, **3D-printed scaffolds for tissue engineering**, and orthotics (insoles, prosthetics sockets). The process is **commercially crucial** because of its low capital cost (entry-level printers from **₹15,000** / $200), minimal material wastage (recyclable spools), and broad material portfolio. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix This section provides **rigorous, step-by-step derivations** of the two most important FDM equations tested in KTU board examinations. --- ## 3.1 DERIVATION 1: The Volumetric Flow Equation — Linking Filament Feed Speed to Road Dimensions ### Problem Statement Derive the relationship between the **filament feed speed** $v_f$, the **filament diameter** $d_f$, the **road width** $w$, the **layer thickness** $t$, and the **print head velocity** $v_p$ in a single-nozzle FDM system. ### Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1 — Define the volumetric flow entering the system.** The filament is a cylinder of diameter $d_f$ moving at a linear feed velocity $v_f$. The volumetric flow rate $Q$ (in mm³/s) entering the liquefier is: $$ Q = \text{(Cross-sectional area of filament)} \times \text{(filament feed velocity)} $$ $$ Q = \frac{\pi \, d_f^{\,2}}{4} \cdot v_f $$ *Reasoning:* The cross-section of a cylinder is a circle, area = $\pi r^2 = \frac{\pi d^2}{4}$. The flow rate is volume per unit time, so we multiply cross-section by linear velocity. **Step 2 — Apply conservation of mass (incompressible polymer melt).** Thermoplastic melts in FDM are incompressible to a very good approximation. The polymer does not change density appreciably from the solid filament to the molten bead (small thermal expansion). Therefore, by the **continuity equation** for steady flow: $$ Q_{in} = Q_{out} $$ $$ Q_{filament} = Q_{road} $$ **Step 3 — Express the volumetric flow leaving the nozzle.** The road deposited on the build platform is a flattened rectangular bead. Its cross-section is approximately a rectangle of width $w$ and height $t$ (the layer thickness). The print head moves at velocity $v_p$, so: $$ Q_{out} = A_{road} \cdot v_p $$ $$ Q_{out} = (w \cdot t) \cdot v_p $$ *Reasoning:* Volume of a small deposited segment of length $dx$ = $A_{road} \cdot dx$. Dividing by time $dt = dx/v_p$ gives $Q_{out} = A_{road} \cdot v_p$. **Step 4 — Equate and solve.** Equating Step 1 and Step 3: $$ \frac{\pi \, d_f^{\,2}}{4} \cdot v_f = w \cdot t \cdot v_p $$ Rearranging for $v_f$: $$ v_f = \frac{4 \cdot w \cdot t \cdot v_p}{\pi \, d_f^{\,2}} $$ Or, expressed as a system of equivalent forms: $$ \boxed{\, v_f = \frac{4 \, w \, t \, v_p}{\pi \, d_f^{\,2}} \,} \quad \text{or} \quad \boxed{\, Q = \frac{\pi \, d_f^{\,2}}{4} \cdot v_f = w \cdot t \cdot v_p \,} $$ ### Numerical Illustration (KTU-style) **Given:** $d_f = 1.75$ mm, $w = 0.45$ mm, $t = 0.20$ mm, $v_p = 60$ mm/s. Find $v_f$. $$ v_f = \frac{4 \times 0.45 \times 0.20 \times 60}{\pi \times (1.75)^{2}} $$ $$ v_f = \frac{21.6}{\pi \times 3.0625} $$ $$ v_f = \frac{21.6}{9.6211} \approx 2.245 \text{ mm/s} $$ Convert to F-rate in mm/min: $v_f \times 60 \approx 134.7$ mm/min — a realistic feed rate for PLA on a 0.4 mm nozzle. --- ## 3.2 DERIVATION 2: Part Density with Positive Air Gap (Riddick-Bert model) ### Problem Statement An FDM part is built with adjacent parallel roads of width $w$, deposited with centre-to-centre pitch $p$. Derive an expression for the part's effective density $\rho_{part}$ in terms of the bulk polymer density $\rho_{bulk}$, given that $p > w$ (positive air gap $g$). ### Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1 — Define the air gap.** By definition: $$ g = w - p $$ If $p > w$ (roads are spaced apart with a gap), then $g < 0$ mathematically. However, in FDM literature, the **air gap** is conventionally defined as the *positive* void width: $$ g_{void} = p - w \quad \text{(for } p > w \text{)} $$ We will use the KTU-conventional notation $g \equiv (p - w) \geq 0$ (the void width). **Step 2 — Set up the 2D unit cell.** Consider a repeating unit cell of length $p$ along the road direction (perpendicular to road length). In cross-section, this cell contains **one full road of width $w$** and **one void of width $g$**. **Step 3 — Compute the fractional solid area.** The cross-sectional area per unit length of road direction: $$ A_{cell} = p \cdot 1 \quad \text{(per unit depth)} $$ $$ A_{solid} = w \cdot 1 $$ The fractional solid area (the **infill density** in 2D) is: $$ \phi = \frac{A_{solid}}{A_{cell}} = \frac{w}{p} $$ Using $p = w + g$: $$ \phi = \frac{w}{w + g} $$ **Step 4 — Relate fractional area to density.** For a homogeneous single-material part, mass per unit cell is proportional to solid area. Thus: $$ \rho_{part} = \rho_{bulk} \cdot \phi $$ $$ \boxed{\, \rho_{part} = \rho_{bulk} \cdot \frac{w}{w + g} \,} $$ or equivalently: $$ \rho_{part} = \rho_{bulk} \cdot \left(1 - \frac{g}{w + g}\right) \approx \rho_{bulk} \cdot \left(1 - \frac{g}{w}\right) \quad \text{for } g \ll w $$ ### Numerical Illustration (KTU-style) **Given:** ABS bulk density $\rho_{bulk} = 1.04$ g/cm³, road width $w = 0.5$ mm, air gap $g = 0.1$ mm. $$ \rho_{part} = 1.04 \times \frac{0.5}{0.5 + 0.1} = 1.04 \times 0.8333 \approx 0.867 \text{ g/cm}^3 $$ A density reduction of **~16.6%** — typical of FDM parts and a frequent KTU numerical answer. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Process Parameters on Part Quality | Process Parameter | Surface Roughness | Tensile Strength (X-axis) | Tensile Strength (Z-axis) | Build Time | Warpage | |---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:| | ↓ Layer thickness $t$ | **↓↓ Better** | ↑ Slightly higher | ↑ Slightly higher | **↑↑ Increases** | Slight ↓ | | ↑ Nozzle temperature | No direct effect | ↑ Marginal | ↑ Higher (better inter-layer bonding) | No effect | ↓ Less warpage | | ↑ Print speed $v_p$ | ↑ Rougher | ↓ Lower | ↓ Lower | **↓↓ Decreases** | ↑ More | | ↑ Air gap $g$ | ↑ Rougher | ↓↓ Lower | ↓ Lower | No effect | No effect | | ↑ Infill density | Marginal | **↑↑ Higher** | **↑↑ Higher** | ↑ Marginal | No effect | | Build orientation change | Affects | **Strongly affects** | **Strongly affects** | Varies | **Strongly affects** | > [!NOTE] > **Interpretation for KTU:** Anisotropy is the single most important disadvantage of FDM. The Z-axis tensile strength is typically **40–80%** lower than the X-Y axis strength because inter-layer bonding is weaker than intra-layer road bonding. This is **almost always asked** in board exams as a 3-mark conceptual question. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram of the FDM System Architecture The following Mermaid diagram shows the functional flow of the FDM system, mapping the **hardware sub-systems**, the **data flow**, and the **material flow** in a single integrated view. ```mermaid flowchart LR A[CAD Model
STEP/IGS] --> B[STL Export
Tessellated Mesh] B --> C[Slicer Software
Cura PrusaSlicer] C --> D[G-code File
Toolpath Instructions] D --> E[FDM Controller
Arduino / 32-bit Board] E -->|Step Pulses| F1[X Motor
Stepper] E -->|Step Pulses| F2[Y Motor
Stepper] E -->|Step Pulses| F3[Z Motor
Stepper] E -->|Step Pulses + Dir| F4[Extruder Motor
Feeder] E -->|PWM Heater| F5[Heater Cartridge
Liquefier Block] E -->|PWM Heater| F6[Heated Bed
Build Platform] M1[Filament Spool
1.75 mm ABS] --> F4 F4 --> F5 F5 --> N[Brass Nozzle
0.4 mm] N --> P[Build Platform
Z Stage] F3 --> P P --> S[Part being built
Layer N] S --> S2[Layer N+1 next] F1 --- G1[X Gantry] F2 --- G1 E -.PID Feedback.-> F5 E -.PID Feedback.-> F6 E -.Thermistor Data.-> F5 E -.Thermistor Data.-> F6 ``` ## 4.2 Process Flow Diagram — Sequence of Operations ```mermaid flowchart TD P1[Start: CAD Model Available] --> P2{STL File
Valid?} P2 -- No --> P3[Repair Mesh
Netfabb / Meshmixer] P3 --> P4[Slicing Engine] P2 -- Yes --> P4 P4 --> P5[Set Process Parameters
Layer Height, Temp, Speed] P5 --> P6[Generate G-code
Toolpath Planner] P6 --> P7[Preheat Nozzle and Bed] P7 --> P8[Deposit First Layer
Adhesion to Bed] P8 --> P9{Layer N Done?} P9 -- No --> P10[Extrude Roads
Infill + Walls] P10 --> P11[Cool and Solidify] P11 --> P12[Lower Z by 1 Layer Thickness t] P12 --> P9 P9 -- Yes --> P13[End: All Slices Done] P13 --> P14[Cool Part to Room Temperature] P14 --> P15[Remove Part from Platform] P15 --> P16[Remove Support Structures] P16 --> P17[Optional Post-Processing
Sanding Painting Annealing] P17 --> P18[Final FDM Part] ``` ## 4.3 Toolpath Pattern Diagram — Raster, Contour, and Infill ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph SL [Single Layer Cross Section] direction LR R1[Contour Pass 1
Outer Wall] --> R2[Contour Pass 2
Inner Wall] --> R3[Raster Infill
0/90 or 45/-45] R3 --> R4[Gaps Between Rasters
Air Gap g] end SL --> NXT[Next Layer: Rotate Raster by 45 deg] ``` ## 4.4 Material Categories Used in FDM ```mermaid flowchart TD MAT[FDM Thermoplastics] --> A1[Commodity Plastics] MAT --> A2[Engineering Plastics] MAT --> A3[High Performance Plastics] MAT --> A4[Flexible Elastomers] MAT --> A5[Composites] A1 --> A1a[PLA
Biodegradable Easy Print] A1 --> A1b[ABS
Tough Heat Resistant] A1 --> A1c[PETG
Chemical Resistant Clear] A2 --> A2a[Nylon PA6 PA12
Tough Abrasion Resistant] A2 --> A2b[PC Polycarbonate
High Impact Strength] A2 --> A2c[ASA
UV Resistant] A3 --> A3a[PEEK
Medical Aerospace] A3 --> A3b[PEI Ultem
High Temp Flame Retardant] A3 --> A3c[PPSU
Chemical Sterilizable] A4 --> A4a[TPU TPE
Flexible Rubber Like] A5 --> A5a[Carbon Fiber Reinforced
CF Nylon CF PLA] A5 --> A5b[Glass Fiber Reinforced] A5 --> A5c[Metal Filled Filament
Bronze Wood] ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner's Note:** When drawing any FDM block diagram, **always show the closed-loop PID feedback** between the controller, the thermistor, and the heater. This is a 2-mark "must-draw" element. A diagram without feedback is considered incomplete and loses marks. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap This section contains **model questions** matching the KTU 2024 Scheme End-Semester Examination (ESE) pattern. The pattern follows: - **Part A:** 3-mark short-answer questions - **Part B:** 14-mark questions with internal choice (a + b sub-parts of 7 marks each) --- ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper PEMET524]** **List any three thermoplastic materials commonly used in FDM. State one key property and one typical application of each.** **Model Answer:** | Material | Key Property | Typical Application | |---|---|---| | **ABS (Acrylonitrile Butadiene Styrene)** | High impact strength, good thermal stability up to ~100 °C | Functional prototypes, automotive interior parts, LEGO bricks | | **PLA (Polylactic Acid)** | Biodegradable, low warping, low printing temperature (~200 °C) | Educational models, concept prototypes, medical anatomical models | | **Nylon (PA12 / PA6)** | High toughness, abrasion resistance, flexibility | Functional end-use parts, gears, jigs, living hinges | --- ### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam - June 2024]** **Differentiate between FDM and FFF. Why are two different terms used for essentially the same process?** **Model Answer:** - **FDM (Fused Deposition Modelling)** is the **trademarked term** owned by **Stratasys Inc.**, the company that patented the technology in **1988** (inventor: Scott Crump). - **FFF (Fused Filament Fabrication)** is the **open-source, generic term** coined by the RepRap community around **2005–2010** to describe the same process after Stratasys's original patents began to expire. - **Technically identical** — both use a thermoplastic filament, a heated nozzle, layer-by-layer deposition, and the same physics. - **Legally distinct** — manufacturers of desktop printers (Ultimaker, Prusa, Creality) prefer to use FFF to avoid trademark infringement and to emphasize open-source compliance. --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]** **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the working principle of the Fused Deposition Modelling (FDM) process with a neat block diagram. List the major sub-systems and state the function of each. **(b) [7 Marks]** In an FDM system, a **1.75 mm** diameter PLA filament is fed at **3.0 mm/s** into a liquefier. The nozzle has a diameter of **0.4 mm** and the print head moves at **80 mm/s**. If the layer thickness is set to **0.20 mm**, determine: (i) The volumetric flow rate of the polymer melt. (ii) The effective road width deposited. (iii) The build time required to produce a single 200 mm × 200 mm square layer, assuming a single continuous perimeter pass and a print speed of 80 mm/s. --- #### MODEL SOLUTION TO QUESTION A **(a) [7 Marks] — Working Principle of FDM** The FDM process works on the principle of **selective material extrusion of a thermoplastic polymer** under computer control. The sequence is: 1. **CAD to STL:** A 3D model is created in CAD software and converted to STL format. 2. **Slicing:** The STL file is processed by a slicer, which divides the model into horizontal layers of thickness $t$. The slicer generates **G-code** containing toolpath instructions. 3. **Material feeding:** A thermoplastic filament from a spool is fed by **drive wheels (hobbed gear + idler bearing)** driven by a stepper motor into the **liquefier**. 4. **Melting:** The liquefier, a heated aluminum block maintained at a temperature above the polymer's melting point (e.g., 200 °C for PLA), melts the filament into a viscous fluid. 5. **Extrusion:** The molten polymer is forced through a **brass nozzle** (typical diameter 0.4 mm) onto the build platform. 6. **Deposition:** The nozzle traces the 2D cross-section of the current layer, depositing a bead called a **"road"** of width $w$ and height $t$ (the layer thickness). 7. **Solidification and bonding:** Each road cools rapidly, solidifies, and bonds thermally to the previously deposited layer. 8. **Z-increment:** The build platform (or nozzle) descends by one layer thickness $t$, and the next layer is deposited. 9. **Post-processing:** After all layers are built, the part is removed, and support structures (if any) are detached. **Major Sub-systems:** | Sub-system | Function | |---|---| | **Filament spool & feeder** | To supply the raw thermoplastic filament at a controlled rate | | **Liquefier (heater block)** | To melt the filament into a flowable polymer melt | | **Nozzle** | To shape the melt into a precise road of defined width | | **Build platform (heated bed)** | To support the part during building and reduce warping | | **X-Y gantry / motion system** | To position the nozzle in the horizontal plane | | **Z-axis drive** | To increment the layer height | | **Controller (microcontroller + firmware)** | To interpret G-code and drive all motors and heaters via PID loops | *(A labelled block diagram — as shown in Section 4.1 — must accompany this answer.)* --- **(b) [7 Marks] — Numerical Solution** **Given:** - Filament diameter: $d_f = 1.75$ mm - Filament feed speed: $v_f = 3.0$ mm/s - Nozzle diameter: $d_n = 0.4$ mm - Print head speed: $v_p = 80$ mm/s - Layer thickness: $t = 0.20$ mm **(i) Volumetric Flow Rate of the Polymer Melt** Using the material continuity equation: $$ Q = \frac{\pi \, d_f^{\,2}}{4} \cdot v_f $$ $$ Q = \frac{\pi \times (1.75)^{2}}{4} \times 3.0 $$ $$ Q = \frac{\pi \times 3.0625}{4} \times 3.0 $$ $$ Q = 2.4053 \times 3.0 = 7.216 \text{ mm}^3/\text{s} $$ **Answer: $Q \approx 7.22 \text{ mm}^3/\text{s}$** **(ii) Effective Road Width** Using $Q = w \cdot t \cdot v_p$: $$ w = \frac{Q}{t \cdot v_p} = \frac{7.216}{0.20 \times 80} = \frac{7.216}{16} \approx 0.4510 \text{ mm} $$ **Answer: $w \approx 0.45$ mm** *(Sanity check: This equals ~1.13 × nozzle diameter, consistent with the typical squash factor of 1.1.)* **(iii) Build Time for a Single 200 × 200 mm Square Layer (Perimeter Only)** Perimeter of the square layer = $4 \times 200 = 800$ mm. If we approximate a single continuous perimeter pass with uniform print speed $v_p = 80$ mm/s: $$ T_{layer} = \frac{L_{perimeter}}{v_p} = \frac{800}{80} = 10 \text{ s} $$ **Answer: $T_{layer} = 10 \text{ seconds}$** *(Note: This is a lower bound. The actual time will be higher due to infill passes, acceleration/deceleration at corners, and Z-hop travel moves.)* --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Module 2]** **(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **process parameters** of FDM in detail. Explain how each parameter affects the **surface finish, dimensional accuracy, and mechanical strength** of the printed part. **(b) [7 Marks]** With a neat sketch, describe the **support structures** used in FDM. When and why are they required? Differentiate between **break-away** and **soluble** supports with examples. --- #### MODEL SOLUTION TO QUESTION B **(a) [7 Marks] — Process Parameters of FDM** The key process parameters of FDM and their effects are summarized below in a KTU-table format: | Parameter | Description | Effect on Surface Finish | Effect on Dimensional Accuracy | Effect on Mechanical Strength | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Layer thickness ($t$)** | Height of each deposited slice | Smaller $t$ → **smoother** finish (less stair-stepping) | Smaller $t$ → better accuracy in Z | Marginal effect; smaller $t$ slightly improves Z-strength | | **Road width ($w$)** | Width of the extruded bead | Smaller $w$ → smoother | Better X-Y resolution | Tighter bonding between roads → higher X-Y strength | | **Air gap ($g$)** | Distance between adjacent roads (controlling porosity) | Positive $g$ → **rougher**, visible voids | Reduces accuracy of internal features | ↓ Tensile strength (less material per cross-section) | | **Nozzle temperature** | Liquefier set-point | Excessively high → oozing, stringing (rougher) | Affects dimensional stability | **Higher $T$** → better inter-layer adhesion → ↑ Z-strength | | **Build orientation** | Direction the part is built in FDM machine | Orientation of stair-stepping changes with angle | **Critical** — overhangs and tolerances depend on orientation | **Strongest** mechanical properties are in the X-Y plane (anisotropic) | | **Print speed ($v_p$)** | Nozzle traverse velocity | Too fast → vibration, ringing artefacts | Degrades accuracy | Faster printing → weaker inter-layer bonding | | **Infill density / pattern** | Percentage of internal material fill | Mostly internal, not visible | Affects internal geometry | ↑ Infill → ↑ strength (linearly up to ~60%) | | **Rasters angle** | Orientation of internal roads in successive layers | Minor | Minor | Alternating 0°/90° or ±45° gives more isotropic strength | **Conclusion:** The **two most dominant parameters** for the KTU 2024 scheme are **layer thickness** and **build orientation**. Together, they control roughly 70% of the part's final quality. --- **(b) [7 Marks] — Support Structures in FDM** **Definition:** Support structures are **sacrificial auxiliary geometries** printed alongside the actual part to provide a stable base for the deposition of overhanging or bridging features. **Why are they needed?** In FDM, each newly deposited road must rest on a solid substrate. If a feature has an overhang angle greater than **~45° from the vertical** (i.e., it juts out horizontally), there is nothing underneath it for the molten plastic to bond to. Without support, the road sags, droops, or collapses due to gravity. The figure below shows the overhang criterion: $$ \theta_{max} = 45° \text{ (typical safe overhang angle)} $$ Any surface inclined at an angle $\theta > 45°$ from the vertical (or $> 45°$ overhang) requires support. **Types of Support Structures:** | Type | Material | Removal Method | Example Use Case | |---|---|---|---| | **Break-away supports** | Same material as part (e.g., ABS) | Manually snapped off / pliers | Standard prototyping with ABS, PLA, Nylon | | **Soluble supports** | Different material (e.g., **PVA**, **HIPS**) | Dissolved in water (PVA) or **d-Limonene** (HIPS) | Complex internal channels, multi-material prints, delicate features | **Comparison:** - **Break-away supports** leave a small witness mark on the part surface and are faster to print. They are unsuitable for **enclosed internal cavities** because they cannot be reached for removal. - **Soluble supports** (e.g., **Stratasys' SR-30 / SR-35**, **Ultimaker PVA**) require a **dual-nozzle printer**. They are essential for **complex internal lattices, medical implants with internal channels, and parts with enclosed voids**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Practical Note for KTU:** When the question asks "Why are supports needed?", a high-scoring answer must mention **three reasons**: (1) to prevent overhang collapse, (2) to reduce **warpage** by anchoring the part to the bed, and (3) to provide **thermal stability** during cooling of tall features. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks** > 1. **Confusing "filament diameter" with "nozzle diameter" in numerical problems.** The filament (1.75 mm) is the *input*; the nozzle (0.4 mm) is the *output restriction*. Most mistakes arise from plugging $d_n$ into the continuity equation instead of $d_f$. > 2. **Forgetting the unit conversion from mm/s to mm³/s** when computing volumetric flow rate. > 3. **Not stating the assumption of incompressibility** when equating $Q_{in} = Q_{out}$. This single phrase can fetch 1 extra mark. > 4. **Confusing glass transition temperature ($T_g$) with melting temperature ($T_m$)** when describing the liquefier temperature. For amorphous polymers (ABS, PLA), the relevant temperature is above $T_g$; for semi-crystalline polymers (Nylon, PEEK), it is above $T_m$. > 5. **Omitting the PID feedback loop** in block diagrams.
