This costs **2 marks** in part-(a) 7-mark questions. > 6. **Not writing units in the final answer** of numerical problems — strict loss of 0.5 to 1 mark per KTU valuation norms. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (High-Density Revision Checklist) > [!NOTE] > **Use this section as your last-minute revision before entering the exam hall.** - ✅ **FDM** stands for **Fused Deposition Modelling** (Stratasys trademark); the open-source equivalent term is **Fused Filament Fabrication (FFF)**. - ✅ Inventor: **Scott Crump**, Year: **1988**, Company: **Stratasys Inc.** - ✅ ASTM/ISO process category: **Material Extrusion**. - ✅ **Five major sub-systems:** Feeder, Liquefier, Nozzle, Build platform (heated bed), Motion system (X-Y-Z gantry), plus the **Controller**. - ✅ Standard filament diameters: **1.75 mm** and **2.85 mm**. Standard nozzle diameters: **0.2, 0.4, 0.6, 0.8 mm**. - ✅ **Key equation (HIGH PRIORITY):** $Q = \frac{\pi d_f^2}{4} v_f = w \, t \, v_p$ - ✅ **Layer thickness range:** 0.05 mm – 0.40 mm. Stratasys default = **0.254 mm (0.010 in)**. - ✅ **Air gap equation:** $g = p - w$. Positive $g$ ⇒ **porous, weaker** part; $g = 0$ ⇒ solid; $g < 0$ ⇒ over-extrusion. - ✅ **Squash factor** (typical): **1.1** — i.e., road width is ~10% larger than nozzle diameter. - ✅ **Overhang limit without support:** **45° from vertical**. - ✅ **Support types:** Break-away (same material) vs. Soluble (PVA in water, HIPS in d-Limonene). - ✅ **Most common materials:** PLA, ABS, PETG (commodity); Nylon (PA6, PA12), PC, ASA (engineering); PEEK, PEI (Ultem) (high-performance); TPU (flexible); CF/GF-reinforced (composites). - ✅ **FDM is anisotropic** — X-Y strength is **40–80% higher** than Z strength. - ✅ **FDM is the lowest-cost AM process**, ideal for **prototyping, low-volume tooling, biomedical models, and jigs/fixtures**. - ✅ **Maximum safe overhang angle = 45°** (no support needed for angles less steep than this). - ✅ **Stair-stepping effect** is the dominant surface-finish limitation; reduced by smaller layer thickness and **post-processing** (sanding, acetone vapour smoothing for ABS). - ✅ **Build orientation** is the single most important factor for mechanical strength and accuracy. - ✅ **PID control** is essential for maintaining liquefier and bed temperature stability. - ✅ **PID feedback** must be shown in **every block diagram** in the exam. > **End of Module 2 — Fused Deposition Modelling (FDM) Notes.** > *Prepared in compliance with KTU 2024 Scheme PEMET524 assessment regulations.*

topics.title: Direct Ink Writing (DIW)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET524 - ADDITIVE MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Direct Ink Writing (DIW)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Direct Ink Writing (DIW)** — also referred to as **Robocasting**, **Direct Write**, or **Fluid-based Material Extrusion** — is a versatile, nozzle-based **Additive Manufacturing (AM)** process belonging to the **Material Extrusion** family (ISO/ASTM 52900:2021 standard). In DIW, a highly viscous, shear-thinning liquid-phase precursor — termed an **"ink"** — is pneumatically, mechanically, or piston-driven through a fine deposition nozzle (typically **diameter: 10 μm – 1 mm**) to fabricate three-dimensional (3D) parts in a layer-by-line and layer-by-layer manner on a computer-controlled XY stage (and Z-axis elevator).

Unlike Fused Deposition Modeling (FDM), DIW does **not** require thermal melting of thermoplastics. The ink is dispensed in a **paste, gel, or colloidal suspension** form, often using **rheology-engineered** formulations (e.g., colloidal gels, sol-gels, hydrogels, polymer melts, concentrated suspensions) that exhibit a **shear-yield stress (τ_y)** high enough to retain shape after extrusion but low enough to flow under nozzle shear.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PEMET524, Module 2):**
> DIW is studied as a **Liquid-Based Material Extrusion Process** under the broader taxonomy of AM processes. The syllabus specifically emphasizes its applicability for **structural ceramics, functionally graded materials (FGM), biomedical scaffolds, and electronic/energy devices**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The Cake Icing Decorator**
> Imagine a professional pastry chef using a piping bag filled with stiff royal icing to draw intricate 3D flowers on a cake. The icing is **too thick to pour** (so it doesn't drip or spread), yet **soft enough to squeeze** through a narrow metal tip under pressure. Once extruded, the icing **holds its shape immediately**, standing tall without collapsing. The chef then draws the next line *on top* of the previous one, building a 3D structure one line at a time. **Direct Ink Writing works exactly the same way** — except:
> - The "icing bag" is a **computer-controlled syringe pump** or **pneumatic extruder**.
> - The "metal tip" is a **precision micronozzle**.
> - The "stiff icing" is a **rheology-tuned colloidal ink** (ceramic, metal, polymer, or biological).
> - The "cake" is a **programmable X-Y-Z motion stage**.
> - After the icing dries, the chef may **bake it** in an oven — in DIW, the green part undergoes **drying, sintering, or curing** to become a dense, functional solid.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants, Material Metrics & Engineering Parameters

The following standard metrics govern the DIW process and **must be memorized** for KTU ESE questions:

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Range | Unit | Role in DIW |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Nozzle inner diameter** | $d_n$ | 10 – 1000 | $\mu m$ | Defines feature resolution & line width |
| **Layer height** | $h_l$ | 50 – 90% of $d_n$ | $\mu m$ | Vertical stacking precision |
| **Shear yield stress** | $\tau_y$ | 10 – 1000 | Pa | Shape retention post-extrusion |
| **Apparent viscosity** | $\eta_{app}$ | 1 – 1000 | Pa·s | Flow resistance through nozzle |
| **Print speed** | $v_p$ | 1 – 50 | mm/s | Traverse velocity of nozzle |
| **Extrusion pressure** | $P$ | 10 – 800 | kPa | Driving pressure (pneumatic) |
| **Solid loading** | $\phi$ | 20 – 60 | vol% | Volume fraction of solids in ink |
| **Volumetric flow rate** | $Q$ | 0.01 – 10 | $\mu L/s$ | Mass deposition rate |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Takeaway:** DIW is a **rheology-dominated** process. Without the correct viscoelastic response, the ink will either (a) **clog the nozzle** (too stiff), or (b) **slump and lose fidelity** (too fluid). The art of DIW lies in **ink formulation chemistry**.

---

## 1.4 Classification within the AM Process Map

According to **ISO/ASTM 52900:2021**, DIW falls under:

$$
\text{Additive Manufacturing} \rightarrow \text{Material Extrusion} \rightarrow \text{Liquid-Based Material Extrusion (DIW)}
$$

| Sub-category | Description |
|---|---|
| **Robocasting** | Original term coined at Sandia National Labs (Cesarano et al., 1990s) for colloidal ceramic inks |
| **3D Bioprinting** | DIW of cell-laden hydrogels (bio-inks) for tissue engineering |
| **Micro-DIW** | Sub-10 μm features via fine nozzles for electronics/optoelectronics |
| **DIW of Composites** | Multi-material inks (e.g., SiC + polymer binder) for graded structures |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principle: The Three-Stage Workflow

The DIW process is best understood as a **three-stage physical-chemical transformation chain**:

### Stage 1 — Ink Formulation (Rheology Engineering)
The ink must satisfy **two contradictory demands**:
- **Flow on demand**: low viscosity at high shear rates (inside the nozzle)
- **Shape retention at rest**: high yield stress (post-deposition)

This is achieved by engineering **shear-thinning (pseudoplastic)** behavior, often modeled by the **Herschel–Bulkley** equation.

### Stage 2 — Filament Deposition (Filament-Level Mechanics)
The ink is forced through a nozzle at a controlled flow rate, where the **velocity profile**, **pressure drop**, and **shear rate** govern filament geometry. After exiting the nozzle, the filament undergoes **die swell** and **post-extrusion relaxation**, then must **self-support** against gravity and the weight of subsequent layers.

### Stage 3 — Solidification / Post-Processing
The "green" (binder-laden) part is dried, crosslinked, **pyrolyzed**, and **sintered** to achieve a dense, functional component. Dimensional shrinkage of **15–25%** is typical during sintering.

---

## 2.2 Core Rheological Models (KTU High-Yield)

### 2.2.1 The Herschel–Bulkley Model (Generalized Non-Newtonian)

This is the **most important equation for DIW**, covering Bingham plastics, shear-thinning, and shear-thickening fluids:

$$
\tau = \tau_y + K \dot{\gamma}^n
$$

Where:
- $\tau$ = shear stress (Pa)
- $\tau_y$ = **yield stress** (Pa) — minimum stress to initiate flow
- $K$ = **consistency index** (Pa·sⁿ)
- $\dot{\gamma}$ = shear rate (s⁻¹)
- $n$ = **flow behavior index** (dimensionless)
  - $n < 1$ → shear-thinning (ideal for DIW)
  - $n = 1$ → Bingham plastic
  - $n > 1$ → shear-thickening (unsuitable)

### 2.2.2 Bingham Plastic Model (Special Case of Herschel–Bulkley)

For $n = 1$:

$$
\tau = \tau_y + \eta_p \dot{\gamma}
$$

Where $\eta_p$ is the **plastic viscosity** (Pa·s). This is the simplest and most frequently cited model in KTU questions.

### 2.2.3 Capillary Number (Dimensionless)

$$
Ca = \frac{\eta_{app} \cdot v_p}{d_n}
$$

Used to assess the **printability window** — too low $Ca$ causes **nozzle clogging**, too high $Ca$ causes **filament breakup / droplet formation** (Rayleigh–Plateau instability).

### 2.2.4 Hagen–Poiseuille Equation (Volumetric Flow Rate)

For a cylindrical nozzle of length $L$ and diameter $d_n$, the pressure-driven flow rate is:

$$
Q = \frac{\pi \cdot \Delta P \cdot d_n^4}{128 \cdot \eta \cdot L}
$$

Where:
- $Q$ = volumetric flow rate (m³/s)
- $\Delta P$ = pressure drop across nozzle (Pa)
- $\eta$ = dynamic viscosity (Pa·s)
- $L$ = nozzle length (m)
- $d_n$ = nozzle diameter (m)

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The **fourth-power dependence** on $d_n$ explains why nozzle diameter is the **most sensitive parameter** in DIW — halving $d_n$ reduces flow by 16×.

### 2.2.5 Wall Shear Rate in a Cylindrical Nozzle

$$
\dot{\gamma}_{wall} = \frac{32 \cdot Q}{\pi \cdot d_n^3}
$$

This must be in the **shear-thinning regime** (typically 1–1000 s⁻¹) for smooth extrusion.

### 2.2.6 Minimum Yield Stress for Shape Retention (Slumping Criterion)

To prevent a cylindrical filament of radius $r$ from slumping under its own weight, the yield stress must satisfy:

$$
\tau_y \geq \rho g r
$$

Where:
- $\rho$ = ink density (kg/m³)
- $g$ = 9.81 m/s² (gravitational acceleration)
- $r$ = filament radius (m)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For KTU Exam:** A typical ceramic ink with $\rho = 2500$ kg/m³ and $r = 250$ μm requires $\tau_y \geq 6.1$ Pa to avoid slumping. Real inks are designed with $\tau_y$ = 50–500 Pa for a safety margin.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet (Markdown Table)

| # | Formula | Symbol Key | Application | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\tau = \tau_y + K\dot{\gamma}^n$ | Herschel–Bulkley | General rheology | Pa, Pa·sⁿ, s⁻¹ |
| 2 | $\tau = \tau_y + \eta_p \dot{\gamma}$ | Bingham plastic | Yield-stress fluids | Pa, Pa·s |
| 3 | $Q = \frac{\pi \Delta P d_n^4}{128 \eta L}$ | Hagen–Poiseuille | Nozzle flow rate | m³/s |
| 4 | $\dot{\gamma}_{wall} = \frac{32Q}{\pi d_n^3}$ | Wall shear rate | Ink extrudability | s⁻¹ |
| 5 | $Ca = \frac{\eta v_p}{d_n}$ | Capillary number | Printability | dimensionless |
| 6 | $\tau_y \geq \rho g r$ | Slumping criterion | Shape retention | Pa, kg/m³, m |
| 7 | $v_p \cdot A_{filament} = Q$ | Continuity | Volumetric balance | m/s, m², m³/s |
| 8 | $\sigma_{green} = K \phi^m$ | Green strength | Mechanical stability | Pa, vol% |
| 9 | $\Delta L / L_0 = 1 - (\phi / \phi_{final})^{1/3}$ | Sintering shrinkage | Post-processing | dimensionless |
| 10 | $Bo^* = \frac{\rho g d_n^2}{\sigma}$ | Bond number | Slumping vs. capillarity | dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility of These Equations:**
> - **Process engineers** use Eq. 1–2 to **design ink recipes** (colloidal stability, binder selection).
> - **Control system designers** use Eq. 3–4 to **set pneumatic pressures** in real-time G-code.
> - **Sintering specialists** use Eq. 9 to **pre-compensate CAD models** for shrinkage.

---

## 2.4 Process Parameter Inter-relationships

The DIW process can be summarized by the following **input–output relationship**:

$$
\text{Print Quality} = f(\underbrace{\tau_y, \eta_p, n, \phi}_{\text{ink rheology}}, \underbrace{d_n, L, v_p, P, h_l}_{\text{process parameters}}, \underbrace{T_{env}, RH}_{\text{ambient}})
$$

A **"printability map"** is empirically constructed by plotting these parameters against each other to identify the **working envelope** where smooth filaments are deposited without clogging, slumping, or breakup.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Volumetric Flow Rate for a Bingham Plastic in a Cylindrical Nozzle

### Step 1 — Statement of the Problem

For a Bingham plastic fluid (with yield stress $\tau_y$ and plastic viscosity $\eta_p$) flowing through a cylindrical nozzle of radius $R$ and length $L$ under pressure drop $\Delta P$, derive the **volumetric flow rate $Q$**.

### Step 2 — Force Balance on a Fluid Cylinder of Radius $r$

Consider an imaginary cylinder of fluid of radius $r$ inside the nozzle. The net force due to pressure acting on the ends of the cylinder must equal the shear force on its lateral surface.

$$
\Delta P \cdot \pi r^2 = \tau(r) \cdot 2\pi r L
$$

### Step 3 — Solve for Local Shear Stress

$$
\tau(r) = \frac{\Delta P \cdot r}{2L}
$$

At the nozzle wall ($r = R$), the wall shear stress is:

$$
\tau_w = \frac{\Delta P \cdot R}{2L}
$$

> **Conversion logic:** This is simply the equilibrium of axial force and circumferential shear — a standard fluid mechanics identity for fully-developed laminar flow.

### Step 4 — Identify the Plug-Flow Core (Bingham-Specific)

In a Bingham plastic, flow occurs **only** where the local shear stress exceeds the yield stress. The radius of the unyielded "plug" core $r_p$ is found by setting $\tau(r_p) = \tau_y$:

$$
\tau_y = \frac{\Delta P \cdot r_p}{2L}
$$

$$
r_p = \frac{2 L \tau_y}{\Delta P}
$$

If $r_p < 0$, the entire fluid yields (no plug). If $r_p \geq R$, no flow occurs — this defines the **minimum pressure** for extrusion.

### Step 5 — Minimum Extrusion Pressure (Critical Condition)

The yield criterion $r_p = R$ gives:

$$
\Delta P_{min} = \frac{2 L \tau_y}{R}
$$

> **Conversion logic:** Below this pressure, the ink **will not flow** — the pneumatic controller must be set above this threshold to initiate extrusion.

### Step 6 — Constitutive Relation in the Yielded Region

For $r_p \leq r \leq R$, the fluid is yielded, so the Bingham model applies:

$$
\tau(r) = \tau_y + \eta_p \left(-\frac{dv}{dr}\right)
$$

### Step 7 — Velocity Profile Integration

Substituting $\tau(r) = \Delta P \cdot r / 2L$ and integrating with the no-slip condition $v(R) = 0$:

$$
-\frac{dv}{dr} = \frac{1}{\eta_p}\left(\frac{\Delta P \cdot r}{2L} - \tau_y\right)
$$

$$
v(r) = \frac{1}{\eta_p}\int_r^R \left(\frac{\Delta P \cdot r'}{2L} - \tau_y\right) dr'
$$

Carrying out the integration:

$$
v(r) = \frac{\Delta P}{4 \eta_p L}\left(R^2 - r^2\right) - \frac{\tau_y}{\eta_p}\left(R - r\right)
$$

### Step 8 — Add the Plug-Flow Velocity (Core Region)

In the unyielded core $0 \leq r \leq r_p$, the fluid moves as a rigid plug at the **centerline velocity** $v_p = v(r_p)$:

$$
v_p = \frac{\Delta P}{4 \eta_p L}\left(R^2 - r_p^2\right) - \frac{\tau_y}{\eta_p}\left(R - r_p\right)
$$

Substituting $r_p = 2L\tau_y/\Delta P$ and simplifying (algebra omitted for brevity but straightforward), the total volumetric flow rate is:

$$
Q = \pi \int_0^R v(r) \cdot 2r \, dr = \pi v_p r_p^2 + 2\pi \int_{r_p}^R v(r) \, r \, dr
$$

### Step 9 — Final Buckingham–Reiner Equation

After integration, the celebrated result is:

$$
Q = \frac{\pi R^4 \Delta P}{8 \eta_p L}\left[1 - \frac{4}{3}\left(\frac{\tau_y}{\tau_w}\right) + \frac{1}{3}\left(\frac{\tau_y}{\tau_w}\right)^4\right]
$$

> **Conversion logic:** This is the **Buckingham–Reiner equation** for Bingham plastic flow in a pipe. For $\tau_y \to 0$, it reduces to the classic **Hagen–Poiseuille law** (Eq. 3 from Section 2), confirming internal consistency.

---

## 3.2 Numerical Worked Example (Typical KTU ESE Pattern)

**Problem:** A ceramic ink with $\tau_y = 80$ Pa and $\eta_p = 5$ Pa·s is extruded through a nozzle of radius $R = 250$ μm and length $L = 30$ mm. Pneumatic pressure is set at $\Delta P = 400$ kPa. Calculate:
(a) Wall shear stress
(b) Plug radius
(c) Volumetric flow rate

**Solution:**

**(a) Wall shear stress:**

$$
\tau_w = \frac{\Delta P \cdot R}{2L} = \frac{400{,}000 \times 250 \times 10^{-6}}{2 \times 0.03} = \frac{100}{0.06} = 1666.67 \text{ Pa}
$$

**(b) Plug radius:**

$$
r_p = \frac{2 L \tau_y}{\Delta P} = \frac{2 \times 0.03 \times 80}{400{,}000} = 1.2 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m} = 12 \, \mu m
$$

**(c) Volumetric flow rate using Buckingham–Reiner:**

First compute the yield-stress ratio:

$$
\frac{\tau_y}{\tau_w} = \frac{80}{1666.67} = 0.048
$$

$$
Q = \frac{\pi (250\times10^{-6})^4 \times 400{,}000}{8 \times 5 \times 0.03}\left[1 - \frac{4}{3}(0.048) + \frac{1}{3}(0.048)^4\right]
$$

$$
Q = \frac{\pi \times 3.906 \times 10^{-15} \times 400{,}000}{1.2} \times \left[1 - 0.064 + \text{negligible}\right]
$$

$$
Q = \frac{4.91 \times 10^{-9}}{1.2} \times 0.936 \approx 3.83 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 3.83 \, \mu L/s
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result Interpretation:** A flow rate of 3.83 μL/s is in the typical DIW working range (0.01 – 10 μL/s from Section 1.3), confirming the chosen parameters yield a **printable** configuration.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Layer-Height Compensation for Sintering Shrinkage

If the final sintered part must have dimension $L_{final}$, and the volumetric solid loading increases from $\phi_{green}$ to $\phi_{final}$ during sintering, the **isotropic linear shrinkage** is:

$$
L_{green} = \frac{L_{final}}{1 - s}
$$

Where $s$ is the **linear shrinkage fraction**:

$$
s = 1 - \left(\frac{\phi_{green}}{\phi_{final}}\right)^{1/3}
$$

**Derivation walkthrough:**

Starting from volume conservation of the solid phase:

$$
V_{green} \cdot \phi_{green} = V_{final} \cdot \phi_{final}
$$

$$
\frac{V_{final}}{V_{green}} = \frac{\phi_{green}}{\phi_{final}}
$$

Taking the cube root (assuming isotropic shrinkage):

$$
\frac{L_{final}}{L_{green}} = \left(\frac{\phi_{green}}{\phi_{final}}\right)^{1/3}
$$

Rearranging:

$$
L_{green} = L_{final} \cdot \left(\frac{\phi_{final}}{\phi_{green}}\right)^{1/3} = \frac{L_{final}}{1 - s}
$$

**Numerical example:** A SiC ink with $\phi_{green} = 0.45$ sintered to $\phi_{final} = 0.95$ has:

$$
s = 1 - (0.45/0.95)^{1/3} = 1 - 0.781 = 0.219 = 21.9\%
$$

> **Conversion logic:** A target sintered dimension of **10 mm** must be printed at **12.8 mm** to achieve the correct final size.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 DIW System — Block-Level Functional Architecture

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **subsystem interactions** of a typical Direct Ink Writing workstation. This is preferred over a physical schematic because DIW systems involve simultaneous fluid-mechanical, mechatronic, and software domains that are best represented as a functional architecture.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[CAD Model STL] --> A2[Slicer Software Cura / Slic3r]
    A2 --> A3[G-Code Toolpath XY Trajectories]
    A3 --> A4[Motion Controller Arduino / ARM]
    A4 --> A5[Stepper Motor Drivers]
    A5 --> A6[XY Gantry and Z Elevator]
    A6 --> A7[Nozzle Deposition Tip 10 um to 1 mm]
    
    B1[Ink Reservoir Syringe Barrel] --> B2[Pressure Regulator Pneumatic Piston or Screw]
    B2 --> B3[Pressure Sensor Inline Feedback]
    B3 --> A7
    B3 --> A4
    
    A7 --> C1[Substrate Build Plate Glass or Sacrificial Layer]
    C1 --> C2[Green Part Layer 1 to Layer n]
    C2 --> C3[Post Processing Dry Cure Sinter]
    C3 --> C4[Final Functional Part]
    
    D1[Ambient Sensors Temperature and RH] --> A4
    D2[Rheometer Offline Ink QC] --> B1
    D3[Vision System Optional Layer Inspection] --> A4
    
    style A1 fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#0d47a1
    style A7 fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100
    style B1 fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#4a148c
    style C3 fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#1b5e20
    style C4 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
```

### 4.1.1 Subsystem Description Table

| Block | Subsystem | Function |
|---|---|---|
| A1–A3 | **Digital Toolchain** | STL → G-code generation |
| A4–A6 | **Motion Control** | XYZ motion via stepper/servo |
| A7 | **Deposition Nozzle** | Geometric definition of bead |
| B1–B3 | **Material Delivery** | Pressure-driven flow control |
| C1–C4 | **Substrate & Post-Processing** | Build platform → solid part |
| D1–D3 | **Monitoring / Quality** | Closed-loop control & inspection |

---

## 4.2 Process State Diagram — DIW Workflow

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> InkFormulation
    InkFormulation: Ink Formulation Ball Milling and Mixing
    InkFormulation --> RheologyQC
    RheologyQC: Rheology QC Yield Stress and Viscosity Check
    RheologyQC --> Slicing: Pass
    RheologyQC --> InkRejection: Fail
    InkRejection --> InkFormulation: Reformulate
    Slicing: Slicing and G-Code Generation
    Slicing --> Deposition
    Deposition: Layer by Layer Deposition
    Deposition --> Drying
    Drying: Drying or UV Cure
    Drying --> NextLayer: Stack Layer n plus 1
    NextLayer --> Deposition
    NextLayer --> Debinding
    Debinding: Debinding Binder Burnout
    Debinding --> Sintering
    Sintering: Sintering High Temperature Densification
    Sintering --> PostProcessing
    PostProcessing: Post Processing Machining and Inspection
    PostProcessing --> [*]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the State Diagram:** The process is **cyclic** between Deposition ↔ Drying ↔ Next Layer until the full part height is reached. Only then does the workflow transition to the **linear** thermal post-processing chain (Debinding → Sintering → Inspection).

---

## 4.3 Filament-Level Physics Cross-Section

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Pneumatic Pressure P] --> P2[Nozzle Inlet]
    P2 --> P3[Converging Flow Region]
    P3 --> P4[Yielded Shear Zone near Wall]
    P4 --> P5[Plug Flow Core Unyielded]
    P5 --> P6[Die Exit Filament Formation]
    P6 --> P7[Post Extrusion Relaxation]
    P7 --> P8[Substrate Adhesion and Shape Retention]
    
    style P4 fill:#ffe0b2
    style P5 fill:#ffccbc
    style P8 fill:#c8e6c9
```

> **Legend:**
> - **Orange (P4)**: Shear-thinned annular region near the wall — high shear rate.
> - **Red-orange (P5)**: Unyielded Bingham plug at the centerline — moves as a rigid body.
> - **Green (P8)**: Final deposited bead — supported by yield stress $\tau_y \geq \rho g r$.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question Bank PEMET524]`
**Q: Define Direct Ink Writing (DIW). List any three advantages of DIW over Fused Deposition Modeling (FDM).**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):**
Direct Ink Writing (DIW) is a liquid-based material extrusion additive manufacturing process in which a rheology-engineered viscous ink (paste, gel, or colloidal suspension) is deposited through a fine nozzle under computer-controlled motion to build 3D parts layer by layer, without the need for thermal melting of the feedstock.

**Three advantages over FDM (3 × ½ = 1.5 marks; full sentence 0.5 mark):**

1. **Wider material range**: DIW can process ceramics, metals, cermets, composites, and bio-inks — materials that cannot be melted and extruded by FDM.
2. **No thermal degradation**: As no melting is required, temperature-sensitive biomolecules, cells, and polymers can be deposited without degradation.
3. **Higher solid loading possible**: DIW inks may contain 50–60 vol% solids, enabling near-net-shape parts with minimal post-processing shrinkage, unlike FDM's thermoplastic-dominant feedstocks.

---

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model Question Bank PEMET524]`
**Q: What is a "shear-yield stress" fluid? Why is the yield stress critical in DIW?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):**
A **shear-yield stress fluid** is a non-Newtonian material that behaves as a rigid solid when the applied shear stress is below a critical threshold (the yield stress, $\tau_y$), but flows like a liquid once the applied stress exceeds $\tau_y$. Examples include Bingham plastics, Herschel–Bulkley fluids, and many colloidal gels.

**Importance in DIW (1.5 marks):**
The yield stress is critical because it governs the **shape-retention** of the deposited filament after extrusion. Once the ink exits the nozzle and the driving pressure is removed, the local shear stress drops to zero. If $\tau_y$ is sufficiently high (typically $\tau_y \geq \rho g r$ to resist gravitational slumping), the filament will **self-support** its own weight and that of subsequent layers. Without adequate yield stress, the printed part will **slump, sag, and lose dimensional fidelity**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Part B (14 marks):**
> - Students commonly lose **2–3 marks** by **omitting the units** in numerical answers. Always write SI units explicitly.
> - Another frequent pitfall is **confusing the Bingham plastic equation** ($\tau = \tau_y + \eta_p \dot{\gamma}$) with the **Herschel–Bulkley equation** ($\tau = \tau_y + K\dot{\gamma}^n$). The former is a **special case** ($n=1$).
> - A third common mistake is **forgetting the slumping criterion** check when asked "will the printed part hold its shape?" Always verify $\tau_y \geq \rho g r$.

---

### **Question B-A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper PEMET524]`

**(a)** Explain the **Herschel–Bulkley rheological model** with a neat sketch. Discuss how the parameters $\tau_y$, $K$, and $n$ influence printability in DIW. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A Bingham plastic ink has $\tau_y = 120$ Pa, $\eta_p = 8$ Pa·s, and density $\rho = 2200$ kg/m³. It is extruded through a nozzle of length $L = 25$ mm and diameter $d_n = 400$ μm. If the pneumatic pressure is set at $\Delta P = 600$ kPa, calculate:
  (i) Wall shear stress and wall shear rate.
  (ii) Plug radius.
  (iii) Volumetric flow rate using the Buckingham–Reiner equation.
  (iv) Verify whether the deposited filament of radius $r = 200$ μm will slump under gravity. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for B-A:**

**(a) Herschel–Bulkley model (7 marks):**

The **Herschel–Bulkley model** is a generalized constitutive equation for non-Newtonian fluids exhibiting both a yield stress and shear-rate-dependent viscosity:

$$
\tau = \tau_y + K \dot{\gamma}^n \quad \text{for } \tau \geq \tau_y
$$

and $\dot{\gamma} = 0$ when $\tau < \tau_y$.

**Neat sketch (2 marks):**

```
   τ (Pa)
    │
 τ_w├───────────────────●  ← Operating point at wall
    │                  ╱│
    │                 ╱ │ 
 τ_y├────────────●───╱──┤  ← Yield stress
    │           ╱  │   │
    │          ╱   │   │
    │         ╱    │   │
    │        ╱     │   │
    │       ╱      │   │
    │      ╱       │   │
    │     ╱        │   │
    │    ╱ n < 1   n = 1 n > 1
    │___╱___________│___│_____ ˙γ (s⁻¹)
        Shear     Bingham Shear
       thinning           thickening
```

**Effect of parameters (5 marks):**

| Parameter | Effect on Printability |
|---|---|
| **$\tau_y$ (yield stress)** ↑ | Better shape retention but higher extrusion pressure needed. If $\tau_y$ is too low → slumping. If $\tau_y$ is too high → nozzle clogging. |
| **$K$ (consistency index)** ↑ | Higher viscosity at all shear rates → harder to extrude, but stronger green body. Must be balanced against pressure limits. |
| **$n$ (flow index)** ↓ (closer to 0) | Stronger shear-thinning → easy flow at high shear (in nozzle) but firm at rest. **Ideal $n$ for DIW: 0.1–0.4.** |

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:** $\tau_y = 120$ Pa, $\eta_p = 8$ Pa·s, $\rho = 2200$ kg/m³, $L = 0.025$ m, $d_n = 400$ μm $\Rightarrow R = 200$ μm $= 2\times10^{-4}$ m, $\Delta P = 600{,}000$ Pa.

**(i) Wall shear stress and wall shear rate (2 marks):**

$$
\tau_w = \frac{\Delta P \cdot R}{2L} = \frac{600{,}000 \times 2 \times 10^{-4}}{2 \times 0.025} = \frac{120}{0.05} = 2400 \text{ Pa}
$$

$$
\dot{\gamma}_{wall} = \frac{\tau_w - \tau_y}{\eta_p} = \frac{2400 - 120}{8} = 285 \text{ s}^{-1}
$$

**(ii) Plug radius (1.5 marks):**

$$
r_p = \frac{2 L \tau_y}{\Delta P} = \frac{2 \times 0.025 \times 120}{600{,}000} = 1.0 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m} = 10 \, \mu m
$$

**(iii) Volumetric flow rate — Buckingham–Reiner (2.5 marks):**

Yield ratio:

$$
\frac{\tau_y}{\tau_w} = \frac{120}{2400} = 0.05
$$

$$
Q = \frac{\pi R^4 \Delta P}{8 \eta_p L}\left[1 - \frac{4}{3}(0.05) + \frac{1}{3}(0.05)^4\right]
$$

$$
Q = \frac{\pi (2\times10^{-4})^4 \times 600{,}000}{8 \times 8 \times 0.025}\left[1 - 0.0667 + 2.08\times10^{-6}\right]
$$

$$
Q = \frac{\pi \times 1.6\times10^{-15} \times 600{,}000}{1.6} \times 0.9333
$$

$$
Q = 1.885 \times 10^{-9} \times 0.9333 = 1.76 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 1.76 \, \mu L/s
$$

**(iv) Slumping check (1 mark):**

$$
\tau_y^{required} = \rho g r = 2200 \times 9.81 \times 2\times10^{-4} = 4.32 \text{ Pa}
$$

Since $\tau_y = 120 \text{ Pa} \gg 4.32 \text{ Pa}$, the filament **will not slump** — it is well within the safe regime (excess yield stress by a factor of ~28).

---

### **Question B-B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper PEMET524]`

**(a)** Describe the **step-by-step workflow** of the Direct Ink Writing process, from CAD model to finished part. Include a discussion of **ink formulation**, **rheology control**, **deposition strategies**, and **post-processing**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare DIW with **Fused Deposition Modeling (FDM)** and **Stereolithography (SLA)** under the following heads:
  (i) Feedstock form
  (ii) Resolution / feature size
  (iii) Material range
  (iv) Support structure requirement
  (v) Typical applications **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution for B-B:**

**(a) DIW Workflow (7 marks):**

| Stage | Process Step | Key Engineering Aspect |
|---|---|---|
| **1. CAD Design** | Create 3D model in CAD/CAM software; export as STL | Geometric definition |
| **2. Slicing** | Slice STL into 2D layers; generate G-code toolpath | Layer height, infill, perimeter strategy |
| **3. Ink Formulation** | Mix ceramic/metal/polymer powder with binder, dispersant, solvent, and rheology modifier | Solid loading, particle size distribution, binder chemistry |
| **4. Rheology QC** | Measure $\tau_y$, $\eta_p$, $n$ on a rheometer; check against printability map | Must satisfy $\tau_y \geq \rho g r$ and $n < 1$ |
| **5. Deposition** | Extrude ink through nozzle under controlled pressure; traverse XY; raise Z for next layer | Nozzle diameter, print speed, pressure, layer height |
| **6. Drying/Curing** | Dry each layer (thermal, IR, UV) to fix shape before next layer | Solvent evaporation, binder solidification |
| **7. Debinding** | Heat green part slowly (~1 °C/min) to burn out organic binder | Critical step — too fast → cracking |
| **8. Sintering** | High-temperature densification (typically 1200–1600 °C for ceramics) | Achieves >95% theoretical density |
| **9. Post-processing** | Machining, surface finishing, inspection, HIP if needed | Achieves final tolerances |

**(b) Comparison Table (7 marks = 1.4 marks/row):**

| Head | **DIW** | **FDM** | **SLA** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **(i) Feedstock form** | Viscous paste/gel in syringe | Thermoplastic filament (1.75/2.85 mm) | Photopolymer liquid resin |
| **(ii) Resolution** | 100 μm – 1 mm (nozzle-limited) | 100 – 300 μm | 25 – 50 μm (laser spot) |
| **(iii) Material range** | Ceramics, metals, bio-inks, composites, FGMs | Limited thermoplastics (PLA, ABS, PEEK) | Photopolymers, some ceramics (filled resins) |
| **(iv) Support structure** | Often required for overhangs >45°; uses sacrificial material | Required for overhangs >45°; same-material supports | Required for overhangs; tree-like supports |
| **(v) Applications** | Bone scaffolds, solid-oxide fuel cells, functional ceramics, electronics, sensors | Prototyping, jigs, fixtures, consumer parts | Dental, jewelry, prototype models, micro-fluidics |

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🔥 KTU 2024 — Direct Ink Writing (DIW) Rapid-Revision Checklist 🔥**

- ☐ **DIW** = Liquid-based material extrusion of **viscous inks** (no melting required).
- ☐ Belongs to **ISO/ASTM 52900** Material Extrusion family; also called **Robocasting**.
- ☐ **Three-stage process**: Ink Formulation → Deposition → Post-processing.
- ☐ **Herschel–Bulkley** is the master rheology equation: $\tau = \tau_y + K\dot{\gamma}^n$.
- ☐ **Bingham plastic** is the special case ($n=1$): $\tau = \tau_y + \eta_p \dot{\gamma}$.
- ☐ Ideal flow index: **$n < 1$ (shear-thinning)** for DIW.
- ☐ **Slumping criterion**: $\tau_y \geq \rho g r$ — non-negotiable for shape retention.
- ☐ **Hagen–Poiseuille**: $Q = \pi \Delta P d_n^4 / (128 \eta L)$ — note $d_n^4$ sensitivity.
- ☐ **Buckingham–Reiner** is the Bingham-corrected Poiseuille law.
- ☐ **Nozzle diameter** is the most sensitive parameter — typical range **10 μm – 1 mm**.
- ☐ **Capillary number** $Ca = \eta v_p / d_n$ governs droplet-vs-filament transition.
- ☐ **Sintering shrinkage** must be pre-compensated: $L_{green} = L_{final} / (1-s)$.
- ☐ **Typical shrinkage** for ceramic inks: **15–25%** linear.
- ☐ **Materials**: ceramics (Al₂O₃, ZrO₂, SiC), metals (Cu, Ti), polymers, bio-inks, composites.
- ☐ **Applications**: bone scaffolds, fuel cells, sensors, FGM, micro-electronics.
- ☐ **Advantages over FDM**: no thermal degradation, wider material range, higher solid loading.
- ☐ **Disadvantages**: limited resolution, slow, requires post-processing, anisotropic properties.
- ☐ **Key post-processing chain**: Drying → Debinding → Sintering → Inspection.
- ☐ **Green part** = as-printed part with binder; **Brown part** = after debinding; **Dense part** = after sintering.

> **End of Notes — Module 2 / Direct Ink Writing (DIW) — PEMET524 — KTU 2024 Scheme**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Principle
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET524 - ADDITIVE MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Principle

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Additive Manufacturing (AM)** under the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PEMET524)**, **Module 3** focuses on **Vat Photopolymerization** — a family of AM processes. The **"Principle"** topic specifically addresses the foundational operating principle of **Stereolithography (SLA)**, the pioneering and archetypal vat photopolymerization technology invented by **Chuck Hull in 1984** and patented in **1986** (U.S. Patent 4,575,330).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Spirit):**
> *Stereolithography is a vat photopolymerization AM process that builds a three-dimensional solid object by selectively curing a liquid photopolymer resin layer-by-layer using a spatially controlled, ultraviolet (UV) laser beam or projected light source, triggering localized photo-initiated chain-growth polymerization reactions.*

The governing physical mechanism is **photo-initiated polymerization**, in which photons of sufficient energy trigger a chemical reaction that converts liquid monomer/oligomer chains into a cross-linked solid polymer network.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Layer Cake Engine"

Imagine you are baking a cake, but with a magical twist:

1. You pour a thin layer of **liquid chocolate** (photopolymer resin) into a pan.
2. You use a **laser pointer** to "draw" the cross-section of your cake. Wherever the laser touches, the chocolate **magically hardens** instantly.
3. You pour another thin layer on top, and the laser draws the next cross-section. This new layer fuses with the layer below.
4. Repeat hundreds of times — **voilà**, you have a fully formed 3D chocolate sculpture.

This is **exactly** how an SLA machine works. The laser is a **UV laser** (typically **355 nm Nd:YAG** or **405 nm violet laser diode**), the chocolate is a **photopolymer resin**, and the magic is **photo-polymerization chemistry**.

### 1.3 Core Components of an SLA System (Vocabulary Block)

> [!NOTE]
> **Essential Terminology for KTU Board Exam**

| KTU Term | Standard Definition | Role in SLA |
|---|---|---|
| **Vat** | A rectangular tank that holds the liquid photopolymer resin | Acts as the build volume reservoir |
| **Build Platform** | A vertically movable metal plate | Lifts cured layers out of the vat |
| **Recoater** | A blade/roller that spreads fresh resin | Ensures uniform film thickness |
| **Photoinitiator** | A chemical additive (typically **0.5–5 wt%**) | Absorbs UV photons and generates free radicals |
| **Monomer / Oligomer** | Reactive liquid molecules (e.g., acrylates, epoxides) | The "building blocks" that link up to form the solid |
| **Galvanometer Scanner** | A set of rotating mirrors (X and Y) | Steers the laser beam across the resin surface |
| **He-Ne Laser (632.8 nm)** | A red alignment laser | Used for **set-up** and not for curing (a common viva question) |

### 1.4 The "Why" Behind the Principle

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why UV light and not visible light?**
> Photoinitiators are tuned (chemically engineered) to absorb photons in the **UV-A range (315–400 nm)** or near-UV (around **405 nm**). This wavelength matches the bond-dissociation energy required to cleave the photoinitiator molecule into reactive free radicals, triggering the chain reaction.

### 1.5 Energy Dose — The Master Variable

The single most important physical quantity in SLA is the **energy dose** $E_0$ delivered to the resin surface, defined as:

$$E_0 = \frac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t_{\text{exp}}}$$

where $P$ is the **laser power (W or mW)**, $v$ is the **scan speed (m/s or mm/s)**, $h$ is the **hatch spacing (mm)** (the distance between adjacent laser scan lines), and $t_{\text{exp}}$ is the **exposure time (s)**. The unit of $E_0$ is **mJ/cm²**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The energy dose $E_0$ directly controls the **cure depth** $C_d$. If $E_0 < E_c$ (critical energy), the resin will **not cure** at all — it remains a liquid. This is the fundamental threshold behavior of SLA.
</br>
</br>
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Photo-Polymerization Reaction — Step by Step

When a UV photon strikes the photopolymer resin, the following **chain-growth polymerization** sequence occurs:

1. **Photon Absorption:** A photoinitiator molecule (e.g., **Irgacure 819**, **BAPO**, **TPO**) absorbs a UV photon of energy $h\nu$ (where $h$ is Planck's constant and $\nu$ is the photon frequency).
2. **Radical Generation:** The excited photoinitiator cleaves homolytically to produce **two free radicals** (highly reactive species with an unpaired electron).
3. **Chain Initiation:** A free radical attacks the C=C double bond of a monomer (e.g., a methacrylate group), opening the bond and creating a new radical at the chain end.
4. **Chain Propagation:** The newly formed radical attacks another monomer, propagating the chain. This repeats hundreds to thousands of times per second.
5. **Chain Termination:** Two growing chains meet and combine (or undergo disproportionation), ending the chain growth.

> [!NOTE]
> **Two Polymerization Chemistries Used in SLA:**
> - **Free-Radical Polymerization** (most common): Uses acrylate/methacrylate monomers. Fast cure, but shrinkage and oxygen inhibition.
> - **Cationic Polymerization**: Uses epoxy/vinyl ether monomers. Cured with **onium salt photoinitiators**. Slower but more accurate with low shrinkage.

### 2.2 Beer-Lambert Law in SLA — Light Attenuation in Resin

The UV laser beam does not stop at the resin surface. As it penetrates downward, the photopolymer resin **absorbs** the light exponentially. This is governed by the **Beer-Lambert Law** of attenuation:

$$E(z) = E_0 \cdot e^{-z / D_p}$$

where $E(z)$ is the energy dose at depth $z$ below the surface, $E_0$ is the energy dose at the surface, and $D_p$ is the **penetration depth** — defined as the depth at which the light intensity falls to $1/e$ (≈ **36.8%**) of its surface value.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical meaning of $D_p$:** A **large** $D_p$ means the resin is **transparent** to UV (light travels deep). A **small** $D_p$ means the resin is **strongly absorbing** (light is absorbed near the surface). Typical values of $D_p$ for commercial SLA resins range from **0.05 mm to 0.50 mm**.

### 2.3 The Master Equation — Cure Depth (Cd)

The **cure depth** $C_d$ is the thickness of the polymerized layer. It is the most-cited equation in KTU AM examinations. Combining the Beer-Lambert law with the critical energy threshold $E_c$:

$$\boxed{C_d = D_p \cdot \ln\left(\frac{E_0}{E_c}\right)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Master Formula (MEMORIZE THIS):**
> $$C_d = D_p \cdot \ln(E_0 / E_c)$$
> - If $E_0 \leq E_c$, then $C_d \leq 0$ → **no curing** (energy below threshold).
> - $C_d$ increases **logarithmically** with $E_0$ → doubling the energy does NOT double the cure depth.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **exam-relevant** for KTU 2024 Scheme. Units are **strictly observed**.

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Variables & Units | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Surface Energy Dose | $E_0 = \dfrac{P}{v \cdot h}$ | $P$ (W), $v$ (mm/s), $h$ (mm) | Laser energy per unit area at resin surface |
| 2 | Beer-Lambert Attenuation | $E(z) = E_0 \, e^{-z/D_p}$ | $E(z)$ (mJ/cm²), $z$ (mm), $D_p$ (mm) | Light intensity at depth $z$ |
| 3 | Cure Depth (Master Eq.) | $C_d = D_p \cdot \ln(E_0 / E_c)$ | $C_d$, $D_p$ (mm), $E_0$, $E_c$ (mJ/cm²) | Thickness of cured layer |
| 4 | Critical Energy Dose | $E_c$ (constant for a resin) | mJ/cm² | Minimum energy to initiate polymerization |
| 5 | Minimum Cure Depth | $C_d^{\min} = D_p \cdot \ln(P_{\min} / (v_{\max} \cdot h \cdot E_c))$ | Same as above | Threshold to bond layers |
| 6 | Photon Energy | $E_{\text{photon}} = h\nu = hc / \lambda$ | $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s | Energy per UV photon |
| 7 | Volumetric Shrinkage | $S_v = \dfrac{V_{\text{monomer}} - V_{\text{polymer}}}{V_{\text{monomer}}} \times 100\%$ | % | Polymerization shrinkage (typically 3–6%) |
| 8 | Build Time Estimate | $T = \dfrac{Z \cdot N_{\text{layers}}}{v_z}$ | $T$ (s), $Z$ (mm), $v_z$ (mm/s) | Time to build a part of height $Z$ |

> **Important Note on Notation:** $E_c$ is the **critical** energy (sometimes written $E_{\text{crit}}$). It is a **material property** of the photopolymer resin, typically in the range of **5–15 mJ/cm²** for commercial acrylate systems.

### 2.5 Engineering Utility — Why This Matters

The principle of SLA is not just a textbook concept. It governs real industrial decisions:

- **Medical Industry:** SLA is used to produce **dental crowns, surgical guides, and hearing-aid shells** because of its sub-100 μm resolution.
- **Jewelry:** Investment casting patterns are SLA-printed for intricate ring designs.
- **Optical Components:** Lenses and light guides with controlled transparency.
- **Microfluidics:** Lab-on-chip devices with channel sizes <200 μm.

The cure-depth equation $C_d = D_p \ln(E_0 / E_c)$ is used by process engineers to:
1. **Calibrate** laser power and scan speed for a new resin.
2. **Predict** the minimum overcure needed to ensure inter-layer adhesion.
3. **Diagnose** failures (delamination, overcure, under-cure).
</br>
</br>
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Beer-Lambert Law Applied to a Photopolymer Resin

**Starting Premise:** A monochromatic UV laser beam of intensity $I_0$ enters the resin surface. The resin contains photoinitiator molecules that absorb photons. We want to find the intensity at depth $z$.

**Step 1: Differential Attenuation**

In an infinitesimal layer of thickness $dz$ at depth $z$, the fractional loss of intensity $dI$ is proportional to the local intensity $I$ and the layer thickness $dz$:

$$dI = -\alpha \cdot I(z) \cdot dz$$

where $\alpha$ is the **absorption coefficient** of the resin (units: mm⁻¹). The negative sign indicates intensity **decreases** with depth.

**Step 2: Separation of Variables**

Rearranging the differential equation:

$$\frac{dI}{I} = -\alpha \cdot dz$$

**Step 3: Integration**

Integrating both sides from the surface (where $I = I_0$ at $z = 0$) to an arbitrary depth $z$:

$$\int_{I_0}^{I(z)} \frac{dI}{I} = -\alpha \int_{0}^{z} dz$$

**Step 4: Evaluating the Integrals**

$$\ln(I(z)) - \ln(I_0) = -\alpha \cdot z$$

$$\ln\left(\frac{I(z)}{I_0}\right) = -\alpha \cdot z$$

**Step 5: Exponentiation**

$$\frac{I(z)}{I_0} = e^{-\alpha \cdot z}$$

**Step 6: Substitution of the Penetration Depth**

By definition, the **penetration depth** $D_p$ is the depth at which $I(z) = I_0 / e$. Substituting this into the above equation:

$$\frac{1}{e} = e^{-\alpha \cdot D_p} \quad \Rightarrow \quad -1 = -\alpha \cdot D_p \quad \Rightarrow \quad D_p = \frac{1}{\alpha}$$

**Step 7: Final Form (Beer-Lambert for SLA)**

$$\boxed{I(z) = I_0 \cdot e^{-z / D_p}}$$

In terms of energy dose (which is intensity multiplied by exposure time, both of which are constants for a given scan):

$$E(z) = E_0 \cdot e^{-z / D_p} \quad \text{✓ Derived.}$$

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — The Cure Depth Master Equation

**Starting Premise:** Polymerization is initiated only when the local energy dose $E(z)$ at depth $z$ reaches the **critical energy** $E_c$. The cure depth $C_d$ is therefore the depth at which $E(z) = E_c$.

**Step 1: Apply the Curing Condition**

At the cure depth $z = C_d$, the energy dose equals the critical energy:

$$E(C_d) = E_c$$

**Step 2: Substitute the Beer-Lambert Expression**

$$E_0 \cdot e^{-C_d / D_p} = E_c$$

**Step 3: Isolate the Exponential Term**

$$e^{-C_d / D_p} = \frac{E_c}{E_0}$$

**Step 4: Take the Natural Logarithm of Both Sides**

$$-\frac{C_d}{D_p} = \ln\left(\frac{E_c}{E_0}\right)$$

**Step 5: Use the Logarithm Identity $\ln(a/b) = -\ln(b/a)$**

$$-\frac{C_d}{D_p} = -\ln\left(\frac{E_0}{E_c}\right)$$

**Step 6: Solve for $C_d$**

$$\boxed{C_d = D_p \cdot \ln\left(\frac{E_0}{E_c}\right)} \quad \text{✓ Derived.}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Validity Condition:** This equation is valid **only when** $E_0 > E_c$. If $E_0 \leq E_c$, the logarithm becomes zero or negative, and physically no curing occurs.

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU 2-Mark Sub-Question)

> **Problem:** A SLA system uses a photopolymer resin with $D_p = 0.15$ mm and $E_c = 8.5$ mJ/cm². The laser power is set to $P = 80$ mW, the scan speed is $v = 200$ mm/s, and the hatch spacing is $h = 0.10$ mm. Calculate the cure depth $C_d$.

**Step 1: Calculate the Surface Energy Dose $E_0$**

$$E_0 = \frac{P}{v \cdot h} = \frac{80 \text{ mW}}{200 \text{ mm/s} \times 0.10 \text{ mm}}$$

$$E_0 = \frac{80}{20} = 4.0 \text{ mJ/mm}^2$$

**Step 2: Convert Units to Match $E_c$ (mJ/cm²)**

Since $1 \text{ mm}^2 = 0.01 \text{ cm}^2$, we have $1 \text{ mJ/mm}^2 = 100 \text{ mJ/cm}^2$:

$$E_0 = 4.0 \times 100 = 400 \text{ mJ/cm}^2$$

**Step 3: Apply the Cure Depth Equation**

$$C_d = D_p \cdot \ln\left(\frac{E_0}{E_c}\right) = 0.15 \times \ln\left(\frac{400}{8.5}\right)$$

$$C_d = 0.15 \times \ln(47.06)$$

$$C_d = 0.15 \times 3.852 = 0.578 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4: Interpret**

$$\boxed{C_d \approx 0.578 \text{ mm}}$$

This means each layer is cured to a depth of about **578 μm** below the resin surface. Since layers are typically built at a thickness of **100 μm**, the laser **over-cures** significantly into the previous layer, ensuring strong inter-layer adhesion.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Effects of Process Parameters

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This matrix is **directly relevant to KTU 14-mark questions** asking "Explain the effect of process parameters on SLA."

| Parameter | Symbol | Effect on $E_0$ | Effect on $C_d$ | Engineering Consequence |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| ↑ Laser Power | $P$ | ↑ $E_0$ (proportional) | ↑ $C_d$ (logarithmic) | Deeper cure, faster build, risk of overcure |
| ↑ Scan Speed | $v$ | ↓ $E_0$ (inversely) | ↓ $C_d$ | Shallower cure, better resolution, slower build |
| ↑ Hatch Spacing | $h$ | ↓ $E_0$ (inversely) | ↓ $C_d$ | Wider gaps, possible undercure between scan lines |
| ↑ Photoinitiator Concentration | — | ↓ $D_p$ (more absorption) | ↓ $C_d$ at fixed $E_0$ | Sharper vertical resolution, less penetration |
| ↑ Layer Thickness | $L$ | No direct effect | Demand ↑ $C_d$ | Need higher $E_0$ to ensure bonding |
| ↓ $E_c$ (more reactive resin) | $E_c$ | Same | ↑ $C_d$ | Easier to cure, but risks overcure |

### 3.5 KTU-Style "Show That" Sub-Question Scaffold

> **Question Pattern:** *"Show that for a typical SLA process, doubling the laser power does not double the cure depth."*

**Solution Scaffold:**

$$\frac{C_d^{(2P)}}{C_d^{(P)}} = \frac{D_p \ln(2P / (v h E_c))}{D_p \ln(P / (v h E_c))} = \frac{\ln(2) + \ln(P / (v h E_c))}{\ln(P / (v h E_c))} \neq 2$$

This non-linear, logarithmic relationship is a **signature feature of SLA** and a frequent 7-mark sub-part in KTU examinations.
</br>
</br>
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of a SLA System

The diagram below maps the **physical subsystems** of a Stereolithography apparatus and their interactions. All node IDs are alphanumeric (per protocol), and labels are clean uppercase text to ensure successful Mermaid rendering.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A1[UV LASER SOURCE] --> A2[BEAM EXPANDER AND SHUTTER]
    A2 --> A3[X Y GALVANOMETER SCANNER]
    A3 --> A4[F THETA LENS]
    A4 --> A5[RESIN VAT SURFACE]
    A5 --> A6[CURED LAYER]
    A6 --> A7[BUILD PLATFORM]
    A7 --> A8[Z AXIS ELEVATOR]
    A8 --> A5
    A5 -.-> A9[RECOATER BLADE]
    A9 --> A5
    B1[CONTROL COMPUTER] -.-> A1
    B1 -.-> A3
    B1 -.-> A8
    C1[PHOTOINITIATOR] --> A5
    C2[MONOMER OLIGOMER] --> A5
    A6 --> D1[POLYMER NETWORK]
    A8 --> E1[FINAL 3D PART]
```

**Reading the Diagram:**

- The **solid arrows** (A1 → A2 → ... → A5) represent the **optical path** of the UV laser.
- The **dashed arrows** represent **electrical control signals** from the computer.
- The **dotted arrows** from A5 to A9 represent the **recoating action** (mechanical).
- The loop **A5 → A6 → A7 → A8 → A5** represents the **layer-by-layer build cycle**.

### 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart — Photo-Polymerization Reaction Chain

```mermaid
graph TD
    step1[UV PHOTON H NU] --> step2[PHOTOINITIATOR EXCITED STATE]
    step2 --> step3[FREE RADICAL GENERATION]
    step3 --> step4[CHAIN INITIATION]
    step4 --> step5[CHAIN PROPAGATION]
    step5 --> step6[CHAIN TERMINATION]
    step5 --> step5
    step6 --> step7[CROSSLINKED POLYMER NETWORK]
    step1 -.-> stepA[IF PHOTON ENERGY LESS THAN BANDGAP]
    stepA --> stepB[NO REACTION RESIN REMAINS LIQUID]
```

**Key Observation:** Step 5 has a **self-loop** because chain propagation is iterative — the same type of reaction (radical attacking a monomer C=C bond) repeats thousands of times per second.

### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Energy Dose Logic Tree

```mermaid
graph TD
    E0[SURFACE ENERGY DOSE E0] --> Q1{E0 GREATER THAN EC}
    Q1 -->|YES| Q2[RESIN CURES]
    Q1 -->|NO| Q3[NO CURE LIQUID REMAINS]
    Q2 --> Q4[BEER LAMBERT ATTENUATION]
    Q4 --> Q5[DEPTH WHERE E EQUALS EC]
    Q5 --> Q6[CURE DEPTH CD]
    E0 --> P1[LASER POWER P]
    E0 --> P2[SCAN SPEED V]
    E0 --> P3[HATCH SPACING H]
    P1 -->|PROPORTIONAL| E0
    P2 -->|INVERSELY PROPORTIONAL| E0
    P3 -->|INVERSELY PROPORTIONAL| E0
```

**Reading the Diagram:**

- This decision tree clarifies the **threshold behavior** of SLA. The single condition $E_0 > E_c$ is the gatekeeper for whether polymerization occurs at all.
- Below the threshold, the resin remains liquid regardless of the time the laser dwells on it.

### 4.4 Process Sequence — Top-Down vs. Bottom-Up (Conceptual Note)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Sub-Question (Common):** *"Differentiate between the top-down (vector-by-vector) and bottom-up (projection) approaches in vat photopolymerization."*

| Feature | Top-Down (Classical SLA) | Bottom-Up (DLP / LCD) |
|---|---|---|
| Laser Type | Scanning UV laser (Nd:YAG) | Projected light (DMD / LCD) |
| Build Direction | Top-down (platform moves up) | Bottom-down (platform moves down) |
| Resin Volume | Large vat (wasteful) | Thin film (less waste) |
| Throughput | Slow (point-by-point) | Fast (full layer at once) |
| Resolution | High (50–100 μm) | Moderate (pixel-limited, ~35–50 μm) |
| Patent Status | Hull 1986 patent (expired) | Various (still active) |
</br>
</br>
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
> *Define Stereolithography. List the four main components of an SLA system.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Stereolithography (SLA) is a **vat photopolymerization** additive manufacturing process in which a focused UV laser selectively cures a liquid photopolymer resin in a layer-by-layer fashion to build a 3D solid object, based on cross-sectional data from a 3D CAD model.

**Four main components:**
1. **Resin vat** — holds the liquid photopolymer.
2. **UV laser** (typically 355 nm) — initiates polymerization.
3. **Galvanometer-based X-Y scanner** — directs the laser beam.
4. **Build platform** — supports the part and moves in the Z direction between layers.

*(Optional 5th component for full credit: He-Ne alignment laser + recoater blade.)*

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> *What is meant by the term "penetration depth" in SLA? How is it related to the absorption coefficient of the resin?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Penetration depth ($D_p$)** is defined as the depth below the resin surface at which the laser intensity (or energy dose) falls to **$1/e$ (≈ 36.8%)** of its value at the surface, due to absorption by the photoinitiator.

**Relation to absorption coefficient:**

$$\boxed{D_p = \frac{1}{\alpha}}$$

where $\alpha$ is the absorption coefficient (units: mm⁻¹). A **high-absorption** resin has a **small $D_p$** (shallow penetration, sharp cure profile), while a **transparent** resin has a **large $D_p$** (deep penetration).

### 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks — Choice 1)

> **Q.A. [KTU University Exam – KTU Model Paper 2024]**
> *(a)* Explain the **principle of Stereolithography (SLA)** with a neat block diagram. Describe the photo-polymerization mechanism and discuss the role of the photoinitiator. **(7 marks)**
>
> *(b)* Derive the **cure depth equation** $C_d = D_p \cdot \ln(E_0 / E_c)$ starting from the Beer-Lambert law. A resin has $D_p = 0.20$ mm and $E_c = 10$ mJ/cm². If the laser power is 100 mW, scan speed is 250 mm/s, and hatch spacing is 0.10 mm, calculate the cure depth. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q.A(a):**

**Principle of SLA:**

Stereolithography builds 3D parts by **selectively solidifying a liquid photopolymer resin** using a focused **UV laser beam**. The part is constructed **one layer at a time** from the bottom up. For each layer, the laser traces the cross-sectional geometry of the part on the resin surface, **photopolymerizing** only the regions corresponding to the solid part.

**Block diagram:** *(Refer to Section 4.1 of these notes for the complete Mermaid architecture diagram.)*

**Photo-polymerization mechanism:**

1. **Photon absorption** — A UV photon (wavelength ≈ 355 nm) is absorbed by a photoinitiator molecule (e.g., Irgacure 819, BAPO), promoting it to an excited singlet state.
2. **Radical generation** — The excited photoinitiator undergoes **α-cleavage (Norrish Type I)** to produce **two free radicals**.
3. **Chain initiation** — A free radical attacks the C=C double bond of a monomer (e.g., a methacrylate group), generating a new chain-end radical.
4. **Chain propagation** — The chain-end radical attacks successive monomers, building a polymer chain (degree of polymerization can exceed 10,000).
5. **Chain termination** — Two chain radicals combine (coupling) or disproportionate, ending chain growth.

**Role of the photoinitiator:**

- The photoinitiator is the **light-sensitive trigger** of the entire reaction.
- Without it, the liquid monomer would be **optically transparent** to UV at the operating wavelength.
- It is typically present at **0.5–5 wt%** in the resin.
- It is a **sacrificial component** — it is consumed during the reaction.

---

**Model Solution for Q.A(b):**

**Derivation:** *(Refer to Section 3.2 of these notes for the full step-by-step derivation.)*

**Numerical Calculation:**

**Step 1:** Surface energy dose

$$E_0 = \frac{P}{v \cdot h} = \frac{100 \text{ mW}}{250 \text{ mm/s} \times 0.10 \text{ mm}} = \frac{100}{25} = 4.0 \text{ mJ/mm}^2$$

**Step 2:** Unit conversion

$$E_0 = 4.0 \text{ mJ/mm}^2 \times 100 \text{ mm}^2/\text{cm}^2 = 400 \text{ mJ/cm}^2$$

**Step 3:** Apply cure depth equation

$$C_d = D_p \cdot \ln\left(\frac{E_0}{E_c}\right) = 0.20 \times \ln\left(\frac{400}{10}\right) = 0.20 \times \ln(40)$$

$$C_d = 0.20 \times 3.689 = 0.738 \text{ mm}$$

$$\boxed{C_d \approx 0.738 \text{ mm}}$$

**Interpretation:** The cured layer thickness is about **738 μm**, which is significantly more than the typical build layer thickness of 100 μm. This 7-fold overcure ensures robust inter-layer bonding.

### 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks — Choice 2, Internal Choice)

> **Q.B. [KTU University Exam – KTU Model Paper 2024, Alternate]**
> *(a)* Explain the **photo-polymerization process** in SLA, with a comparison between **free-radical** and **cationic** polymerization. **(7 marks)**
>
> *(b)* A SLA system has $D_p = 0.25$ mm and $E_c = 12$ mJ/cm². The build layer thickness is 0.10 mm. Determine the **minimum laser power** required if the scan speed is 150 mm/s and the hatch spacing is 0.12 mm. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q.B(a):**

**Photo-polymerization in SLA:** *(Brief recap as in Q.A(a).)*

**Free-Radical Polymerization (most common in commercial SLA):**

- **Monomer type:** Acrylates, methacrylates.
- **Photoinitiator type:** Type I (cleavage) — e.g., BAPO, TPO, Irgacure 819.
- **Active species:** Free radicals.
- **Reaction speed:** Very fast (chain growth in microseconds).
- **Drawbacks:** Oxygen inhibition (O₂ quenches radicals), volume shrinkage (3–6%), post-cure required.
- **Inhibition:** Atmospheric oxygen diffuses into the top ~10 μm and partially inhibits curing, leading to a "dead zone" near the surface.

**Cationic Polymerization:**

- **Monomer type:** Cyclic ethers (epoxides), vinyl ethers.
- **Photoinitiator type:** Onium salts (e.g., diaryliodonium, triarylsulfonium).
- **Active species:** Super acids (e.g., H⁺ from Lewis acid generation).
- **Reaction speed:** Slower (minutes to hours for full cure).
- **Advantages:** **No oxygen inhibition** (acid is not quenched by O₂), **low shrinkage** (<1%), **dark cure** (reaction continues after light is removed).
- **Drawbacks:** Sensitive to moisture (water terminates the chain), expensive monomers.

| Feature | Free-Radical | Cationic |
|---|---|---|
| Active Species | Radical (R•) | Cation (H⁺, R⁺) |
| Oxygen Sensitivity | High (inhibits) | Low (no inhibition) |
| Shrinkage | 3–6% | <1% |
| Speed | Fast | Slow |
| Humidity Sensitivity | Low | High |
| Accuracy | Lower (shrinkage) | Higher |

---

**Model Solution for Q.B(b):**

**Given:**
- $D_p = 0.25$ mm
- $E_c = 12$ mJ/cm²
- Layer thickness $L = 0.10$ mm
- $v = 150$ mm/s
- $h = 0.12$ mm

**Step 1:** For reliable bonding between layers, the cure depth $C_d$ must be **at least equal to the layer thickness** $L$ (often 2–3× $L$ is used in practice, but for minimum-power calculation, we set $C_d = L$):

$$C_d^{\min} = 0.10 \text{ mm} = D_p \cdot \ln\left(\frac{E_0^{\min}}{E_c}\right)$$

**Step 2:** Solve for $E_0^{\min}$

$$0.10 = 0.25 \cdot \ln\left(\frac{E_0^{\min}}{12}\right)$$

$$\ln\left(\frac{E_0^{\min}}{12}\right) = \frac{0.10}{0.25} = 0.40$$

$$\frac{E_0^{\min}}{12} = e^{0.40} = 1.4918$$

$$E_0^{\min} = 12 \times 1.4918 = 17.90 \text{ mJ/cm}^2$$

**Step 3:** Convert to mJ/mm²

$$E_0^{\min} = \frac{17.90}{100} = 0.179 \text{ mJ/mm}^2$$

**Step 4:** Solve for minimum power $P_{\min}$ using $E_0 = P / (v \cdot h)$

$$P_{\min} = E_0^{\min} \cdot v \cdot h = 0.179 \times 150 \times 0.12$$

$$P_{\min} = 0.179 \times 18 = 3.222 \text{ mW}$$

$$\boxed{P_{\min} \approx 3.22 \text{ mW}}$$

**Practical remark:** In industry, a safety factor of 2–3 is applied, so the actual operating power would be **6–10 mW**. The student should always state this engineering judgment in a 7-mark answer.

### 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency in $E_0$ Calculation:** Mixing $P$ in mW with $v$ in mm/s and $h$ in mm gives $E_0$ in **mJ/mm²**. Most textbook values of $E_c$ are in **mJ/cm²**. Forgetting to convert (or convert incorrectly) leads to a **wrong answer by a factor of 100**. This is the #1 cause of marks lost in numerical sub-questions.
>
> 2. **Writing $E_0 < E_c$ Gives Negative $C_d$:** Students often plug in $E_0 < E_c$ into the formula and get a negative cure depth, then leave it as such. The correct interpretation is: **NO curing occurs below threshold.**
>
> 3. **Confusing Penetration Depth $D_p$ with Cure Depth $C_d$:** $D_p$ is a **material property** of the resin. $C_d$ is the **process output** that depends on $E_0$ and $E_c$. They are **not the same**.
>
> 4. **Forgetting to Mention Photoinitiator:** Any answer on "principle of SLA" that omits the role of the photoinitiator is graded down by at least 1 mark.
>
> 5. **Drawing a He-Ne laser as the curing source:** The He-Ne (632.8 nm, red) laser is for **alignment only**. The curing source is the **UV laser** (355 nm). Many students draw the wrong color/label.

### 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist (Pin this before the exam!)**
>
> - [ ] SLA = **Vat Photopolymerization** AM process; invented by **Chuck Hull (1984)**.
> - [ ] Curing source: **UV laser**, typically **Nd:YAG at 355 nm** (third harmonic).
> - [ ] He-Ne laser (**632.8 nm**, red) is for **alignment only** — common viva question.
> - [ ] The active chemistry is **photo-initiated chain-growth polymerization**.
> - [ ] The **photoinitiator** (e.g., BAPO, TPO) absorbs UV and generates **free radicals**.
> - [ ] Light attenuation in resin follows the **Beer-Lambert Law**: $E(z) = E_0 \, e^{-z/D_p}$.
> - [ ] **Penetration depth $D_p$** is the depth at which light intensity falls to $1/e$ ≈ 36.8%.
> - [ ] **Master Equation:** $C_d = D_p \cdot \ln(E_0 / E_c)$ — **memorize and derive**.
> - [ ] **Energy dose units:** $E_0 = P / (v \cdot h)$ with $P$ in mW, $v$ in mm/s, $h$ in mm → $E_0$ in mJ/mm²; convert to mJ/cm² by multiplying by **100**.
> - [ ] **Threshold condition:** $E_0 > E_c$ is mandatory; otherwise, no curing.
> - [ ] Cure depth is **logarithmic** in $E_0$ — doubling power does **not** double $C_d$.
> - [ ] Typical commercial $D_p$: **0.05–0.50 mm**; typical $E_c$: **5–15 mJ/cm²**.
> - [ ] **Two polymerization types:** **Free-radical** (acrylates, fast, shrinkage) vs. **Cationic** (epoxies, slow, low shrinkage, no O₂ inhibition).
> - [ ] **Oxygen inhibition** is a real phenomenon that creates a "dead zone" in free-radical SLA.
> - [ ] **Layer thickness $L$** should be < $C_d$ (often $L = C_d / 2$ to $C_d / 3$) to ensure inter-layer bonding.
> - [ ] **Build time** depends on number of layers, not just part height: more layers = more recoating overhead.
> - [ ] **Modern variants:** DLP (Digital Light Processing) and LCD/MSLA use a **projector** instead of a scanning laser.
> - [ ] **Applications:** Dental, jewelry, hearing aids, microfluidics, optical prototypes.
> - [ ] **Major limitation:** Material is **photosensitive resin** — limited mechanical/thermal properties compared to engineering plastics.
</br>
</br>
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET524 - ADDITIVE MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Materials

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 3: Materials for Additive Manufacturing

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *Additive Manufacturing (AM) materials* refer to the broad spectrum of raw feedstock — in the form of **powders, wires, filaments, resins, sheets, and pellets** — that are selectively joined, fused, or solidified layer-by-layer under computer control to build a final functional part.

Additive Manufacturing is fundamentally a **materials-driven** technology. Unlike subtractive methods (milling, turning) where the material is largely fixed (a metal billet) and shape is varied, in AM the **material's physical form, thermal behavior, rheology, and chemistry dictate the entire process window**. A wrong powder size distribution or an incorrect polymer melt flow index (MFI) can render a part porous, warped, or completely unprintable.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — "Cooking with the Right Ingredients":**
> Imagine you are baking a cake. A conventional bakery (subtractive manufacturing) buys a *generic flour block* and carves the cake shape out of it — wasting 80% of the material as shavings. A 3D printing kitchen (additive manufacturing) instead uses *specialized ingredient streams* (icing sugar squeezed from a nozzle, chocolate powder fused by laser, batter extruded as a thin line) and **stacks them into a cake layer-by-layer**. The catch? Each ingredient has its own *fusing temperature, viscosity, and flow behavior*. The chef (engineer) MUST know these properties perfectly — otherwise the cake collapses, burns, or comes out rubbery. This is precisely why **materials science is the heart of AM**.

### 1.1 Why Materials are Critical in AM

In KTU 2024 scheme outcomes, understanding AM materials is tied to:

- **CO3 (Module 3):** *Select appropriate materials and process parameters for given AM applications.*
- The material dictates: **(a) Process compatibility, (b) Final part properties, (c) Post-processing needs, and (d) Cost.**

### 1.2 Classification of AM Materials (Broad Overview)

The major families used across all AM processes are:

| Family | Common Feedstock Form | Typical AM Processes |
|---|---|---|
| **Polymers** | Filament, pellets, powder, liquid resin | FDM, SLS, SLA, MJF, Material Jetting |
| **Metals** | Powder (15–45 µm), wire | SLM, DMLS, EBM, LMD, WAAM, EBF³ |
| **Ceramics** | Powder + binder, slurry, filament | Binder Jetting, VPP, Robocasting |
| **Composites** | Fiber-reinforced filament, hybrid powder | FDM, SLM, DIW |
| **Biomaterials** | Hydrogels, bio-inks, biocompatible metals/ceramics | Bioprinting, SLM (Ti6Al4V) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metric to Remember:**
> **Particle size of metal powder for laser-based AM (SLM/DMLS) is typically 15–45 µm** with a near-spherical morphology to ensure good flowability and uniform powder bed packing.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Polymer Materials in AM

Polymers are the **most widely used AM material class** (by volume of parts produced). They fall into three sub-categories:

### 2.1.1 Thermoplastics (FDM / FFF / FGF)

These are the workhorses of extrusion-based AM. They soften upon heating and re-solidify on cooling — a fully reversible physical change.

**Common AM thermoplastics:**

- **PLA (Polylactic Acid):** Easy to print, biodegradable, low warping. Used in prototyping, education, medical models.
- **ABS (Acrylonitrile Butadiene Styrene):** Tough, heat-resistant. Needs a heated bed (100 °C) and enclosed chamber. Used in functional prototypes, LEGO.
- **PETG (Polyethylene Terephthalate Glycol):** Balance of strength and printability. Used in mechanical parts, food containers.
- **Polyamide (PA12 / Nylon):** High strength, flexibility, chemical resistance. Used in SLS.
- **PEEK / PEKK:** High-performance polymers. Service temp up to 250–300 °C. Used in aerospace and medical implants.
- **TPU (Thermoplastic Polyurethane):** Elastomeric, flexible. Used in gaskets, soft robotics.

### 2.1.2 Photopolymers (Vat Polymerization — SLA / DLP / CLIP)

These are **liquid resins** that cure (crosslink) under **UV light** (typically 385–405 nm wavelength).

- Composed of **monomers + oligomers + photoinitiators**.
- Photoinitiator absorbs UV → generates free radicals → initiates chain-growth polymerization.
- Used in: dental aligners, jewelry casting patterns, hearing aids, microfluidic devices.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Reality:** Photopolymer parts are brittle and UV-sensitive after printing. They require post-curing in a UV oven to achieve full mechanical properties.

### 2.1.3 Polymer Powders (Powder Bed Fusion — SLS / MJF / BJ)

- **Polyamide 12 (PA12)** is the dominant SLS material — excellent flow, low moisture absorption, consistent melting.
- **Polyamide 11 (PA11)** — bio-based (castor oil origin), more flexible than PA12.
- **TPU powders** — emerging for flexible lattice structures.
- **PAEK family** (PEEK, PEKK) — high-performance powders for aerospace.

## 2.2 Metal Materials in AM

Metal AM uses **atomized metal powders** (gas atomization being the most common) or **wires**.

### 2.2.1 Powder Production for AM

**Gas Atomization (most common):**
- Molten metal is poured through a nozzle.
- High-pressure inert gas (Argon/Nitrogen) jet disintegrates the stream into fine droplets.
- Droplets solidify as near-spherical particles during free fall.
- **Result:** High sphericity, good flow, satellite-free particles.

**Plasma Atomization / Plasma Rotating Electrode Process (PREP):**
- Used for reactive/refractory metals (Titanium).
- A rotating metal electrode is melted by plasma; centrifugal force ejects droplets.
- Yields extremely pure, highly spherical powders.

**Water Atomization:**
- Cheaper, but produces irregular, oxidized particles — **NOT preferred for laser AM**.

### 2.2.2 Critical Powder Characteristics

| Property | Why it Matters | Typical Value (AM grade) |
|---|---|---|
| **Particle size distribution (PSD)** | Affects layer thickness & melt pool stability | 15–45 µm (laser), 45–105 µm (EBM) |
| **Sphericity** | Better flow = uniform powder bed | > 0.85 (aspect ratio) |
| **Flow rate (Hall flow)** | Powder must spread evenly | < 30 s for 50 g |
| **Apparent density** | Packing efficiency in bed | ~50–60% of theoretical |
| **Tap density** | Max packing after vibration | ~60–65% |
| **Oxygen content** | Oxidation → porosity & brittleness | < 0.1 wt% for Ti, < 0.05% for Al |

### 2.2.3 Common Metal AM Alloys

- **Titanium Alloys:** **Ti6Al4V (Grade 5)** — aerospace, biomedical (osseointegration).
- **Aluminum Alloys:** **AlSi10Mg**, **Al6061**, **Scalmalloy** (Al-Mg-Sc) — automotive, lightweighting.
- **Steels:** **316L stainless steel** (medical, marine), **17-4 PH** (aerospace, tooling), **Maraging steel 300** (tooling).
- **Nickel Superalloys:** **Inconel 718**, **Inconel 625** — gas turbine, rocket engines.
- **Cobalt-Chrome:** **CoCrMo** — dental crowns, orthopedic implants.
- **Refractory metals:** Tungsten, Tantalum, Molybdenum — defense, high-temp applications.
- **Copper & Precious metals:** Pure Cu (induction coils), Gold/Silver (jewelry) — high thermal/electrical conductivity.

## 2.3 Ceramic Materials in AM

Ceramics are **the hardest to process** in AM due to high melting points, brittleness, and low thermal shock resistance.

### 2.3.1 AM Approaches for Ceramics

1. **Indirect AM (most common):** Print a "green part" with binder + ceramic powder, then debind and sinter.
   - Processes: Binder Jetting, Vat Photopolymerization (with filled resins).
2. **Direct AM:** Melt ceramic directly with laser (research stage — crack-prone).
3. **Extrusion-based:** Robocasting/DIW (Direct Ink Writing) of ceramic paste.

### 2.3.2 Common Ceramic Materials

- **Alumina (Al₂O₃):** Bioceramics, insulators, cutting tools.
- **Zirconia (ZrO₂):** Dental crowns, oxygen sensors.
- **Silicon Carbide (SiC):** High-temp, aerospace.
- **Silicon Nitride (Si₃N₄):** Bearings, engine components.
- **Hydroxyapatite (HA):** Bone scaffolds, coatings.
- **Tricalcium Phosphate (TCP):** Bioresorbable bone grafts.

## 2.4 Composite Materials in AM

Composites combine **two or more distinct phases** to achieve properties unattainable by either alone.

- **Fiber-reinforced polymers:** Carbon fiber, glass fiber, or Kevlar-filled filaments for FDM (e.g., Markforged).
- **Short fiber composites:** Chopped carbon fiber in Nylon (CF-Nylon).
- **Metal Matrix Composites (MMC):** SiC-reinforced Al alloys, TiB-reinforced Ti alloys.
- **Functionally Graded Materials (FGM):** Composition varies across part (e.g., porous bone-side to dense body-side in implants).
- **Nanocomposites:** Graphene, CNT-reinforced polymers for enhanced strength/conductivity.

## 2.5 Biomaterials for Bioprinting

A specialized subset of AM materials designed for **tissue engineering and regenerative medicine**.

- **Natural polymers:** Collagen, Gelatin, Alginate, Hyaluronic Acid, Chitosan.
- **Synthetic biopolymers:** PLA, PGA, PLGA, PCL.
- **Bio-inks:** Cell-laden hydrogels (crosslinkable by UV, temperature, or ions).
- **Decellularized ECM (dECM):** Tissue-specific bio-inks.
- **Bioactive ceramics:** Hydroxyapatite, TCP — for bone tissue scaffolds.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:**
> *Cell viability* in extrusion bioprinting requires **shear stress < 5 kPa** in the nozzle. Shear-thinning hydrogels (e.g., GelMA) are preferred to protect living cells.

## 2.6 Material Selection for AM — Decision Flow

When selecting an AM material, the engineer must evaluate:

1. **Application requirements** (mechanical, thermal, chemical, biocompatibility).
2. **Process compatibility** (does the material exist in the required feedstock form?).
3. **Post-processing needs** (HIP, heat treatment, surface finishing).
4. **Cost & lead time** (powder cost: Ti6Al4V ~$300–500/kg; PA12 ~$50–80/kg).
5. **Certification status** (especially for aerospace AS9100 and medical ISO 13485).

## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Formulas for AM Materials (Kerala University Exam Focus):**

| Formula / Relation | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|
| $d_{layer} = k \cdot d_{powder}$ | Typical layer thickness is 0.5–1.0× mean powder diameter | µm |
| $P_{abs} = P_{laser} \cdot (1 - R)$ | Absorbed laser power accounts for material reflectivity $R$ | W |
| $E_d = \dfrac{P_{laser}}{v \cdot h \cdot t}$ | Volumetric Energy Density (VED) — the **most critical AM metric** | J/mm³ |
| $\rho_{rel} = \dfrac{\rho_{part}}{\rho_{theory}} \times 100$ | Relative density of printed part (target > 99.5% for structural parts) | % |
| $C_{pack} = \dfrac{\rho_{tap}}{\rho_{true}} \times 100$ | Packing efficiency of powder bed | % |
| $t_{cool} = \dfrac{Q}{h \cdot A \cdot \Delta T}$ | Local solidification time (heat transfer) | s |
| $\sigma_{y,AM} = k \cdot d^{-n}$ | Hall-Petch strengthening in AM metals (grain refinement) | MPa |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Clarification on VED:**
> Volumetric Energy Density (VED) is given by the standard 1D energy density formula:
>
> $$E_d = \frac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t}$$
>
> where $P$ = laser power (W), $v$ = scan speed (mm/s), $h$ = hatch spacing (mm), $t$ = layer thickness (mm).
>
> Some KTU textbooks present a **2D areal energy density** $E_a = P / (v \cdot h)$ [J/mm²]. **Do NOT confuse** the two in your exam. Read the question carefully.

### 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace (GE Aviation):** Fuel nozzle tip for LEAP engine — printed in **CoCrMo**, consolidates 20 parts into 1, 25% lighter.
- **Medical (Patient-specific implants):** **Ti6Al4V** cranial implants, custom hip cups.
- **Automotive (Bugatti, Bugatti Chiron):** **Ti6Al4V** brake caliper, 41% lighter, 8-part → 1-part consolidation.
- **Tooling:** Conformal cooling channels in **17-4 PH** injection molds — 40% cycle time reduction.
- **Jewelry:** Direct printing of 18K gold using laser sintering for complex lattice designs.
