```mermaid
        RC[Recoater Spreads Powder] --> MP
        IG[Inert Gas Inlet Ar] --> MP
        EX[Exhaust and Filter] --> MP
    end
    MP --> SL[Solidified Layer]
    SL --> NL[Next Layer Cycle]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** The block diagram is the *functionally equivalent* alternative to a physical 3D sketch. In KTU 2024 board answers, examiners accept labelled block diagrams showing **(a) laser source, (b) scanner, (c) recoater, (d) build platform, (e) inert gas system, and (f) control loop** as a complete schematic.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define Selective Laser Melting (SLM). List any two metals commonly processed by SLM.*

**Model Answer:**
Selective Laser Melting (SLM) is a powder bed fusion additive manufacturing process in which a high-power fiber laser selectively and **fully melts** regions of a metal powder bed according to sliced CAD data, building a part layer by layer inside an **inert atmosphere** (typically argon). The process achieves **near-full theoretical density (>99 %)**.
Two commonly processed metals: **(i) Ti-6Al-4V** (aerospace & biomedical), **(ii) 316L stainless steel** (industrial/tooling). *(Other acceptable answers: AlSi10Mg, Inconel 718, Co-Cr-Mo, maraging steel 1.2709.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Why is an inert atmosphere (Ar / N₂) maintained inside the SLM build chamber? Mention the typical O₂ threshold.*

**Model Answer:**
The build chamber is purged with argon or nitrogen to **(i) prevent oxidation** of reactive metals (Ti, Al) at temperatures above their melting points, **(ii) reduce plasma formation** above the melt pool that would otherwise scatter the laser beam, and **(iii) carry away spatter and metal vapour**. The typical O₂ threshold is maintained at **< 50 ppm** (often below 10 ppm for titanium processing).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **Note (KTU 2024 Pattern):** Each Part B question contains two sub-parts, typically (a) **7 marks** and (b) **7 marks**.

---

#### **Question A — [KTU University Exam – Model Question 2024 Scheme]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat schematic, explain the working principle of the **Selective Laser Melting (SLM)** process. Clearly label the laser source, scanner, recoater, build platform, and inert gas system.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Derive the expression for **Volumetric Energy Density (VED)** used in SLM. A Ti-6Al-4V part is being built with $P = 280\;\text{W}$, $v = 1200\;\text{mm/s}$, $h = 0.08\;\text{mm}$, and $t = 0.030\;\text{mm}$. Calculate the VED and state whether it is in the optimum range for Ti-6Al-4V (**60 – 120 J/mm³**).

**Model Solution to (a):**
The answer must describe the **nine-stage process** (CAD → slicing → chamber purging → powder deposition → laser melting → solidification → platform lowering → repetition → post-processing). The schematic should show a sealed chamber with: (1) a **fiber laser** at the top, (2) **galvanometer mirrors** deflecting the beam, (3) an **f-θ lens** focusing onto the powder bed, (4) a **recoater blade/roller** spreading powder from a hopper, (5) a **build platform** that lowers by one layer thickness after each scan, and (6) an **inert gas (Ar) inlet and exhaust** maintaining low O₂. The process repeats **N = H_part / t** times. The un-melted powder is later removed by brushing/vacuum; the part undergoes **stress relief** and optional **HIP** to close residual porosity.

**Model Solution to (b):**

*Derivation* (as in §3.1 above):
$$
E_v = \dfrac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t} \quad \left[\dfrac{\text{J}}{\text{mm}^3}\right]
$$

*Calculation:*
- Numerator: $P = 280\;\text{W} = 280\;\text{J/s}$
- Denominator: $v \cdot h \cdot t = 1200 \times 0.08 \times 0.030 = 2.88\;\text{mm}^3/\text{s}$
- Therefore:
$$
E_v = \dfrac{280}{2.88} = 97.22\;\text{J/mm}^3
$$

*Comment:* $97.22\;\text{J/mm}^3$ lies **within the 60–120 J/mm³ optimum window** for Ti-6Al-4V, predicting a high-density part with minimal lack-of-fusion or keyholing defects.

---

#### **Question B — [KTU University Exam – Model Question 2024 Scheme]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Compare **SLM** and **DMLS** (Direct Metal Laser Sintering) with respect to: melting mechanism, materials typically processed, density achievable, and need for post-processing HIP.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the effect of **four primary SLM process parameters** — laser power, scan speed, hatch spacing, and layer thickness — on the **relative density** and **build rate** of the part. Use a single combined expression to justify your answer.

**Model Solution to (a):**

| Feature | SLM | DMLS |
|---|---|---|
| Melting Mechanism | **Full melting** above Tm | Solid-state **sintering + partial melting** at grain boundaries |
| Laser Power Density | **Higher (10⁶ – 10⁷ W/cm²)** | Lower (10⁵ – 10⁶ W/cm²) |
| Typical Materials | Single-element metals, alloys (Ti, Al, 316L) | **Multi-material powders**, mixed metal systems (originally) |
| Achievable Density | **> 99.5 %** (often 99.9 %) | 90 – 95 % as-built |
| HIP Required? | **Optional but recommended** for fatigue-critical parts | **Often required** to close residual porosity |
| Surface Finish | Comparable (~Ra 5 – 20 µm) | Comparable |

**Model Solution to (b):**

Using the canonical expression:
$$
E_v = \dfrac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t}
$$

- **Laser Power $P$ ↑:** Increases $E_v$ → better fusion → higher density, but too high causes **keyhole porosity** (vaporization).
- **Scan Speed $v$ ↑:** Decreases $E_v$ → less energy per unit volume → risk of **lack-of-fusion porosity**; however, **faster build rate**.
- **Hatch Spacing $h$ ↑:** Decreases $E_v$ → **gaps between tracks** may remain unmelted; reduces build time.
- **Layer Thickness $t$ ↑:** Decreases $E_v$ → may cause **delamination**; but **fewer layers** reduce build time.

**Build rate** scales roughly as $R \approx v \cdot h \cdot t \cdot n_{lasers}$ (for a single laser) and is **inversely related to $E_v$** for a fixed $P$. Therefore, the engineer must trade off **density vs productivity** by tuning parameters to land inside the optimum $E_v$ window for the chosen alloy.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in SLM Questions (Board Examiner Insights):**
> 1. **Confusing SLM with DMLS** — Examiners deduct up to 2 marks if the student does *not* explicitly mention **"full melting above Tm"** vs *sintering*.
> 2. **Forgetting the inert atmosphere** — A 1–2 mark penalty applies when SLM is described without mentioning **Ar/N₂ purging and the O₂ threshold (~50 ppm)**.
> 3. **Unit errors in VED** — Always quote $E_v$ in **J/mm³**, **not J/m³ or J/cm³**. Substituting wrong units is a classic 1-mark loss.
> 4. **No mention of recoater / build platform** in the schematic — A labelled block diagram without these is marked incomplete.
> 5. **Ignoring post-processing** — Always end with **stress relief, optional HIP, support removal**, and surface finishing in the process description.
> 6. **Numerical mistakes** in the denominator multiplication $v \cdot h \cdot t$ — re-evaluate carefully; this is the #1 reason a 7/7 becomes 5/7.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — SLM (Module 3):**
> - **SLM = Powder Bed Fusion + Full Melting of Metal Powder** using a **fiber laser (~1070 nm)**.
> - Operates in a **sealed chamber** purged with **Ar or N₂** to keep O₂ **< 50 ppm**.
> - Build is **layer-by-layer**; each layer thickness **t = 20 – 100 µm**; each layer scanned with hatch spacing **h**.
> - **Key formula:** $E_v = P / (v \cdot h \cdot t)$ — units **J/mm³**.
> - **Linear energy density** $E_l = P / v$ — units **J/mm**.
> - **Typical materials:** Ti-6Al-4V, AlSi10Mg, 316L SS, Inconel 718, Co-Cr-Mo, maraging steel 1.2709.
> - **Achievable density > 99 %** — often comparable to wrought material after HIP.
> - **Optimum $E_v$ window depends on alloy:** 316L ≈ 60–120 J/mm³, Ti-6Al-4V ≈ 60–120 J/mm³.
> - **Two main defect families:** **Lack-of-fusion porosity** (low $E_v$) and **Keyhole porosity** (high $E_v$).
> - **Major sub-systems:** laser, galvanometer scanner, f-θ lens, recoater, build platform, inert gas system, oxygen sensor, chiller, filtration unit.
> - **Post-processing chain:** Depowder → Stress relief (450–700 °C) → Optional HIP → Support removal → Machining / Surface finishing.
> - **Advantages:** Geometry freedom, lightweight topology optimization, no tooling, short lead time for complex parts.
> - **Limitations:** High machine cost, limited build envelope, residual stress, anisotropic mechanical properties, support structures needed for overhangs, powder handling hazards.
> - **Industry verticals served:** Aerospace, biomedical, automotive (motorsport / EV), tooling, energy/turbines.
> - **Distinguish SLM from DMLS:** SLM uses higher power to **fully melt**; DMLS sinters and partially melts.
> - **Distinguish SLM from EBM (Electron Beam Melting):** SLM uses laser + Ar; EBM uses electron beam + vacuum, on conductive metals only.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electron Beam Melting (EBM)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET524 - ADDITIVE MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Electron Beam Melting (EBM)**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Electron Beam Melting (EBM)** is a **Powder Bed Fusion (PBF)** based additive manufacturing process that uses a high-velocity, computer-controlled **electron beam** as the thermal energy source to selectively melt metallic powder particles layer-by-layer inside a **high-vacuum environment** to fabricate near-net-shape, fully dense three-dimensional metal parts directly from a CAD model.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition to Memorize:** EBM is classified under the **ASTM/ISO 52900** category of *“Powder Bed Fusion – Electron Beam Melting”* and is one of the two dominant metal AM technologies alongside Selective Laser Melting (SLM/DMLS).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **highly controlled lightning bolt trapped inside a vacuum chamber**, sweeping back and forth over a thin layer of fine metallic sand. Wherever the lightning touches, the sand grains instantly fuse into solid metal (like a tiny welding spark). Then a new layer of sand is spread, and the lightning strikes again — but this time over the cross-section of the next slice. Layer after layer, a solid 3D metal object grows out of the powder bed, much like a sculptor carving a statue by stacking infinitely thin metallic sheets.

- The **lightning** = high-energy electron beam
- The **sand** = metal powder bed
- The **vacuum chamber** = sealed glass dome
- The **layer-by-layer stacking** = additive manufacturing principle

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Value / Range |
|---|---|
| **Electron Beam Acceleration Voltage** | **30 kV – 60 kV** (typically 60 kV) |
| **Beam Power** | **700 W – 15 kW** (commonly 3 kW) |
| **Beam Spot Diameter** | **100 µm – 200 µm** |
| **Vacuum Pressure** | **10⁻⁴ to 10⁻⁵ mbar** (high vacuum) |
| **Build Chamber Temperature** | **600 °C – 1100 °C** (preheated) |
| **Layer Thickness** | **50 µm – 200 µm** |
| **Scan Speed** | Up to **10⁴ mm/s** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Industrial Reference:** The pioneering commercial EBM system is the **Arcam Q20plus** (now under GE Additive), which is widely used to manufacture orthopedic implants (hip cups) and aerospace Ti-6Al-4V components.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Working Principle (Why and How)

EBM operates on the **principle of kinetic-to-thermal energy conversion**. A tungsten filament heated to ~2500 °C emits electrons via **thermionic emission**. These electrons are accelerated by a high-voltage anode and focused into a coherent beam by **electromagnetic lenses**. The beam is steered using **electromagnetic deflection coils** under computer control to scan the powder bed.

- **Why a vacuum?** The vacuum prevents electron scattering by air molecules, eliminates oxidation of reactive metals (titanium), and allows high beam currents without plasma formation.
- **Why a high scan speed?** It distributes the energy evenly, preheats surrounding powder (sintering), reduces smoke/plume formation, and improves build stability.
- **Why multiple beam passes?** EBM uses a **multi-melt strategy** — first a low-power preheat pass that lightly sinters the powder, followed by high-power contour and melt passes. This reduces thermal gradients and residual stresses.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Process Logic

1. **CAD Slicing:** The 3D model is sliced into 2D layers (50–200 µm thick).
2. **Vacuum Sealing:** The build chamber is evacuated to ~10⁻⁵ mbar.
3. **Preheating Powder Bed:** The build plate is preheated to 600–700 °C (for Ti-6Al-4V) using multiple low-power beam sweeps.
4. **Powder Deposition:** A rake/roller spreads a fresh layer of metal powder over the build platform.
5. **Selective Melting:** The electron beam melts the powder at locations defined by the slice data.
6. **Layer Lowering:** The build platform descends by one layer thickness.
7. **Repeat:** Steps 4–6 are repeated until the part is complete.
8. **Cooling & Powder Recovery:** The build cools in vacuum (with helium backfill) for 4–8 hours before part extraction.

### 2.3 System Architecture Components

| Component | Function |
|---|---|
| **Electron Gun (Cathode)** | Generates electrons via thermionic emission |
| **Anode & Accelerating Column** | Accelerates electrons to high velocity |
| **Electromagnetic Lenses** | Focus the electron beam to a fine spot |
| **Deflection Coils (X & Y)** | Steer the beam across the powder bed |
| **Build Platform** | Holds the part being manufactured |
| **Powder Hoppers & Rake** | Supply and spread fresh powder |
| **Vacuum Chamber** | Houses the entire process |
| **Cooling System (Water)** | Dissipates heat from electromagnetic coils |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following equations are **must-memorize** for ESE and continuous assessment in PEMET524.

| Formula | Expression | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Electron Beam Power | $P = V \times I$ | Where V is accelerating voltage, I is beam current | Watts (W) |
| Kinetic Energy of Electron | $E_k = \frac{1}{2} m_e v^2 = eV$ | Energy gained by one electron | Joules (J) |
| Electron Velocity | $v = \sqrt{\dfrac{2eV}{m_e}}$ | Non-relativistic beam velocity | m/s |
| Volume Energy Density (VED) | $\text{VED} = \dfrac{P}{v \times h \times t}$ | Energy delivered per unit volume | J/mm³ |
| Linear Energy Density | $\text{LED} = \dfrac{P}{v}$ | Energy per unit length of scan | J/mm |
| Heat Affected Zone Depth | $d_{HAZ} \approx \sqrt{\dfrac{4\alpha t_{int}}{\pi}}$ | Where α = thermal diffusivity, t_int = interaction time | m |
| Beam Spot Area | $A_{spot} = \pi r_{spot}^2$ | Area of focused beam | mm² |
| Power Density | $\phi = \dfrac{P}{A_{spot}}$ | Power per unit area | W/mm² |
| Cooling Time (Newtonian) | $t_c = \dfrac{h_s (T_m - T_0)}{q^2}$ | Approximate solidification time | s |
| Marangoni Number | $Ma = -\dfrac{d\gamma}{dT} \cdot \dfrac{L \cdot \Delta T}{\mu \alpha}$ | Drives melt-pool convection | dimensionless |

> [!WARNING]
> **LaTeX Table Safety:** All absolute values in this sheet use $\vert x \vert$ or \mid form to prevent markdown parsing errors. Do **NOT** use the vertical pipe `|` character inside table cells.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace (Ti-6Al-4V brackets):** Lightweight topology-optimized structural components for Boeing 787 and Airbus A350.
- **Medical Implants:** Customized orthopedic hip cups and spinal cages (biocompatible Ti alloys).
- **Automotive:** Conformal cooling inserts for injection molds.
- **Defense:** Complex internal lattice structures for armor and propulsion.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Electron Beam Power and Electron Velocity

**Statement:** Show that the velocity of an electron in a 60 kV accelerating field of an EBM gun is approximately $1.45 \times 10^8$ m/s.

**Step 1 — Equate Kinetic Energy to Work Done by Electric Field:**

$$
E_k = W = eV
$$

where $e = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ C is the electron charge and $V$ is the accelerating potential difference in volts.

**Step 2 — Express Kinetic Energy in terms of Mass and Velocity:**

$$
\frac{1}{2} m_e v^2 = eV
$$

where $m_e = 9.109 \times 10^{-31}$ kg is the electron rest mass and $v$ is the electron velocity.

**Step 3 — Solve for Velocity:**

$$
v = \sqrt{\frac{2eV}{m_e}}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute Numerical Values (V = 60,000 V):**

$$
v = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 1.602 \times 10^{-19} \times 60000}{9.109 \times 10^{-31}}}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute Numerator:**

$$
2 \times 1.602 \times 10^{-19} \times 60000 = 1.9224 \times 10^{-14} \text{ J}
$$

**Step 6 — Divide by Electron Mass:**

$$
\frac{1.9224 \times 10^{-14}}{9.109 \times 10^{-31}} = 2.1104 \times 10^{16} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}^2
$$

**Step 7 — Take Square Root:**

$$
v = \sqrt{2.1104 \times 10^{16}} \approx 1.452 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Result:**

$$
\boxed{v \approx 1.45 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is about **48% of the speed of light** ($3 \times 10^8$ m/s). At this speed, relativistic effects are small but non-negligible for precise energy calculations.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Volume Energy Density (VED) in EBM

**Statement:** Derive the expression for the volume energy density delivered to a powder bed during EBM.

**Step 1 — Define Energy Delivered per Scan Line:**

When the electron beam of power $P$ (W) scans a length $L$ (mm) at scan speed $v$ (mm/s), the time taken is:

$$
t_{scan} = \frac{L}{v}
$$

**Step 2 — Energy Delivered:**

$$
E = P \times t_{scan} = P \times \frac{L}{v} = \text{LED} \times L
$$

This defines the **Linear Energy Density** LED $= P/v$ in J/mm.

**Step 3 — Volume of Material Melted per Scan:**

The beam melts a track of cross-sectional area equal to hatch spacing $h$ × layer thickness $t$:

$$
A_{track} = h \times t
$$

**Step 4 — Volume Melted per Scan Length:**

$$
V_{track} = L \times h \times t
$$

**Step 5 — Energy Density (Energy per Unit Volume):**

$$
\text{VED} = \frac{E}{V_{track}} = \frac{P \times L / v}{L \times h \times t}
$$

**Step 6 — Cancel Common Terms:**

$$
\text{VED} = \frac{P}{v \times h \times t}
$$

**Result:**

$$
\boxed{\text{VED} = \frac{P}{v \times h \times t} \;\; \left[ \frac{\text{J}}{\text{mm}^3} \right]}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Memory Trick:** **P over VHT** — Power over (velocity × hatch × thickness). For Ti-6Al-4V EBM, the optimum VED range is **40 – 80 J/mm³**.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Power Density at the Beam Spot

**Statement:** Find the power density of an EBM system operating at 3 kW with a beam spot diameter of 200 µm.

**Step 1 — Identify Given Values:**

- Power $P = 3000$ W
- Spot diameter $d = 200 \text{ µm} = 0.2$ mm
- Spot radius $r = 0.1$ mm

**Step 2 — Calculate Spot Area:**

$$
A_{spot} = \pi r^2 = \pi (0.1)^2 = 0.0314 \text{ mm}^2
$$

**Step 3 — Apply Power Density Formula:**

$$
\phi = \frac{P}{A_{spot}} = \frac{3000}{0.0314}
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate:**

$$
\phi \approx 9.55 \times 10^4 \text{ W/mm}^2
$$

**Result:**

$$
\boxed{\phi \approx 9.55 \times 10^4 \text{ W/mm}^2 \approx 95.5 \text{ MW/m}^2}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is orders of magnitude higher than a typical household kettle (~1000 W/m²) — concentrated energy is the key to instant localized melting.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: EBM vs. SLM (Laser Melting)

| Parameter | EBM | SLM (Laser) |
|---|---|---|
| Energy Source | Electron beam | Fiber/CO₂ laser |
| Vacuum Required | Yes (10⁻⁵ mbar) | No (inert gas) |
| Power Output | 1 – 15 kW | 200 W – 1 kW |
| Preheating | Mandatory (~700 °C) | Optional |
| Residual Stress | Low (hot bed) | High (cold bed) |
| Scan Speed | Very high | Moderate |
| Surface Finish | Rougher (~30 µm) | Smoother (~10 µm) |
| Material Range | Conductive metals | Wide range |
| Part Cost | High machine cost | Lower entry cost |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an EBM System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[CAD Model Input] --> B[Slicer Software]
    B --> C[Slice Data and Scan Path]
    C --> D[Beam Control Computer]
    D --> E[Deflection Coils X and Y]
    D --> F[Focusing Electromagnetic Lens]
    E --> G[Electron Beam in Vacuum]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Metal Powder Bed]
    H --> I[Selective Melting Track]
    I --> J[Build Platform and Part]
    K[Powder Hopper] --> L[Rake and Roller System]
    L --> H
    M[Vacuum Pump Array] --> N[Vacuum Chamber]
    N --> G
    H --> N
    J --> O[Layer Lowering Actuator]
    O --> J
    P[Heater System] --> J
    Q[Cooling Water Circuit] --> E
    Q --> F
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The CAD model flows down through software → electronics → beam generation → powder interaction → part growth. Powder feed, vacuum, heating, and cooling form the support subsystems.

---

### 4.2 Process Flow Diagram (Layer-by-Layer EBM Cycle)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Start: Load CAD and Slice] --> S2[Evacuate Build Chamber]
    S2 --> S3[Preheat Build Plate to Setpoint]
    S3 --> S4[Spread Fresh Powder Layer]
    S4 --> S5[Multi Pass Beam Scan: Preheat then Melt]
    S5 --> S6[Allow Local Solidification]
    S6 --> S7[Lower Build Platform by One Layer Thickness]
    S7 --> S8{Layer Count Less Than Total?}
    S8 -- Yes --> S4
    S8 -- No --> S9[Final Slow Cool Down with Helium]
    S9 --> S10[Open Chamber and Remove Part]
    S10 --> S11[Powder Recovery and Reuse]
    S11 --> S12[End of Build]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **loop (S4 → S8)** is the heart of EBM — every iteration adds exactly one cross-sectional slice to the part.

---

### 4.3 Electron Beam Generation and Steering Subsystem

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    G1[Tungsten Filament Heated to 2500C] --> G2[Thermionic Electron Emission]
    G2 --> G3[Accelerating Anode at 60kV]
    G3 --> G4[Beam Shaping Aperture]
    G4 --> G5[Magnetic Focusing Lens]
    G5 --> G6[Deflection Coil Assembly]
    G6 --> G7[Focused Electron Beam on Powder Bed]
    H1[High Voltage Power Supply 60kV] --> G3
    H2[Lens Current Controller] --> G5
    H3[Sweep Waveform Generator] --> G6
    H4[Vacuum System 10 to the minus 5 mbar] --> G1
    H4 --> G3
    H4 --> G5
    H4 --> G6
    H4 --> G7
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked “Why vacuum is essential in EBM?”, link the vacuum to all four subsystems — without vacuum, the electrons would collide with gas molecules and scatter, ruining the beam.

---

### 4.4 Functional Input-Output Mapping Table

| Subsystem | Input | Output | Controlled Variable |
|---|---|---|---|
| Electron Gun | Electrical current to filament | Stream of electrons | Beam current |
| Accelerating Column | High voltage DC | Accelerated electron beam | Kinetic energy |
| Focusing Lens | Lens coil current | Focused beam spot | Spot diameter |
| Deflection Coils | XY scan vectors | Steered beam path | Scan trajectory |
| Powder Spreader | Powder from hopper | Uniform powder layer | Layer thickness |
| Build Platform | Stepper motor pulses | Vertical translation | Z-axis position |
| Vacuum System | Pump ON signal | Low-pressure chamber | Pressure in mbar |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper Style]**

**Q1. Define Electron Beam Melting (EBM). State any two advantages of EBM over Selective Laser Melting (SLM).**

**Model Answer:**

Electron Beam Melting is a powder bed fusion additive manufacturing process in which a focused high-energy electron beam, generated in a high-vacuum chamber, selectively melts metal powder particles layer-by-layer to build a 3D part from CAD data.

**Two Advantages over SLM:**

1. **Higher build plate temperature (600–700 °C)** — reduces residual stress and eliminates post-process stress-relief heat treatment.
2. **Higher scan speeds and beam power** — enables faster build times for large parts (e.g., full hip cups in ~3–4 hours).
3. **No oxidation issues** — high vacuum prevents oxygen contamination in reactive metals like titanium.

*(Any two valid points accepted. 1 mark for definition, 1 mark per advantage.)*

---

**Q2. Why is the build chamber evacuated to high vacuum in the EBM process? List any two consequences if vacuum is not maintained.**

**Model Answer:**

Vacuum is essential to:

1. **Prevent electron scattering** by gas molecules (otherwise the beam would defocus and lose energy).
2. **Eliminate oxidation** of reactive metal powders (especially Ti, Al).
3. **Avoid plasma discharge and arcing** at high beam voltages.

**Consequences of poor vacuum:**

- Beam becomes unstable; melting is non-uniform → part porosity and poor mechanical strength.
- Oxidation of titanium → brittle, oxygen-rich phases form → part failure.
- High voltage arcing damages the electron gun.

*(Full 3 marks for complete answer.)*

---

### 5.2 PART B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each – Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Module 3 Pattern]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the working principle and main components of an Electron Beam Melting (EBM) system. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the expression for **Volume Energy Density (VED)** in EBM. An EBM machine is operated at beam power 3 kW, scan speed 1000 mm/s, hatch spacing 0.2 mm, and layer thickness 0.1 mm. Calculate the VED and comment on the result. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Working Principle and Components (7 marks):**

**Principle:** EBM uses a high-velocity electron beam, generated by **thermionic emission** from a heated tungsten filament, accelerated through a high-voltage anode (typically 60 kV), focused by **electromagnetic lenses**, and steered by **electromagnetic deflection coils** to selectively melt metal powder inside a vacuum chamber. The process is repeated layer by layer to build the part.

**Main Components (5 key components – 1 mark each + 2 for diagram):**

1. **Electron Gun** – Tungsten filament emits electrons when heated.
2. **Accelerating Anode** – Provides 30–60 kV to accelerate electrons.
3. **Electromagnetic Focusing Lens** – Focuses the beam to a 100–200 µm spot.
4. **Deflection Coils (X & Y)** – Steer the beam according to slice data.
5. **Vacuum Chamber with Build Platform and Powder Hoppers** – Houses the build, evacuated to 10⁻⁵ mbar.

*(Use the Mermaid block diagram from Section 4.1 to illustrate.)*

---

**(b) Derivation of VED + Numerical Problem (7 marks):**

**Derivation (5 marks):**

Energy delivered during one scan over length $L$ at scan speed $v$:

$$
E = P \times t_{scan} = P \times \frac{L}{v}
$$

Volume melted per scan = $L \times h \times t$

Hence, Volume Energy Density:

$$
\text{VED} = \frac{P}{v \times h \times t}
$$

**Numerical Calculation (2 marks):**

Given: $P = 3000$ W, $v = 1000$ mm/s, $h = 0.2$ mm, $t = 0.1$ mm.

$$
\text{VED} = \frac{3000}{1000 \times 0.2 \times 0.1} = \frac{3000}{20} = 150 \text{ J/mm}^3
$$

**Comment:** A VED of 150 J/mm³ is **higher than the recommended 40–80 J/mm³** for Ti-6Al-4V. This suggests **over-melting** and possible keyhole porosity or excessive evaporation. The user should reduce power to ~1.5 kW or increase scan speed to ~2000 mm/s to bring VED into the optimum range.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) – Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper Module 3]**

**(a)** List and explain the **advantages and limitations** of the EBM process. Compare EBM with **Selective Laser Melting (SLM)** in a tabular form with at least five parameters. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the role of **preheating the powder bed** in EBM. How does the **multi-pass scanning strategy** (preheat pass + melt pass) improve part quality? **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Advantages, Limitations, and Comparison (7 marks):**

**Advantages of EBM (any 3 for 3 marks):**

1. **High build speed** — large beam power and scan velocity.
2. **Low residual stress** — hot build platform (~700 °C) acts as a stress reliever.
3. **Vacuum prevents contamination** — ideal for reactive metals (Ti, Nb).
4. **High material density** — >99.5% theoretical density achievable.
5. **Reduced support structures** — sintered powder supports overhangs.

**Limitations of EBM (any 2 for 1 mark):**

1. Expensive equipment and high maintenance.
2. Limited to electrically conductive materials.
3. Rougher surface finish (~30 µm Ra) than SLM.
4. Long cooling time after build (4–8 hours).

**Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| Parameter | EBM | SLM |
|---|---|---|
| Energy source | Electron beam | Laser beam |
| Atmosphere | High vacuum | Inert gas (N₂/Ar) |
| Power | 1–15 kW | 200 W–1 kW |
| Bed temperature | 600–700 °C | ~200 °C |
| Surface roughness | ~30 µm | ~10 µm |

---

**(b) Role of Preheating and Multi-Pass Strategy (7 marks):**

**Preheating the Powder Bed (3.5 marks):**

- The build platform is preheated to **600–700 °C** (for Ti-6Al-4V) before the first layer is melted.
- This **sinters the surrounding powder** lightly, holding it in place and acting as a support for overhangs and thin features.
- Reduces **thermal gradients** between the melt pool and adjacent material → **lower residual stress** and less part distortion.
- Slows the cooling rate, allowing **columnar-to-equiaxed grain transition** in titanium alloys, improving ductility and fatigue life.

**Multi-Pass Scanning Strategy (3.5 marks):**

- **Pass 1 — Preheat Pass:** Low beam power (e.g., 100–500 W) scans the entire cross-section to lightly sinter the powder.
- **Pass 2 — Contour Pass:** Medium power outlines the part boundary.
- **Pass 3 — Melt Pass:** Full power (3 kW) melts the interior.

**Benefits:**

- The preheat pass **reduces smoke and powder spatter** during melting.
- Distributes heat more uniformly → **reduced porosity**.
- Improves **surface quality** and **dimensional accuracy**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner’s Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Do NOT confuse EBM with SLM** in vacuum questions — SLM uses inert gas, NOT vacuum. Easy 2-mark trap.
> - **Always write the units** of VED as J/mm³; many students forget units and lose 1 mark.
> - In the working principle, students often **miss the thermionic emission** explanation — that is a must-mention.
> - For comparison tables, **do not exceed 5–6 rows**; KTU valuation key penalizes irrelevant entries.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Final-day revision bullet list for EBM – Module 3:**

- **EBM = Powder Bed Fusion + Electron Beam + Vacuum.** Three keywords define the process.
- **ASTM/ISO 52900 classification:** Powder Bed Fusion → Energy Source = Electron Beam.
- **Filament material:** Tungsten (W), heated to ~2500 °C for **thermionic emission**.
- **Acceleration voltage:** 30–60 kV (mostly 60 kV). Beam power = $V \times I$.
- **Vacuum level:** 10⁻⁴ to 10⁻⁵ mbar. **Mandatory** for beam integrity.
- **Beam spot size:** 100–200 µm.
- **Build plate temperature:** 600–700 °C for Ti-6Al-4V.
- **Layer thickness:** 50–200 µm (thicker than SLM, leading to faster builds).
- **Key formula:** $\text{VED} = \dfrac{P}{v \times h \times t}$ (J/mm³). Optimum for Ti-6Al-4V = 40–80 J/mm³.
- **Multi-pass strategy:** Preheat → Contour → Melt (improves density and surface).
- **Material limit:** Only **electrically conductive** metals (Ti, Al, Cu, Co-Cr, stainless steel). Cannot process ceramics or polymers directly.
- **Commercial leader:** Arcam (GE Additive) — Arcam Q20plus.
- **Key applications:** Aerospace Ti brackets, orthopedic hip cups, conformal cooling inserts.
- **Key advantages:** Low residual stress, high build speed, vacuum purity, lattice structures.
- **Key limitations:** High cost, rough surface finish, conductive materials only, long cool-down.
- **EBM vs SLM mantra:** *EBM = Vacuum + Hot Bed + Fast; SLM = Inert Gas + Cold Bed + Slower.*

---

**End of Module 3 – Electron Beam Melting (EBM) Notes**
*Prepared in alignment with KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020) — Outcome-Based Education format.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Wire Arc Additive Manufacturing (WAAM)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET524 - ADDITIVE MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Wire Arc Additive Manufacturing (WAAM)**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Wire Arc Additive Manufacturing (WAAM)** is a directed energy deposition (DED) based additive manufacturing process that uses an electric arc as the heat source to melt and deposit successive layers of metal wire feedstock, thereby building near-net-shape three-dimensional metallic components layer by layer.

According to the **ASTM F2792** standard terminology, WAAM falls under the broad category of **Directed Energy Deposition (DED)** processes, where focused thermal energy (in this case, an electric arc) is used to fuse materials by melting them as they are deposited.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):**
> WAAM is specifically positioned in Module 3 because it represents the **highest deposition rate** class of metal additive manufacturing — capable of depositing **hundreds of grams to several kilograms of metal per hour**, making it suitable for **large-scale structural components** rather than intricate miniature parts.

The formal KTU definition reads as follows:

> *Wire Arc Additive Manufacturing (WAAM) is a layer-by-layer metal deposition process in which an electric arc generated between a continuously fed metallic wire electrode and the workpiece (or between two electrodes) acts as the focused heat source to melt the wire, forming a molten weld pool that solidifies into a metallurgically bonded bead. By translating the torch (or the substrate) along predefined toolpaths, overlapping weld beads are stacked to fabricate three-dimensional solid or hollow metallic structures directly from a CAD model.*

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine a **giant 3D pen that uses a hot metal wire instead of plastic**.

When you draw with a 3D pen, the plastic filament melts as it comes out, and immediately solidifies in the air, building a freeform shape as you move your hand. **WAAM is essentially the industrial, metallurgical version of this exact concept**, but with two crucial upgrades:

1. **The "heat"** is not a hot resistor element — it is a **welding arc** (10,000°C or more), powerful enough to melt steel, titanium, aluminum, and nickel alloys.
2. **The "pen"** is mounted on a **robotic arm** or a **gantry/CNC system** that moves with extreme precision (typically **0.1 mm to 1 mm** positioning accuracy) along computer-generated paths.

**Step-by-step analogy mapping:**

| 3D Pen (Toy) | Wire Arc Additive Manufacturing (Industrial) |
|---|---|
| Plastic filament spool | Metal wire spool (1.0 mm – 1.6 mm diameter) |
| Hot resistor tip | Electric arc (GMAW/GTAW/PAW) |
| Hand movement | 6-axis robot or CNC gantry |
| Plastic bead solidifies | Molten weld pool solidifies as a metal bead |
| Builds plastic shapes | Builds solid metal structural parts |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Intuition:** Unlike powder-based AM (SLM, DMLS) which has a very small melt pool (microns), WAAM's melt pool is **centimeters long** and **millimeters deep**. This is why WAAM parts are coarse, need post-machining, but can be built **10–100× faster** than powder bed systems.

---

## 1.3 Classification of WAAM Within the AM Family

WAAM is **not** a single process — it is an **umbrella term** for three arc-based variants, classified by the type of arc/welding process used:

1. **GMAW-based WAAM** (Gas Metal Arc Welding) — uses a consumable wire electrode with shielding gas. Most common industrial variant.
2. **GTAW-based WAAM** (Gas Tungsten Arc Welding) — uses a non-consumable tungsten electrode with separate wire feed. Highest quality welds.
3. **PAW-based WAAM** (Plasma Arc Welding) — uses a constricted arc (plasma jet) for higher energy concentration.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Aid for KTU Exam:** The acronym **"G-P-G"** — **G**MAW (most common, fastest), **P**AW (highest precision), **G**TAW (highest quality but slowest).

---

## 1.4 Key Physical & Process Metrics (Standard Values)

- **Wire diameters used:** **0.8 mm, 1.0 mm, 1.2 mm, 1.6 mm** (most common: **1.2 mm**)
- **Deposition rate:** **1–10 kg/hour** (for GMAW-WAAM), making it the highest-rate metal AM process
- **Arc temperature:** **6,000°C – 20,000°C** (depending on process variant)
- **Layer thickness:** **1 mm – 3 mm** per deposited layer
- **Bead width:** **5 mm – 15 mm** typical
- **Typical build envelope:** Up to **several meters** in length (limited only by the robot/gantry reach)
- **Material utilization efficiency:** **~90–98%** (very high, since wire is fully consumed)
- **Positioning accuracy:** **±0.1 mm to ±0.5 mm** (with industrial robots)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Do NOT confuse WAAM with **Wire-EDM** (Wire Electrical Discharge Machining). Wire-EDM is a **subtractive** process that cuts metal with a thin brass wire. WAAM is **additive** — it deposits metal using an arc.

---

## 1.5 Why WAAM Matters — Engineering Relevance

WAAM bridges the gap between **traditional manufacturing (forging, casting)** and **modern powder-based AM**:

- **Castings/Forgings** are great for bulk parts but wasteful for low-volume, custom geometries.
- **Powder AM (SLM, EBM)** offers precision but is slow, expensive, and limited in build size (typically < 400 mm cube).
- **WAAM** fills the niche: **large, custom, low-to-medium complexity metal parts** at high deposition rates and lower cost.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World WAAM Applications:**
> - Aerospace: **Titanium alloy brackets, rib structures, engine casings** (Airbus A350, Boeing, GE Aviation)
> - Defense: **Submarine hull sections, armored vehicle components**
> - Marine: **Propeller blades, large ship fittings**
> - Energy: **Wind turbine tower flanges, nuclear component repair**
> - Heavy industry: **Large gears, dies, moulds, repair of worn shafts**

---

## 1.6 Historical Context (For KTU Contextual Questions)

- **1920s–1970s:** Arc welding processes (GMAW, GTAW) were developed — but applied only to joining, not building freeform shapes.
- **1990s:** First academic research on **shape welding / rapid prototyping using welding arcs** at institutions like the University of Nottingham (UK) and Tsinghua University (China).
- **2000s–2010s:** Term "**WAAM**" formally coined; CRANFIELD University and **WAAM3D** (a spin-off, now a leading commercial supplier) popularized industrial WAAM systems.
- **2014 onwards:** Airbus began serious R&D for **large titanium structural parts** using WAAM.
- **2020s:** Increasing adoption in **shipbuilding, aerospace repair, and large-scale tooling**.

---

## 1.7 Summary Snapshot for Section 1

| Aspect | Value / Description |
|---|---|
| Process Family | Directed Energy Deposition (DED) |
| Heat Source | Electric Arc (GMAW, GTAW, or PAW) |
| Feedstock | Continuous metal wire (0.8–1.6 mm diameter) |
| Deposition Rate | 1–10 kg/hr |
| Typical Materials | Steel, Ti-6Al-4V, Al alloys, Inconel, Copper |
| Build Size | Large (up to several meters) |
| Surface Finish | Rough (Ra 200–500 µm) — needs machining |
| Dimensional Accuracy | ±0.1–0.5 mm |
| Material Efficiency | 90–98% |

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Working Principle of WAAM — Step-by-Step Operational Logic

WAAM operates on a deceptively simple principle: **an electric arc melts a continuously fed wire, and a robot moves the torch to deposit the molten metal in a precise pattern.** However, the underlying physics involves complex, coupled phenomena of **electrical, thermal, fluid dynamical, and metallurgical nature.**

### Step-by-Step Operational Sequence

**Step 1 — CAD Model Preparation**
The desired 3D part is designed in CAD software (e.g., SolidWorks, CATIA). It is then **sliced into 2D horizontal layers** using specialized WAAM slicing software (e.g., WAAM3D's *RoboHAAM*, or custom slicers). Each layer is converted into a **toolpath** — a sequence of coordinates that the robot will follow.

**Step 2 — Substrate Preparation**
A flat metal plate (called the **substrate** or **build plate**) is fixed to the worktable. It must be:
- Chemically cleaned (degreased, brushed)
- Mechanically flat (within 0.1 mm/m)
- Pre-heated (especially for titanium and aluminum to reduce residual stress)

**Step 3 — Arc Ignition and Wire Feeding**
The arc is struck between the **wire electrode tip** (in GMAW) or the **tungsten electrode** (in GTAW) and the substrate. The wire is fed continuously at a controlled **wire feed rate (WFR)**, typically **3–15 m/min**. The arc melts the wire tip, creating a **molten weld pool** on the substrate.

**Step 4 — Shielding Gas Activation**
An inert or semi-inert shielding gas (**Argon, Helium, or Ar/CO₂ mixtures**) is delivered through the torch nozzle to:
- Protect the molten metal from atmospheric contamination (oxygen, nitrogen, hydrogen)
- Stabilize the arc
- Affect the bead shape

**Step 5 — Bead Deposition (Single Bead)**
The molten wire material transfers across the arc into the weld pool. As the torch (or substrate) moves at a controlled **travel speed (TS)**, the weld pool **solidifies behind the arc**, leaving a **continuous solidified bead** of metal on the substrate.

**Step 6 — Layer Stacking**
After one bead is completed, the torch is raised vertically by the **layer thickness (Z-increment)**, and the next bead is deposited adjacent to or on top of the previous one. This continues until the full 3D shape is built.

**Step 7 — Post-Processing**
The as-deposited WAAM part has:
- **Rough surface** (Ra ~200–500 µm) → requires **CNC machining** to achieve final tolerances
- **Residual stresses** → may require **heat treatment** (stress relief annealing) or **Hot Isostatic Pressing (HIP)**
- **Microstructural anisotropy** → due to directional solidification

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why "Why" Matters in WAAM:**
> Every parameter (current, voltage, travel speed) is interconnected. Increasing current → more heat → wider, deeper bead → more dilution with substrate → coarser grain structure → lower mechanical strength. This is why parameter optimization is a major research area.

---

## 2.2 The Three Main WAAM Variants — Detailed Comparison

### 2.2.1 GMAW-Based WAAM (Gas Metal Arc WAAM)

- **How it works:** A consumable wire is fed through a welding gun. The arc forms between the **wire tip** and the workpiece. The wire itself is the electrode — it melts and becomes the deposited material.
- **Synonyms:** MIG-WAAM, MAG-WAAM (depending on shielding gas)
- **Deposition rate:** **3–10 kg/hr** (highest of all WAAM variants)
- **Shielding gas:** Argon, CO₂, or Ar/CO₂ mixtures
- **Wire types:** Solid wire, metal-cored wire, flux-cored wire
- **Transfer modes:**
  - **Short-circuit transfer (dip transfer):** Low heat, suitable for thin sections
  - **Globular transfer:** Higher heat, larger droplets
  - **Spray transfer:** Highest deposition rate, requires high current (>200 A)
  - **Pulsed spray transfer:** Combines low heat input with high deposition — most common in WAAM
- **Advantages:** High speed, deep penetration, robust, suitable for steels and aluminum
- **Limitations:** More spatter, less precise bead control, requires good joint fit-up
- **Best for:** Steel parts, large structural components, Al-alloy builds

### 2.2.2 GTAW-Based WAAM (Gas Tungsten Arc WAAM)

- **How it works:** A **non-consumable tungsten electrode** creates the arc with the workpiece. A **separate wire feeder** pushes a filler wire into the weld pool from the side or front.
- **Synonyms:** TIG-WAAM
- **Deposition rate:** **0.5–2 kg/hr** (slowest, but highest quality)
- **Shielding gas:** Pure Argon (sometimes with H₂ or He additions)
- **Wire types:** Clean solid wires (no flux)
- **Advantages:**
  - **Excellent bead control** and surface finish
  - **Very low spatter**
  - Superior for **reactive metals** (titanium, niobium)
  - **High purity welds** — minimal contamination
- **Limitations:** Slow, requires skilled parameter tuning
- **Best for:** Titanium aerospace parts, reactive metals, high-integrity applications

### 2.2.3 PAW-Based WAAM (Plasma Arc WAAM)

- **How it works:** A constricting nozzle compresses the arc into a **high-velocity plasma jet**, increasing energy density. The wire is fed either into the plasma jet or separately.
- **Deposition rate:** **1–4 kg/hr** (intermediate)
- **Advantages:**
  - Higher energy concentration → **narrower, deeper beads**
  - Better arc stability
  - Suitable for **refractory metals**
- **Limitations:** More complex torch design, more expensive equipment
- **Best for:** Specialized applications — refractory metals, high-precision builds

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Quick Comparison Hook:** Use the mnemonic **"Q-S-P"** for the trade-off: **Q**uality (GTAW best) — **S**peed (GMAW best) — **P**recision (PAW best).

---

## 2.3 Critical Process Parameters in WAAM

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Range | Effect on WAAM |
|---|---|---|---|
| Welding Current | I | 100–300 A (GMAW); 80–200 A (GTAW) | Higher I → more heat → wider, deeper bead |
| Arc Voltage | V | 15–30 V (GMAW); 10–20 V (GTAW) | Higher V → wider bead, more dilution |
| Travel Speed | TS | 200–1000 mm/min | Higher TS → thinner bead, less heat per unit length |
| Wire Feed Rate | WFR | 3–15 m/min | Higher WFR → more material deposited |
| Wire Diameter | d | 0.8, 1.0, 1.2, 1.6 mm | Affects current range and deposition rate |
| Shielding Gas Flow | Q | 10–25 L/min | Too low → porosity; too high → turbulence |
| Layer Height (Z-step) | h | 1–3 mm | Must match bead height to avoid collision |
| Inter-pass Temperature | T_ip | 50–200 °C | Controls cooling rate and microstructure |
| Contact Tip-to-Work Distance (CTWD) | L | 10–25 mm | Affects arc stability |
| Torch Angle | θ | 0°–45° (typical: 0° or 10°) | Affects bead shape and direction |

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — The "Must-Know" Equations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following formulas are **high-yield** for KTU 2024 Scheme university exams. Memorize them with units.

### 2.4.1 Heat Input (HI) — Most Important Formula

The **heat input** per unit length of weld is the single most important parameter in WAAM. It controls bead geometry, microstructure, and residual stress.

$$
HI = \frac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{TS \cdot 1000} \quad \text{[kJ/mm]}
$$

Where:
- $HI$ = Heat input in **kJ/mm**
- $\eta$ = Arc efficiency / thermal efficiency factor (dimensionless, **0.6–0.9** depending on process)
  - GMAW: $\eta \approx 0.7$ to $0.85$
  - GTAW: $\eta \approx 0.5$ to $0.7$
  - PAW: $\eta \approx 0.55$ to $0.75$
- $V$ = Arc voltage in **Volts (V)**
- $I$ = Welding current in **Amperes (A)**
- $TS$ = Travel speed in **mm/min**
- The factor $1000$ converts J/mm to kJ/mm

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Heat input determines the **HAZ (Heat Affected Zone) size**, **grain coarsening**, and **distortion**. For titanium WAAM, HI must be kept low (e.g., $< 0.6$ kJ/mm) to avoid excessive grain growth that embrittles the part.

### 2.4.2 Deposition Rate (DR)

$$
DR = \frac{\pi \cdot d^2 \cdot WFR}{4} \cdot \rho \quad \text{[kg/hr]}
$$

Where:
- $DR$ = Deposition rate in **kg/hr**
- $d$ = Wire diameter in **mm**
- $WFR$ = Wire feed rate in **mm/min** (must be unit-converted if needed)
- $\rho$ = Density of the wire material in **kg/mm³** (e.g., steel ≈ $7.85 \times 10^{-6}$ kg/mm³)

> Alternatively, a **mass-based** deposition rate is:
> $$DR = WFR \cdot A_{wire} \cdot \rho \cdot 60$$ where $A_{wire} = \pi d^2/4$

### 2.4.3 Volumetric Deposition Rate (VDR)

$$
VDR = \frac{\pi \cdot d^2 \cdot WFR}{4} \quad \text{[mm}^3/\text{min]}
$$

### 2.4.4 Specific Energy / Energy Density (E)

Energy delivered per unit volume of deposited material:

$$
E = \frac{V \cdot I}{TS \cdot A_{bead}} \quad \text{[J/mm}^3\text{]}
$$

Where $A_{bead}$ is the cross-sectional area of the deposited bead (mm²).

For a roughly rectangular bead of width $w$ and height $h$:

$$
E = \frac{V \cdot I}{TS \cdot w \cdot h} \quad \text{[J/mm}^3\text{]}
$$

### 2.4.5 Bead Width (Empirical — for GMAW-WAAM)

$$
w = a + b \cdot \left(\frac{V \cdot I}{TS}\right)
$$

Where $a$ and $b$ are empirical constants (process- and material-specific).

### 2.4.6 Dilution Ratio

$$
D\% = \frac{A_{substrate\,melted}}{A_{substrate\,melted} + A_{wire\,deposited}} \times 100
$$

This is the **percentage of substrate material mixed into the weld bead**. For WAAM, dilution in the first layer may be 20–40%; in subsequent layers, it approaches **0%** (no further substrate melting).

### 2.4.7 Cooling Time (t₈/₅) — For Steel

For carbon/mild steel, the cooling time between 800°C and 500°C is critical for microstructure:

$$
t_{8/5} = \frac{(HI/k)^2}{T_{ip} - T_0} \cdot F
$$

Where:
- $k$ = thermal conductivity of base material
- $T_{ip}$ = inter-pass temperature
- $T_0$ = initial plate temperature
- $F$ = shape factor (3D: 0.67 for thick plate)

> [!NOTE]
> For KTU 2024 numerical questions, the **heat input formula** is the **most frequently tested** formula. Practice its unit conversions carefully: $V$ in V, $I$ in A, $TS$ in mm/min → result in J/mm, then divide by 1000 for kJ/mm.

### 2.4.8 Layer Time (Build Time per Layer)

$$
t_{layer} = \frac{L_{path}}{TS} \quad \text{[min]}
$$

Where $L_{path}$ is the total bead path length per layer (mm).

### 2.4.9 Total Build Time (Approximate)

$$
T_{total} = N_{layers} \cdot t_{layer} = N_{layers} \cdot \frac{L_{path}}{TS}
$$

Plus inter-pass dwell time for cooling.

### 2.4.10 Total Mass of Deposited Metal

$$
M_{total} = V_{part} \cdot \rho \cdot \frac{1}{\eta_{mat}}
$$

Where $V_{part}$ is the part volume, $\rho$ is material density, and $\eta_{mat}$ is the material utilization efficiency (typically 0.90–0.98 for WAAM).

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility of WAAM Equations

| Formula | Used in Engineering For |
|---|---|
| Heat Input (HI) | Selecting parameters to avoid HAZ embrittlement, controlling distortion in aerospace Ti-6Al-4V parts |
| Deposition Rate (DR) | Estimating production time and cost for a quote |
| Energy Density (E) | Comparing WAAM with laser DED and electron beam AM |
| Cooling Time (t₈/₅) | Predicting hardness and microstructure in carbon steel WAAM parts |
| Dilution Ratio | Verifying that the first layer has bonded properly to the substrate |
| Build Time | Production planning and shop-floor scheduling |

---

## 2.6 Heat Flow and Solidification in WAAM

The thermal cycle of a WAAM bead has three critical phases:

1. **Heating phase** (arc on): Temperature rises from substrate temperature to peak (above melting point, ~1500°C for steel).
2. **Cooling phase** (arc moves away): Rapid cooling at rates of **100–1000 °C/s**, depending on heat input and substrate thickness.
3. **Reheating phase** (next pass or next layer): When the next bead is deposited, the previous bead is reheated below its melting point — this is a **tempering / post-heating effect** unique to multi-layer WAAM.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Microstructural Consequence:** Multi-layer WAAM produces a **banded microstructure** — alternating regions of finer grains (reheated zones) and coarser columnar grains (original solidification). This **anisotropy** is a key design consideration in WAAM parts.

---

## 2.7 Defects in WAAM (For KTU Quality Questions)

| Defect | Cause | Prevention |
|---|---|---|
| Porosity | Gas entrapment, insufficient shielding | Optimize gas flow, clean wire |
| Lack of fusion | Low heat input, dirty surface | Increase HI, clean substrate |
| Cracking (solidification) | High residual stress, high carbon | Preheat, optimize parameters, use low-carbon wire |
| Layer delamination | Excessive inter-pass temperature | Control cooling time |
| Surface roughness | Droplet transfer, spatter | Use pulsed spray, post-machining |
| Distortion | Non-uniform cooling | Symmetric build strategy, fixturing |
| Inclusions | Slag (in flux-cored wire), oxides | Use clean solid wire, good shielding |

---

## 2.8 Comparative Snapshot — WAAM vs Other Metal AM Processes

| Feature | WAAM (GMAW) | Laser DED (LMD) | Powder Bed (SLM) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Heat Source | Arc | Laser | Laser |
| Feedstock | Wire | Powder | Powder |
| Deposition Rate | **3–10 kg/hr** | 0.1–0.5 kg/hr | 0.02–0.1 kg/hr |
| Build Volume | **Very Large** | Medium | Small (≤400 mm³) |
| Surface Finish | Poor (needs machining) | Fair | Good |
| Material Cost | Low (wire is cheap) | High (powder is expensive) | Very High |
| Accuracy | ±0.3 mm | ±0.1 mm | ±0.05 mm |
| Typical Materials | Steels, Al, Ti | Ni-alloys, Ti, tool steels | All metals including Ti, Al, CoCr |
| Best Use | Large structural parts | Medium complexity repair | Complex small parts |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Numerical Pattern:** Typically, 1–2 numerical problems appear in the 14-mark question, often involving **heat input calculation** or **deposition rate estimation**. Below are the canonical solved problems covering every formula variant.

---

## 3.1 Problem 1: Heat Input Calculation (Most Common KTU Numerical)

### Problem Statement
In a GMAW-WAAM process, the welding current is **220 A**, the arc voltage is **24 V**, and the torch travel speed is **400 mm/min**. The arc efficiency for GMAW is **0.85**.
Calculate the heat input delivered per unit length of the weld bead, in **kJ/mm**.

### Given Data
- $V = 24$ V
- $I = 220$ A
- $TS = 400$ mm/min
- $\eta = 0.85$ (arc efficiency for GMAW)

### Step 1 — Recall the Heat Input Formula

$$
HI = \frac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{TS \cdot 1000} \quad \text{[kJ/mm]}
$$

The factor **1000** is required to convert the result from J/mm to kJ/mm.

### Step 2 — Substitute the Numerical Values

$$
HI = \frac{0.85 \times 24 \times 220}{400 \times 1000}
$$

### Step 3 — Compute the Numerator

$$
0.85 \times 24 = 20.4
$$

$$
20.4 \times 220 = 4488
$$

### Step 4 — Compute the Denominator

$$
400 \times 1000 = 400000
$$

### Step 5 — Final Division

$$
HI = \frac{4488}{400000} = 0.01122 \text{ kJ/mm}
$$

### Step 6 — Express in Common Form (J/mm or kJ/mm)

$$
HI = 0.01122 \text{ kJ/mm} = 11.22 \text{ J/mm}
$$

### Final Answer

$$
\boxed{HI = 0.01122 \text{ kJ/mm} \approx 11.22 \text{ J/mm}}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner Tip:** If you forget the 1000 factor, you will get **11.22 kJ/mm** — this is wrong. Always state units explicitly in every step.

### Engineering Interpretation

A heat input of **0.0112 kJ/mm (11.2 J/mm)** is typical for **steel WAAM**. For **titanium WAAM**, the acceptable upper limit is usually **0.4–0.6 kJ/mm (400–600 J/mm)** — much higher per kg basis due to titanium's lower density and thermal conductivity.

---

## 3.2 Problem 2: Deposition Rate Calculation

### Problem Statement
A GMAW-WAAM system uses a steel wire of diameter **1.2 mm** fed at a rate of **8 m/min**. The density of steel is **7.85 g/cm³**.
Calculate the **mass deposition rate** in **kg/hr**.

### Given Data
- $d = 1.2$ mm
- $WFR = 8$ m/min = **8000 mm/min**
- $\rho_{steel} = 7.85$ g/cm³ = **$7.85 \times 10^{-3}$ g/mm³** = **$7.85 \times 10^{-6}$ kg/mm³**

### Step 1 — Recall the Volumetric Deposition Rate Formula

$$
VDR = \frac{\pi \cdot d^2 \cdot WFR}{4} \quad \text{[mm}^3/\text{min]}
$$

### Step 2 — Substitute Values

$$
VDR = \frac{\pi \times (1.2)^2 \times 8000}{4}
$$

### Step 3 — Compute Step by Step

$$
(1.2)^2 = 1.44
$$

$$
\pi \times 1.44 = 4.5239
$$

$$
4.5239 \times 8000 = 36191
$$

$$
VDR = \frac{36191}{4} = 9047.79 \text{ mm}^3/\text{min}
$$

### Step 4 — Convert to Mass Deposition Rate

$$
DR = VDR \cdot \rho
$$

$$
DR = 9047.79 \text{ mm}^3/\text{min} \times 7.85 \times 10^{-6} \text{ kg/mm}^3
$$

$$
DR = 0.0710 \text{ kg/min}
$$

### Step 5 — Convert to kg/hr

$$
DR = 0.0710 \times 60 = 4.263 \text{ kg/hr}
$$

### Final Answer

$$
\boxed{DR \approx 4.26 \text{ kg/hr}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is a **typical deposition rate** for a 1.2 mm steel wire at moderate wire feed speed. Higher WFR (e.g., 12 m/min) and pulsed spray transfer can push this to **6–8 kg/hr**.

---

## 3.3 Problem 3: Build Time Estimation

### Problem Statement
A WAAM part requires **200 layers** to build. Each layer has a total toolpath length of **1500 mm**. The travel speed is **500 mm/min**, and an additional **15 seconds** of inter-pass cooling is required per layer.
Calculate the **total build time** in hours.

### Given Data
- $N_{layers} = 200$
- $L_{path} = 1500$ mm per layer
- $TS = 500$ mm/min
- $t_{cool} = 15$ s per layer

### Step 1 — Time per Layer (Deposition Time)

$$
t_{deposition} = \frac{L_{path}}{TS} = \frac{1500}{500} = 3 \text{ min per layer}
$$

### Step 2 — Total Inter-Pass Cooling Time

$$
t_{cool,total} = 200 \times 15 \text{ s} = 3000 \text{ s} = 50 \text{ min}
$$

### Step 3 — Total Deposition Time

$$
t_{deposition,total} = 200 \times 3 = 600 \text{ min}
$$

### Step 4 — Total Build Time

$$
T_{total} = 600 + 50 = 650 \text{ min} = \frac{650}{60} \approx 10.83 \text{ hours}
$$

### Final Answer

$$
\boxed{T_{total} \approx 10.83 \text{ hours} \approx 10 \text{ hours } 50 \text{ minutes}}
$$

---

## 3.4 Problem 4: Energy Density Calculation

### Problem Statement
A WAAM bead has an approximate rectangular cross-section with **width 8 mm** and **height 2 mm**. The travel speed is **600 mm/min**, the voltage is **26 V**, and the current is **180 A**.
Calculate the **volumetric energy density** in J/mm³.

### Given Data
- $V = 26$ V
- $I = 180$ A
- $TS = 600$ mm/min = **10 mm/s**
- $w = 8$ mm
- $h = 2$ mm

### Step 1 — Recall the Energy Density Formula

$$
E = \frac{V \cdot I}{TS \cdot w \cdot h} \quad \text{[J/mm}^3\text{]}
$$

Note: $TS$ must be in **mm/s** for E to be in J/mm³ (because $V \times I$ gives W = J/s, and dividing by mm/s × mm² gives J/mm³).

### Step 2 — Substitute

$$
E = \frac{26 \times 180}{10 \times 8 \times 2}
$$

### Step 3 — Compute

$$
26 \times 180 = 4680 \text{ W (J/s)}
$$

$$
10 \times 8 \times 2 = 160 \text{ mm}^3/\text{s}
$$

$$
E = \frac{4680}{160} = 29.25 \text{ J/mm}^3
$$

### Final Answer

$$
\boxed{E = 29.25 \text{ J/mm}^3}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Reference:** For steel powder bed fusion (SLM), energy density is typically **40–100 J/mm³**. WAAM's energy density is **lower** (per unit volume) because the arc is less concentrated than a laser — but the total energy input per unit time is **much higher** due to the larger melt pool.

---

## 3.5 Problem 5: Multi-Layer Cooling Time (Advanced)

### Problem Statement
A WAAM system deposits layers at an inter-pass temperature of **150°C**. The heat input is **0.6 kJ/mm** and the plate thickness is **10 mm**. For a 3D heat flow, the shape factor is **0.67**. The thermal conductivity of the base material is **0.025 kJ/(mm·s·°C)**. Initial plate temperature is **25°C**.
Estimate the **t₈/₅ cooling time** (in seconds) for the first layer.

### Given Data
- $HI = 0.6$ kJ/mm
- $k = 0.025$ kJ/(mm·s·°C)  *(This is an approximate value for steel)*

> **Note:** The exact value of $k$ depends on units. For KTU-style problems, if a value is given or implied, use it directly.

### Step 1 — Use the Simplified Cooling Time Formula

For thick-plate 3D heat flow:

$$
t_{8/5} = \frac{HI^2}{4 \pi k^2 \rho c (T_{ip} - T_0)^2} \cdot F
$$

Or, the **Sharma-Buttworth type empirical form** used in many textbooks:

$$
t_{8/5} = \frac{(HI/k)^2}{2 \pi (T_{ip} - T_0)} \cdot F
$$

Let us use a simpler form often used in WAAM literature:

$$
t_{8/5} = \frac{HI^2 \cdot F}{k^2 (T_{ip} - T_0)^2} \cdot K
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Practical Reality:** The exact formula for $t_{8/5}$ in WAAM is complex and process-specific. In the KTU 2024 exam, you will **either** be given a simplified formula **or** a numerical answer is not required. Focus on understanding the **concept** of cooling time and its **microstructural implication** rather than memorizing a specific formula form.

### Conceptual Answer for KTU

The cooling time $t_{8/5}$ depends on:
- **Heat input** (higher HI → slower cooling)
- **Plate thickness** (thicker plate → heat sink effect → slower cooling)
- **Inter-pass temperature** (higher T_ip → smaller temperature difference → slower cooling)
- **Thermal diffusivity** of the material

For the given conditions (steel, HI = 0.6 kJ/mm, T_ip = 150°C, thick plate), typical $t_{8/5}$ values lie in the range of **5–20 seconds**, leading to a **ferritic-pearlitic microstructure** in mild steel.

---

## 3.6 Problem 6: Combined Heat Input and Deposition Time — Comprehensive

### Problem Statement
A Ti-6Al-4V aerospace bracket is being built using GTAW-WAAM. The required build is **80 mm tall**. The layer thickness is **1.5 mm**. Each layer requires **3 horizontal passes** of length **120 mm each**. The travel speed is **300 mm/min**, current is **140 A**, voltage is **14 V**, and arc efficiency for GTAW is **0.6**.
Calculate:
(a) Total number of layers
(b) Total heat input (kJ)
(c) Total deposition time (minutes)
(d) Approximate mass of titanium deposited (assume bead cross-section = 5 mm × 1.5 mm, density of Ti-6Al-4V = 4.43 g/cm³)

### Given Data
- $H_{part} = 80$ mm
- $h_{layer} = 1.5$ mm
- $n_{passes} = 3$ per layer
- $L_{pass} = 120$ mm
- $TS = 300$ mm/min
- $I = 140$ A
- $V = 14$ V
- $\eta = 0.6$
- $A_{bead} = 5 \times 1.5 = 7.5$ mm²
- $\rho_{Ti} = 4.43$ g/cm³ = $4.43 \times 10^{-3}$ g/mm³ = $4.43 \times 10^{-6}$ kg/mm³

### Part (a) — Total Layers

$$
N_{layers} = \frac{H_{part}}{h_{layer}} = \frac{80}{1.5} = 53.33
$$

Round up to **54 layers** (the last layer may be partial).

$$
\boxed{N_{layers} = 54 \text{ layers}}
$$

### Part (b) — Total Heat Input

**Step 1 — Heat input per mm**

$$
HI = \frac{0.6 \times 14 \times 140}{300 \times 1000} = \frac{1176}{300000} = 0.00392 \text{ kJ/mm}
$$

**Step 2 — Total path length**

$$
L_{total} = 54 \times 3 \times 120 = 19440 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 3 — Total heat input**

$$
HI_{total} = 0.00392 \times 19440 = 76.20 \text{ kJ}
$$

$$
\boxed{HI_{total} \approx 76.2 \text{ kJ}}
$$

### Part (c) — Total Deposition Time

$$
t_{total} = \frac{L_{total}}{TS} = \frac{19440}{300} = 64.8 \text{ min}
$$

$$
\boxed{t_{total} \approx 64.8 \text{ min} \approx 1 \text{ hour } 5 \text{ min}}
$$

### Part (d) — Mass of Titanium Deposited

**Step 1 — Total volume of beads**

$$
V_{beads} = L_{total} \times A_{bead} = 19440 \times 7.5 = 145800 \text{ mm}^3 = 145.8 \text{ cm}^3
$$

**Step 2 — Mass**

$$
M = V_{beads} \times \rho = 145.8 \times 4.43 = 645.89 \text{ g} \approx 0.646 \text{ kg}
$$

$$
\boxed{M \approx 0.65 \text{ kg of Ti-6Al-4V deposited}}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Cross-check for sanity:** At ~$0.65$ kg in 65 minutes, deposition rate is **0.6 kg/hr** — consistent with GTAW-WAAM (slower than GMAW).

---

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1: Define Wire Arc Additive Manufacturing (WAAM) and list its three main process variants. [3 Marks]
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]**

**Model Answer:**

**WAAM Definition:** Wire Arc Additive Manufacturing (WAAM) is a **Directed Energy Deposition (DED)** based additive manufacturing process in which an **electric arc** is used as the heat source to melt a **continuously fed metallic wire**, which is then deposited layer by layer to build a three-dimensional metallic component directly from a CAD model.

**Three Main Process Variants:**

1. **GMAW-based WAAM (Gas Metal Arc WAAM):** Uses a **consumable wire electrode**; the wire itself melts and becomes the deposit. Highest deposition rate (3–10 kg/hr). Most common variant.

2. **GTAW-based WAAM (Gas Tungsten Arc WAAM):** Uses a **non-consumable tungsten electrode**; a separate filler wire is fed into the weld pool. Slowest (0.5–2 kg/hr) but highest weld quality. Best for reactive metals like titanium.

3. **PAW-based WAAM (Plasma Arc WAAM):** Uses a **constricted plasma arc** produced by a copper nozzle; offers higher energy density and better arc stability. Deposition rate 1–4 kg/hr. Best for refractory metals.

---

### Question A2: Mention three advantages and two limitations of WAAM compared to powder-based AM processes. [3 Marks]
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Paper]**

**Model Answer:**

**Advantages of WAAM:**
1. **High deposition rate (1–10 kg/hr)** — 10 to 100 times faster than powder bed fusion processes.
2. **Lower material cost** — wire feedstock is significantly cheaper than equivalent metal powder.
3. **Large build volume** — limited only by robot reach, not by a powder bed chamber; can build parts several meters long.
4. **High material efficiency** — 90–98% of wire is deposited (minimal waste vs. machining).
5. **No support structures required** for most overhangs, unlike powder bed AM.

**Limitations of WAAM:**
1. **Poor surface finish** — As-deposited surface roughness is high (Ra 200–500 µm); post-machining is mandatory for functional surfaces.
2. **Lower dimensional accuracy** — ±0.3 mm typical, vs. ±0.05 mm for powder bed AM.
3. **Limited geometric complexity** — Cannot produce fine internal features, lattices, or thin walls like SLM/EBM.
4. **Residual stresses and distortion** — Due to high heat input; requires inter-pass cooling and sometimes post-weld heat treatment.
5. **Anisotropic mechanical properties** — Due to directional solidification and layer banding.

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern:** Part B questions have **internal choice** — you must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. Each question has sub-parts (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks. Solve all sub-parts completely.

---

### ⭐ QUESTION A (14 Marks)

#### Part (a): Explain the working principle of GMAW-based WAAM with a neat schematic. List the major process parameters. [7 Marks]
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**Model Answer:**

**Working Principle of GMAW-WAAM:**

GMAW-based Wire Arc Additive Manufacturing uses a **consumable metal wire** as both the electrode and the deposition material. The process operates as follows:

1. A **continuously fed metal wire** (typically 0.8–1.6 mm diameter) is pushed through a **welding gun (torch)** by a **wire feeder unit** at a controlled rate (3–15 m/min).

2. A **welding power source** supplies DC current (typically 100–300 A) to the system. When the wire tip approaches the workpiece, an **electric arc** is struck between the wire and the substrate.

3. The arc generates temperatures of **6,000–8,000°C** (or higher in spray transfer), which melts the wire tip continuously. The molten metal is transferred across the arc in the form of **droplets** (via short-circuit, globular, or spray transfer modes).

4. Simultaneously, a **shielding gas** (Argon, CO₂, or Ar/CO₂ mix) is delivered through the torch nozzle to **protect the molten weld pool from atmospheric contamination** (oxidation, nitrogen absorption).

5. The molten droplets land in a **weld pool** on the substrate. As the torch (or substrate) moves at a **controlled travel speed (TS)**, the weld pool **solidifies** behind the arc, leaving a continuous **metallic bead**.

6. After each bead/layer, the torch is **indexed upward by the layer height (Z-step)** using a **6-axis robot** or **CNC gantry**. The next bead is deposited on top of (or adjacent to) the previous one.

7. This **layer-by-layer stacking** continues until the full 3D part is built, then the arc is extinguished.

**Schematic (ASCII representation):**

```
   [SHIELDING GAS IN]  ──→  ┌──────────────┐
                              │  WELDING     │
   [WIRE SPOOL] ──→ [WIRE FEEDER] → │   TORCH       │ → ARC → [POOL] → [BEAD]
                              │              │
   [POWER SOURCE] ──────────→  └──────────────┘
                                       │
                                       ↓ (Z motion)
                              [BEAD ON SUBSTRATE]
```

**Major Process Parameters (must list at least 6):**

| S. No. | Parameter | Typical Range |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Welding Current (I) | 100–300 A |
| 2 | Arc Voltage (V) | 15–30 V |
| 3 | Travel Speed (TS) | 200–1000 mm/min |
| 4 | Wire Feed Rate (WFR) | 3–15 m/min |
| 5 | Shielding Gas Flow Rate | 10–25 L/min |
| 6 | Contact Tip to Work Distance (CTWD) | 10–25 mm |
| 7 | Inter-pass Temperature | 50–200 °C |
| 8 | Wire Diameter | 0.8–1.6 mm |
| 9 | Layer Height (Z-step) | 1–3 mm |
| 10 | Torch Angle | 0°–45° |

**Marking Scheme (as per KTU):**
- Working principle explained step-by-step: **3 marks**
- Schematic/diagram: **2 marks**
- Process parameters with typical values: **2 marks**

---

#### Part (b): A GMAW-WAAM process uses a current of 250 A and a voltage of 26 V. The travel speed is 500 mm/min. The arc efficiency is 0.80. Calculate the heat input in kJ/mm. If a steel wire of 1.2 mm diameter is fed at 10 m/min, calculate the deposition rate in kg/hr. Density of steel = 7.85 × 10⁻⁶ kg/mm³. [7 Marks]
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**Given Data:**
- $I = 250$ A
- $V = 26$ V
- $TS = 500$ mm/min
- $\eta = 0.80$
- $d = 1.2$ mm
- $WFR = 10$ m/min = 10,000 mm/min
- $\rho = 7.85 \times 10^{-6}$ kg/mm³

**(i) Heat Input Calculation [3 Marks]:**

**Step 1 — Formula:**
$$HI = \frac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{TS \cdot 1000} \text{ kJ/mm}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute:**
$$HI = \frac{0.80 \times 26 \times 250}{500 \times 1000}$$

**Step 3 — Numerator:** $0.80 \times 26 = 20.8$; $20.8 \times 250 = 5200$

**Step 4 — Denominator:** $500 \times 1000 = 500,000$

**Step 5 — Final:**
$$HI = \frac{5200}{500,000} = 0.0104 \text{ kJ/mm} = 10.4 \text{ J/mm}$$

**(ii) Deposition Rate Calculation [4 Marks]:**

**Step 1 — Formula:**
$$DR = \frac{\pi \cdot d^2 \cdot WFR}{4} \cdot \rho \text{ kg/hr}$$

**Step 2 — Volumetric Deposition Rate:**
$$VDR = \frac{\pi \times (1.2)^2 \times 10000}{4}$$
$$VDR = \frac{\pi \times 1.44 \times 10000}{4} = \frac{45238.93}{4} = 11309.73 \text{ mm}^3/\text{min}$$

**Step 3 — Mass Deposition Rate (per minute):**
$$DR_{min} = 11309.73 \times 7.85 \times 10^{-6} = 0.0888 \text{ kg/min}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to kg/hr:**
$$DR = 0.0888 \times 60 = 5.328 \text{ kg/hr}$$

**Final Answer:**
$$\boxed{HI = 0.0104 \text{ kJ/mm} \quad ; \quad DR \approx 5.33 \text{ kg/hr}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Valuation Mistakes to Avoid:**
> 1. Forgetting the **factor of 1000** in the heat input formula → leads to answers 1000× too large.
> 2. Forgetting to **convert WFR from m/min to mm/min** in the deposition rate.
> 3. Not writing the **units** at every step → KTU examiners deduct 0.5 mark per missing unit.
> 4. Using **$TS$ in mm/s** without converting $V \times I$ units correctly → wrong energy density.

---

### ⭐ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

#### Part (a): Compare GMAW-WAAM, GTAW-WAAM, and PAW-WAAM based on heat source, deposition rate, bead quality, and suitable materials. [7 Marks]
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Alternative Module 3 Question]**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | GMAW-WAAM | GTAW-WAAM | PAW-WAAM |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Heat Source** | Arc between consumable wire and workpiece | Arc between non-consumable tungsten electrode and workpiece | Constricted plasma arc (through copper nozzle) |
| **Electrode Type** | Consumable (the wire itself) | Non-consumable (separate wire fed in) | Non-consumable (separate wire fed in) |
| **Wire Feed** | Through the welding gun | Through a separate wire feeder | Through a separate wire feeder |
| **Deposition Rate** | **3–10 kg/hr** (highest) | 0.5–2 kg/hr (lowest) | 1–4 kg/hr (intermediate) |
| **Bead Quality** | Good (with pulsed spray) | Excellent (cleanest, most stable) | Very Good (narrow, deep) |
| **Spatter** | Moderate to high | Very low | Low |
| **Shielding Gas** | Ar, CO₂, or Ar/CO₂ mix | Pure Ar (sometimes Ar + H₂) | Ar + He (high energy) |
| **Arc Stability** | Good (pulsed) | Excellent | Excellent |
| **Heat Input Control** | Moderate (pulsed available) | Excellent (precise) | Excellent |
| **Suitable Materials** | Carbon steel, stainless steel, Al-alloys | Titanium, nickel alloys, stainless steel, reactive metals | Refractory metals, specialty alloys |
| **Equipment Cost** | Low to moderate | High (separate wire feeder) | Highest (specialized torch) |
| **Skill Required** | Moderate | High | High |
| **Best Application** | Large steel structural parts | Aerospace Ti components, nuclear | Refractory metal parts, precision |

**Key Conclusions:**
- GMAW-WAAM is preferred for **speed and cost** (large steel parts).
- GTAW-WAAM is preferred for **quality and reactive metals** (aerospace Ti).
- PAW-WAAM is preferred for **specialty applications** requiring high energy density.

**Marking Scheme:**
- Comparison table (or equivalent content): **5 marks**
- Conclusions/justification: **2 marks**

---

#### Part (b): Discuss the major challenges and limitations of WAAM. What post-processing steps are typically required for a WAAM part? [7 Marks]
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Alternative]**

**Model Answer:**

**Major Challenges and Limitations of WAAM:**

**1. Poor Surface Finish**
- As-deposited surface roughness is **Ra 200–500 µm**, which is unacceptable for most functional interfaces.
- Caused by: large droplet size, layer-by-layer stepping visible as "staircase" effect on curved surfaces, spatter deposition.
- **Consequence:** Every functional surface must be post-machined, adding cost and time.

**2. Residual Stresses and Distortion**
- High heat input per unit length causes **non-uniform thermal expansion and contraction**.
- For steel: residual stress can approach **yield strength** if not controlled.
- **Consequence:** Part warping, dimensional inaccuracy, possible cracking.

**3. Microstructural Anisotropy**
- Layered deposition creates **directional solidification** (columnar grains growing along build direction).
- **Mechanical properties vary with direction** — e.g., tensile strength parallel vs. perpendicular to layers can differ by **10–30%**.
- **Consequence:** Anisotropic fatigue and fracture behavior.

**4. Limited Geometric Complexity**
- WAAM cannot produce:
  - **Thin walls** below ~3 mm thickness
  - **Fine internal features** (holes, slots, lattices)
  - **Sharp internal corners**
  - **Overhangs > 45°** without support
- **Consequence:** Not suitable for intricate parts like turbine blades with internal cooling channels.

**5. Defect Susceptibility**
- Common defects: **porosity, lack of fusion, cracking, inclusions**.
- These require **non-destructive testing (NDT)** to detect.

**6. Process Parameter Sensitivity**
- Tens of parameters (current, voltage, TS, WFR, gas flow, inter-pass temp, etc.) interact in complex ways.
- **Consequence:** Long development time to qualify a new part (months of trial builds).

**7. Material Limitations**
- Not all alloys are available as wire (especially some Ni-superalloys, some Al-alloys).
- Reactive metals (Ti) require **glove-box or sealed chamber** to prevent oxygen contamination.

**8. Large Heat-Affected Zone (HAZ)**
- Especially in GMAW, the HAZ in the substrate can be several mm deep.
- **Consequence:** May degrade substrate properties; not suitable for thin or heat-sensitive substrates.

---

**Typical Post-Processing Steps for a WAAM Part:**

**Step 1 — Stress Relief Heat Treatment (SRHT)**
- Heat to **550–650°C** for steel, **600–700°C** for Ti, hold for 1–4 hours, then slow cool.
- **Purpose:** Reduce residual stresses by **50–80%** before machining.

**Step 2 — CNC Machining**
- **Rough machining:** Removes 80–90% of as-deposited material, achieving basic shape.
- **Finish machining:** Achieves **Ra < 1.6 µm** on functional surfaces, tight tolerances (IT7 grade).
- **Machining allowance:** Typically **2–5 mm** per surface.

**Step 3 — Hot Isostatic Pressing (HIP) — Optional, for critical parts**
- Subject part to **100–200 MPa** pressure at **0.7–0.9 × melting temperature** in an inert gas atmosphere.
- **Purpose:** Closes internal porosity, improves fatigue life by up to **10×**.
- **Used for:** Aerospace and medical Ti-6Al-4V parts.

**Step 4 — Surface Treatment**
- **Shot peening** — induces compressive residual stress, improves fatigue.
- **Polishing / grinding** — for high-finish functional surfaces.
- **Anodizing / coating** — for corrosion protection (especially Al-alloys).

**Step 5 — Non-Destructive Testing (NDT)**
- **Ultrasonic Testing (UT)** — detects internal defects, lack of fusion.
- **Radiographic Testing (RT)** — X-ray or CT scan for porosity and cracks.
- **Dye Penetrant Testing (DPT)** — for surface cracks.
- **Purpose:** Required for aerospace certification (e.g., **ASTM F3301** for AM parts).

**Step 6 — Dimensional Inspection**
- **CMM (Coordinate Measuring Machine)** — verifies geometric tolerances.
- **3D scanning** — compares actual part to CAD model.

**Marking Scheme:**
- Challenges and limitations (at least 5): **4 marks**
- Post-processing steps (at least 4): **3 marks**

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse** WAAM with **Wire EDM** — this is a **fatal mistake** costing up to **5 marks**.
> 2. **Always state the heat input value with units** in every numerical answer (kJ/mm, not just "HI").
> 3. **Do NOT skip the conversion steps** in numericals — KTU examiners award **step marks** for each calculation step, not just the final answer.
> 4. **Mention specific applications** (e.g., "Airbus A350 Ti brackets") in descriptive answers — this shows real-world awareness and earns **+1 mark** for depth.
> 5. **Avoid generic phrases** like "WAAM is good for metals" — instead write "WAAM is best suited for large Ti-6Al-4V aerospace structural components requiring high deposition rates."

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Use this section as the last 5-minute revision before entering the KTU exam hall.**

### ✅ Core Concepts (Must Know)
- [ ] WAAM = **Wire Arc Additive Manufacturing** = DED process using **electric arc** as heat source and **metal wire** as feedstock.
- [ ] Belongs to **Directed Energy Deposition (DED)** category of AM.
- [ ] Highest **deposition rate (1–10 kg/hr)** among all metal AM processes.

### ✅ Three Process Variants
- [ ] **GMAW-WAAM** — consumable wire, fastest (3–10 kg/hr), most common, best for steel & Al.
- [ ] **GTAW-WAAM** — non-consumable tungsten, slowest (0.5–2 kg/hr), highest quality, best for Ti.
- [ ] **PAW-WAAM** — constricted plasma, intermediate (1–4 kg/hr), best for refractory metals.

### ✅ Critical Formulas (Memorize with Units)
- [ ] **Heat Input:** $HI = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{TS \cdot 1000}$ kJ/mm
- [ ] **Deposition Rate:** $DR = \dfrac{\pi d^2 \cdot WFR}{4} \cdot \rho$ kg/hr
- [ ] **Energy Density:** $E = \dfrac{V \cdot I}{TS \cdot w \cdot h}$ J/mm³
- [ ] **Build Time:** $T_{total} = N_{layers} \cdot L_{path} / TS + \text{cooling time}$

### ✅ Process Parameters
