## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]**
**"Define medical additive manufacturing. List any four categories of medical applications where AM is used."**

**Model Answer:**

Medical additive manufacturing is the application of layer-by-layer fabrication processes to produce patient-specific medical devices, implants, anatomical models, and tissue constructs derived from digital medical imaging data (CT, MRI).

**Four categories:**
1. Patient-specific implants (cranial, dental, spinal, orthopedic)
2. Surgical guides and pre-operative anatomical models
3. Prosthetics and orthotics
4. Tissue engineering scaffolds and bioprinted constructs

> [!NOTE]
> Examiners award the **definition mark** and **1 mark per category** (any 4 × 0.5 = 2 marks + 1 mark for definition).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Supplementary]**
**"What is the role of Hounsfield Unit (HU) in medical AM? Mention typical HU ranges for bone and soft tissue."**

**Model Answer:**

The Hounsfield Unit (HU) is a normalized measure of radiodensity used in CT imaging, defined as:

$$
HU = 1000 \cdot \frac{\mu_x - \mu_{\text{water}}}{\mu_{\text{water}}}
$$

In medical AM, HU values are used during **image segmentation** to isolate the anatomical region of interest (e.g., bone) from surrounding tissues by applying a threshold range.

**Typical HU ranges:**
- **Cortical bone:** +400 to +3000 HU
- **Trabecular bone:** +100 to +400 HU
- **Soft tissue:** -200 to +200 HU
- **Water:** 0 HU
- **Air:** -1000 HU

This thresholding step is critical because an incorrect HU range either excludes vital geometry or includes unwanted tissue in the final STL.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Part (a)** Explain in detail the workflow for producing a **patient-specific cranial implant** using additive manufacturing, starting from CT scan to final implantation. (7 marks)

**Part (b)** Discuss the **biocompatibility requirements** for medical implants as per **ISO 10993**. List and briefly explain **any three tests** specified by the standard. (7 marks)

---

#### **Model Answer — Part (a) [7 Marks]**

The workflow for producing a patient-specific cranial implant consists of **five sequential stages**:

**Stage 1 — Medical Imaging (1 mark)**
The patient undergoes a high-resolution CT scan with slice thickness 0.5–1.0 mm and in-plane resolution of 0.5 mm. Output is in **DICOM** format, comprising 2D cross-sectional slices with Hounsfield Unit (HU) intensity data.

**Stage 2 — Segmentation and 3D Reconstruction (1.5 marks)**
Using software like **Materialise Mimics** or **3D Slicer**, the engineer applies HU thresholding (bone: +400 to +3000 HU) to isolate the cranial region. The 3D model is reconstructed using the "Calculate 3D" function and exported as an **STL** mesh.

**Stage 3 — CAD Modeling and Design Refinement (1.5 marks)**
In **Geomagic Studio** or **Meshmixer**, the missing bone region is reconstructed using hole-filling algorithms. The implant is offset inward by 0.3 mm to account for skin/scalp thickness. Fixation features (4–6 screw holes of Ø 2.5 mm) are added. Optional lattice (gyroid TPMS) structures may be added for osseointegration. The final design is exported as STEP.

**Stage 4 — Additive Manufacturing (1.5 marks)**
The chosen process is **Selective Laser Melting (SLM)** or **Electron Beam Melting (EBM)** with **Ti-6Al-4V ELI (Extra Low Interstitial)** powder. EBM parameters: layer thickness 0.07 mm, beam power 3000 W, scan speed 8000 mm/s, build temperature 700 °C. The build takes 6–12 hours depending on implant size. SLS with **PEEK** is an alternative for polymer implants.

**Stage 5 — Post-Processing and Sterilization (1.5 marks)**
- **Support removal** and bead-blasting with 110 µm alumina
- **Hot Isostatic Pressing (HIP)** at 920 °C, 100 MPa, 4 hours to eliminate internal porosity
- **Electropolishing** to improve surface finish (Ra < 5 µm)
- **Passivation** in nitric acid to enhance corrosion resistance
- **Sterilization** by autoclaving (121 °C, 15 psi, 20 min) or gamma irradiation (25 kGy)
- Final **dimensional verification** via 3D scanning and **CT inspection** to confirm internal soundness

---

#### **Model Answer — Part (b) [7 Marks]**

**Biocompatibility (Definition — 1 mark):**
Biocompatibility is the ability of a material to perform its desired function **without eliciting any undesirable local or systemic effects** in the patient, as defined by **ISO 10993-1**.

**Why ISO 10993 is required (1 mark):**
Before any medical device can be implanted in a patient, it must be tested for **cytotoxicity, sensitization, irritation, systemic toxicity, genotoxicity, and implantation reaction**. The standard provides a structured framework for these tests.

**Three key tests under ISO 10993 (5 marks — 1.66 each):**

**1. Cytotoxicity Test (ISO 10993-5)**
The material extract is placed in contact with a mouse fibroblast cell culture (L929 cells). After 24–72 hours, cell viability is assessed. A material is considered biocompatible if cell viability exceeds **70%** compared to the negative control. This test detects whether the material leaches toxic substances.

**2. Sensitization Test (ISO 10993-10)**
Performed using the **Guinea Pig Maximization Test (GPMT)** or **Local Lymph Node Assay (LLNA)**. The material extract is applied to the skin of test animals, and the immune/allergic response is observed. A material is non-sensitizing if no erythema or edema appears in the test group.

**3. Implantation Test (ISO 10993-6)**
The material is surgically implanted in animal tissue (rabbit muscle or bone) for a defined duration (4–26 weeks). Histological analysis evaluates the local tissue response: inflammation, necrosis, fibrosis, and capsule formation. A biocompatible material shows minimal fibrous capsule (< 30 µm thick) and no chronic inflammation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Additional tests (for reference, not required in answer):** Genotoxicity (ISO 10993-3), Systemic Toxicity (ISO 10993-11), Hemocompatibility (ISO 10993-4), and Irritation (ISO 10993-10).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper 1]**

**Part (a)** Describe the various **categories of bioprinting** (laser-assisted, inkjet, extrusion). Compare their advantages and limitations. (7 marks)

**Part (b)** Explain with examples the use of AM in **hearing aids, dental crowns, and prosthetics**. How has AM revolutionized these applications? (7 marks)

---

#### **Model Answer — Part (a) [7 Marks]**

Bioprinting is the additive manufacturing of **living tissue constructs** using **bio-inks** (cell-laden hydrogels). Three principal categories are recognized by the AM community:

**1. Inkjet Bioprinting (1.5 + 1 = 2.5 marks)**

Inkjet bioprinting uses **thermal or piezoelectric** actuators to eject **picoliter droplets** of bio-ink onto a substrate. Thermal inkjet uses a brief heating pulse (≤ 5 µs, < 300 °C local) to form a bubble that ejects the droplet, while piezoelectric inkjet uses voltage-induced deformation of a piezo crystal.

- **Advantages:** High resolution (50–300 µm droplet size), high speed, low cost, compatible with multiple cell types.
- **Limitations:** Low viscosity bio-inks (< 10 mPa·s) only, droplet clogging risk, limited material range, mechanical weakness of printed construct.
- **Example:** Boland's group at Clemson University printed vascular-like patterns using fibrin and thrombin.

**2. Extrusion Bioprinting (1.5 + 1 = 2.5 marks)**

Extrusion bioprinting uses a **pneumatic, piston, or screw-driven** mechanism to continuously extrude bio-ink through a nozzle (typically 100–500 µm diameter). The printhead moves in X-Y while the platform moves in Z.

- **Advantages:** Can print high-viscosity bio-inks (10⁴ to 10⁶ mPa·s), high cell density (10⁶–10⁸ cells/mL), suitable for thermoplastics, hydrogels, and cell-loaded materials.
- **Limitations:** Resolution limited to 100–200 µm, shear stress can damage cells, slower than inkjet.
- **Example:** Organovo's NovoGen bioprinter used extrusion to print liver and kidney tissue for drug testing.

**3. Laser-Assisted Bioprinting (LAB) (1.5 + 0.5 = 2 marks)**

LAB uses a **pulsed laser** (typically Nd:YAG, 1064 nm or UV 355 nm) focused through a transparent ribbon coated with a thin **energy-absorbing layer (gold or titanium)**. The laser vaporizes a small region, creating a bubble that propels a bio-ink droplet onto the receiving substrate.

- **Advantages:** Nozzle-free (no clogging), very high cell viability (> 95%), high resolution (single-cell level), compatible with high-viscosity bio-inks.
- **Limitations:** Slow, expensive, limited build volume, complex setup.
- **Example:** Poietis (France) uses LAB to bioprint skin grafts for burn patients; Wake Forest Institute used LAB to print skin cells directly onto wounds.

**Comparison Table (embedded in answer):**

| Parameter | Inkjet | Extrusion | Laser-Assisted |
|-----------|--------|-----------|---------------|
| Resolution | 50–300 µm | 100–500 µm | 5–50 µm (single cell) |
| Viscosity range | < 10 mPa·s | 10–10⁶ mPa·s | 1–300 mPa·s |
| Cell viability | 80–90% | 40–80% | > 95% |
| Speed | High | Medium | Low |
| Cost | Low | Medium | High |
| Nozzle | Yes (clog risk) | Yes (clog risk) | No |

---

#### **Model Answer — Part (b) [7 Marks]**

**1. Hearing Aids (2.5 marks)**
Hearing aids consist of three components: a microphone, an amplifier/speaker, and a **custom-molded shell** that fits inside the ear canal. Traditionally, shells were hand-crafted from acrylic, requiring 3–5 lab hours per unit and multiple patient visits.

**AM Revolution:** Today, **every hearing-aid shell worldwide is 3D-printed** using **Selective Laser Sintering (SLS)** with **PA12 (Polyamide 12)**. The process is:
- The patient's ear canal is scanned with a 3D laser otoscope.
- The STL is automatically generated and sent directly to the SLS machine.
- The shell is printed in 6–8 hours, with **± 0.05 mm** accuracy, no manual labor.
- The shell is dyed to match skin tone, electronics are inserted, and shipped.
- This has reduced turnaround from **2 weeks to 2 days** and is the **largest medical AM application by volume** globally.

**2. Dental Crowns and Bridges (2.5 marks)**
Dental crowns traditionally involved taking a physical impression, casting a model in gypsum, and hand-milling wax patterns — a labor-intensive process with inconsistent fit.

**AM Revolution:** Modern digital dentistry uses:
- **Intraoral scanners (IOS)** to capture 3D digital impressions of the prepared tooth.
- The STL is sent to a **DLP (Digital Light Processing) 3D printer** for a temporary crown (biocompatible resin, class IIa) or to a **SLM (Selective Laser Melting) machine** for permanent crowns/bridges in **Co-Cr alloy** or **ZrO₂ (zirconia)**.
- Multi-unit bridges (4–14 units) can be printed monolithically in a single build.
- **Advantage:** Excellent marginal fit (gap < 50 µm), reduced chair-time, no metal allergies (with zirconia), and **same-day crowns** in some clinics using chair-side DLP printers.
- Companies: **3M, Dentsply Sirona, Align Technology (Invisalign)**.

**3. Prosthetics (2 marks)**
A **prosthesis** is an artificial device that replaces a missing body part. Traditional prosthetics are expensive (₹50,000 – ₹5,00,000), heavy, and have long lead times.

**AM Revolution:**
- **Sockets** (the part that interfaces with the residual limb) are SLS-printed in PA12, often with **custom lattice structures** to reduce weight by 30–40% and improve breathability.
- **Cosmetic covers** are FFF-printed and dyed to match the patient's skin.
- **Socket-less sockets** (e.g., Walter Reilly's design) use AM to integrate compliance and rigidity gradients.
- **3D-printed hands and arms** (e.g., e-NABLE community) cost ₹5,000–₹20,000 — accessible in low-income countries.
- **Multi-material AM** (PolyJet) allows flexible fingers + rigid palm in a single build.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall / Common Mistakes:**
> 1. **Confusing SLA with DLP** — SLA uses a laser scanning a vat; DLP uses a projected light pattern curing an entire layer at once. Examiners deduct **1 mark** for this error.
> 2. **Writing $|x|$** inside tables — this BREAKS the markdown rendering. Use $\lvert x \rvert$.
> 3. **Forgetting sterilization** — many students describe build & post-processing but skip sterilization. Examiners explicitly test this in 14-mark questions.
> 4. **Calling implants "bio-printed"** — bio-printing refers to **living cells**, not metal implants. Use "AM" or "3D-printed" for non-biological parts.
> 5. **Mixing up ASTM F2792 with ISO 10993** — F2792 = AM process terminology; ISO 10993 = biocompatibility. Do not interchange.
> 6. **Not mentioning DICOM → STL conversion** — this is a critical 1.5 mark step in any medical AM workflow question.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (High-Yield — Read this the night before the exam!)**

- **Definition:** Medical AM = layer-by-layer fabrication of patient-specific medical devices from digital medical imaging data.
- **Key Standards:** **ASTM F2792-12 / ISO/ASTM 52900** (process), **ISO 10993-1** (biocompatibility), **ASTM F3082** (Ti-6Al-4V powder).
- **Workflow:** **CT/MRI → DICOM → Segmentation (HU threshold) → STL → CAD Refinement → AM Build → Post-Processing → Sterilization → Implantation**.
- **Hounsfield Unit (HU):** Bone = **+400 to +3000 HU**; Soft tissue = **-200 to +200 HU**; Air = **-1000 HU**; Water = **0 HU**.
- **STL File Size:** $S = 80 + 4 + N \times 50$ bytes (binary STL); 1 million triangles ≈ **~48 MB**.
- **Porosity Formula:** $P = \left(1 - \dfrac{\rho_{\text{actual}}}{\rho_{\text{theoretical}}}\right) \times 100\%$. Optimal bone scaffold porosity = **60–80%**, pore size = **200–500 µm**.
- **Process–Material Map:**
  - SLA/DLP → resins, dental, surgical guides
  - SLS/MJF → nylon (PA12, PA11), prosthetics, hearing aids
  - SLM/DMLS/EBM → Ti-6Al-4V, Co-Cr, SS316L (metallic implants)
  - Bioprinting → hydrogels (alginate, GelMA, collagen)
- **The "Five I's":** Individualization, Integration, Internal complexity, Immediacy, Implantability.
- **Sterilization Methods:** Autoclave (121 °C/15 psi/15 min), EtO gas (37 °C, 60% RH, 3 h), Gamma irradiation (25 kGy).
- **Famous Examples:** Oxford Performance Materials' PEEK skull, Stryker Tritanium spinal cage, Invisalign aligners, GE LEAP fuel nozzle (aerospace "etc."), Bugatti brake caliper (automotive "etc.").
- **Bioprinting Categories:** Inkjet, Extrusion, Laser-Assisted (LAB).
- **Common Pitfalls to Avoid:** Mix-up of SLA/DLP; missing sterilization step; misuse of biocompatibility vs. process standards; ignoring DICOM→STL conversion.
